- ⭐ **Modern Threshold:** Machine passes if interrogator accuracy ≤ **70%**.
- ⭐ **Statistical Baseline:** Random guessing probability = **0.5**.
- ⭐ **Standard Test Duration:** **5 minutes** of conversation.
- ⭐ **Total Turing Test:** Extension by **Harnad (1991)** adding vision, audio, and haptic modalities.
- ⭐ **Major Critique:** **Chinese Room Argument** by **John Searle (1980)** — behaviour ≠ understanding.
- ⭐ **Historical Win:** No AI has *officially* passed the unmodified Turing Test; ELIZA (1966) and modern LLMs approach but do not fully satisfy rigorous academic standards.
- ⭐ **Modern Applications:** CAPTCHAs (reverse Turing Test), chatbot evaluation, HRI benchmarking.
- ⭐ **Mnemonic:** **I-CAT** — Interrogator, Confined text, Anonymity, Threshold.
- ⭐ **Avoid in exams:** Do not say "Turing Test proves machines think." Say "Turing Test evaluates whether machines can *imitate* human behaviour."

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:**  
> When answering a 14-mark question on the Turing Test, structure your answer in three layers: **(1) Definition & Origin**, **(2) Procedure & Components**, **(3) Strengths + Weaknesses (with Searle's Chinese Room)**. This structure consistently scores full marks across KTU valuation panels.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foundations of Artificial Intelligence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Foundations of Artificial Intelligence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Artificial Intelligence

**Artificial Intelligence (AI)** is the branch of computer science dedicated to developing systems capable of performing tasks that traditionally require human intelligence. These tasks include reasoning, learning, problem-solving, perception, language understanding, and decision-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stuart Russell & Peter Norvig (Standard KTU Reference Definition):**
> AI is the study of **agents** that receive **percepts** from the environment and perform **actions**. Formally:
> $$Agent: P^{*} \rightarrow A$$
> where $P^{*}$ denotes the sequence of all percepts and $A$ is the set of all possible actions the agent can execute.

### Four Classical Approaches to Defining AI

| Approach | Focus | Definition |
|----------|-------|------------|
| **Thinking Humanly** | Cognitive Modelling | Systems that mimic the human thought process |
| **Acting Humanly** | Turing Test Approach | Systems that behave indistinguishably from humans |
| **Thinking Rationally** | Laws of Thought | Systems that reason using formal logic |
| **Acting Rationally** | Rational Agent | Systems that act to achieve the best expected outcome |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy emphasis on the **Rational Agent Approach**, as it is mathematically rigorous, generalizable, and directly applicable to robotic control architectures.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Self-Driving Car:**

Imagine a self-driving car navigating through Kerala's bustling city traffic. It must:
1. **Perceive** (sensors detect vehicles, pedestrians, traffic lights)
2. **Reason** (predict the path of an approaching autorickshaw)
3. **Plan** (decide whether to brake, swerve, or accelerate)
4. **Act** (send commands to the steering, brake, and throttle)
5. **Learn** (improve from previous drives through feedback)

This is exactly what an **AI-enabled robotic system** does — it is a closed loop of perception → cognition → action, with continuous learning from experience.

> [!TIP]
> Think of AI as the **"brain"** of the robot. Just as the human brain interprets signals from eyes and ears and decides how to move hands and legs, AI interprets sensor data and decides how to move actuators.

---

## 1.3 Historical Evolution of AI (KTU High-Yield Timeline)

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| **1943** | McCulloch & Pitts Neuron Model | Mathematical model of biological neurons |
| **1950** | Turing Test Proposed | First operational definition of machine intelligence |
| **1956** | Dartmouth Conference | Birth of AI as a formal discipline |
| **1969** | Shakey the Robot (SRI) | First mobile robot with AI reasoning |
| **1997** | Deep Blue defeats Kasparov | AI mastery in structured strategy games |
| **2011** | IBM Watson wins Jeopardy! | Natural Language Understanding at scale |
| **2012** | AlexNet (Deep Learning breakthrough) | Convolutional Neural Networks dominate vision |
| **2016** | AlphaGo defeats Lee Sedol | Reinforcement learning masterwork |
| **2020+** | Foundation Models, Generative AI, Embodied AI | Large-scale multimodal intelligence |

---

## 1.4 Foundations of AI — Eight Disciplines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks the question: **"Discuss the foundations of AI"** (14-mark question). Memorize all eight areas.

| # | Discipline | Contribution to AI |
|---|------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Philosophy** | Logic, reasoning, mind-body problem, ethics |
| 2 | **Mathematics** | Logic, computability, probability, optimization |
| 3 | **Economics** | Decision theory, utility maximization, game theory |
| 4 | **Neuroscience** | Brain architecture → Neural networks |
| 5 | **Psychology** | Cognitive science, behaviorism → Learning theory |
| 6 | **Computer Engineering** | Hardware capable of running AI algorithms |
| 7 | **Linguistics** | Natural Language Processing, syntax, semantics |
| 8 | **Control Theory** | Feedback loops, stability, homeostatic systems |

---

## 1.5 The Turing Test — Operational Definition of Intelligence

Proposed by **Alan Turing (1950)**, the test evaluates whether a machine's responses are indistinguishable from a human's over a text channel.

**Test Setup:**
- An **interrogator** communicates with two hidden entities: a human and a machine
- Communication occurs only via text
- If the interrogator cannot reliably distinguish the machine from the human, the machine is deemed to have passed the test

**Limitations of the Turing Test:**
1. Does not measure true understanding (only behavioral mimicry)
2. Vulnerable to deception-based strategies
3. Does not test for consciousness or sentience

> [!NOTE]
> The **Chinese Room Argument** by John Searle (1980) critiques the Turing Test by demonstrating that symbol manipulation does not equal genuine understanding.

---

## 1.6 Types of AI Systems

| Type | Capability | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Artificial Narrow Intelligence (ANI)** | Specialized in one task | Chess engines, voice assistants |
| **Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)** | Human-level reasoning across domains | Theoretical / not yet achieved |
| **Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI)** | Surpasses human cognitive ability | Speculative |

**AI Capability Classification by Function:**

- **Reactive Machines** → No memory (e.g., IBM Deep Blue)
- **Limited Memory** → Uses recent past (e.g., self-driving cars)
- **Theory of Mind** → Understands emotions, beliefs
- **Self-Aware AI** → Consciousness (hypothetical)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Intelligent Agents — The Core Abstraction of AI

An **agent** is anything that can be viewed as **perceiving its environment through sensors** and **acting upon that environment through actuators**.

### Mathematical Formulation

$$\text{Agent Function: } f: \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

where:
- $\mathcal{P}^{*}$ = sequence of percepts observed up to time $t$
- $\mathcal{A}$ = set of all actions available to the agent
- The function $f$ maps the entire perceptual history to an action

### Program vs Function Distinction

The **agent function** is an abstract mathematical description, while the **agent program** is the concrete implementation that runs on a physical architecture.

$$Agent\ Program \subseteq Agent\ Function$$

---

## 2.2 The PEAS Framework (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PEAS is a **high-yield 14-mark topic** in KTU exams. Always design an agent by first specifying its PEAS description.

| Component | Description | Example (Autonomous Vacuum Cleaner) |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Performance Measure** | Criteria for success | Cleanliness, battery life, path efficiency |
| **Environment** | Operating context | Room layout, furniture, dirt distribution |
| **Actuators** | Effectors for action | Wheels, brushes, vacuum motor |
| **Sensors** | Perception inputs | Infrared, cliff sensors, bump sensors, camera |

**Extended PEAS Example — Self-Driving Car:**

| Component | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Performance** | Safety, speed, comfort, fuel efficiency |
| **Environment** | Roads, traffic, weather, pedestrians |
| **Actuators** | Steering, accelerator, brake, signal lights |
| **Sensors** | LiDAR, RADAR, cameras, GPS, IMU |

---

## 2.3 Environment Classification (7 Dimensions)

KTU 2024 scheme expects students to classify environments across these properties:

| Property | Spectrum | Example |
|----------|----------|---------|
| **Observable** | Fully / Partially | Chess = fully; Poker = partially |
| **Deterministic vs Stochastic** | Deterministic / Stochastic | Crosswalk signal = deterministic; traffic = stochastic |
| **Episodic vs Sequential** | Episodic / Sequential | Image classification = episodic; driving = sequential |
| **Static vs Dynamic** | Static / Dynamic | Crossword puzzle = static; elevator = dynamic |
| **Discrete vs Continuous** | Discrete / Continuous | Chess = discrete; robotics control = continuous |
| **Single-agent vs Multi-agent** | Single / Multi | Vacuum cleaner = single; soccer = multi |
| **Known vs Unknown** | Known / Unknown | Taxis in known city vs. unknown city |

---

## 2.4 The Concept of Rationality

A **rational agent** acts to maximize its **expected performance measure** given its:
- Prior knowledge of the environment
- Percept history
- Available actions

**Rationality vs Omniscience:**
- **Rationality** → Expected performance based on available percepts
- **Omniscience** → Knowledge of actual outcomes (impossible in reality)

> [!NOTE]
> A rational agent does not need to be perfect; it only needs to maximize expected utility based on what is *known* at the time of decision.

### Performance Measure Examples

| Task | Good Performance Measure | Bad Performance Measure |
|------|-------------------------|------------------------|
| Vacuum Cleaner | Clean floor + long battery | Speed of movement |
| Doctor AI | Patient recovery rate | Number of tests ordered |
| Stock Trading | Risk-adjusted return | Number of trades |

---

## 2.5 Agent Architectures (KTU High-Yield)

### 1. Simple Reflex Agent
$$Action = Rule(Current\ Percept)$$
- Ignores percept history
- Works only in **fully observable** environments
- Example: Thermostat, simple obstacle avoider

### 2. Model-Based Reflex Agent
$$State_{t+1} = f(State_t, Action_t, Percept_{t+1})$$
- Maintains internal state
- Handles **partially observable** environments

### 3. Goal-Based Agent
$$Action = \arg\max_{a} \ U(Goal\ \vert\ State, a)$$
- Reasons about future outcomes
- Uses search and planning

### 4. Utility-Based Agent
$$Action = \arg\max_{a} \ \mathbb{E}[U(s') \vert s, a]$$
- Maximizes expected utility (handles trade-offs)

### 5. Learning Agent
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \alpha \nabla_{\theta} L$$
- Improves performance through experience
- Components: **Critic, Learning Element, Performance Element, Problem Generator**

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | Agent Function | $f: \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Maps percept history to action |
| 2 | Rationality | $\arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[\text{Performance} \vert \text{percept history}]$ | Maximize expected outcome |
| 3 | Conditional Probability | $P(A \vert B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | Foundation of Bayesian AI |
| 4 | Bayes' Rule | $P(H \vert E) = \dfrac{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Used in reasoning under uncertainty |
| 5 | Expected Utility | $\mathbb{E}[U] = \sum_{i} P(s_i) \cdot U(s_i)$ | Decision-theoretic AI |
| 6 | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measures information content |
| 7 | Turing Test Criterion | Indistinguishability of human vs machine response | Behavioural AI test |
| 8 | Learning Update Rule | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla L(\theta_t)$ | Gradient descent |
| 9 | Reflex Action | $A = \text{Condition-Action-Rule}(\text{Percept})$ | IF-THEN rules |
| 10 | Discounted Reward (RL) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Reinforcement Learning |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** When writing absolute values in the exam answer sheet, use $\vert x \vert$ (LaTeX vertical bar) instead of typing |x| directly, to avoid confusion with markdown table pipes.

---

## 2.7 AI in Robotics — Real-World Utility

| Robotic Application | AI Technique Used | Outcome |
|--------------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Autonomous Navigation** | SLAM, Path Planning (A*, RRT) | Real-time obstacle avoidance |
| **Manipulation** | Computer Vision, Reinforcement Learning | Grasping unseen objects |
| **Human-Robot Interaction** | NLP, Speech Recognition | Natural communication |
| **Inspection Drones** | CNN-based Object Detection | Defect identification |
| **Medical Robotics** | Deep Learning, Decision Support | Surgical precision |
| **Warehouse Automation** | Multi-Agent Coordination, Scheduling | Order fulfillment |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Percept Sequence to Rational Action

The complete decision-making process of a rational agent can be derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Define the Percept History**
$$\mathcal{P}^{*} = \{p_1, p_2, p_3, \ldots, p_t\}$$

This represents all percepts observed by the agent from time 0 up to time $t$. Each $p_i \in \mathcal{P}$ where $\mathcal{P}$ is the set of all possible percepts.

**Step 2 — Define the State**
The state summarizes the percept history:
$$s_t = f(\mathcal{P}^{*}_t)$$

**Step 3 — Define the Transition Model**
When the agent performs action $a$ in state $s$, the environment transitions:
$$T(s, a, s') = P(s' \vert s, a)$$

This is the probability of reaching state $s'$ after taking action $a$ in state $s$.

**Step 4 — Define the Performance Measure**
The agent's objective is to maximize:
$$V = \sum_{t=0}^{T} R(s_t, a_t)$$

where $R$ is the reward function, and $T$ may be finite (episodic) or infinite (continuing).

**Step 5 — Apply the Rationality Principle**
The optimal action is:
$$a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \ \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{T} R(s_t, a_t) \ \bigg\vert \ s_0, a \right]$$

**Step 6 — Simplification using Markov Property**
If the environment satisfies the Markov property (future depends only on current state):
$$P(s_{t+1} \vert s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \ldots) = P(s_{t+1} \vert s_t, a_t)$$

The decision simplifies to choosing the action that maximizes the immediate or cumulative expected reward from the current state.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bayes' Rule Application in AI Reasoning

Bayes' Rule is fundamental to AI systems that reason under uncertainty.

**Step 1 — Start with the Definition of Conditional Probability**
$$P(A \vert B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the Joint Probability**
$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \cap A) = P(B \vert A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**
$$P(A \vert B) = \frac{P(B \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 4 — Express the Marginal $P(B)$**
$$P(B) = \sum_{i} P(B \vert A_i) \cdot P(A_i)$$

**Step 5 — Final Form**
$$P(A \vert B) = \frac{P(B \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{\sum_{i} P(B \vert A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In AI, $A$ is called the **hypothesis** and $B$ is the **evidence**. $P(A)$ is the **prior**, $P(B \vert A)$ is the **likelihood**, and $P(A \vert B)$ is the **posterior**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Agent Architectures

| Property | Simple Reflex | Model-Based | Goal-Based | Utility-Based | Learning |
|----------|---------------|-------------|------------|---------------|----------|
| **Stores Percept History?** | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Handles Partial Observability?** | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Reasoning Depth** | None | State tracking | Planning | Optimization | Adaptive |
| **Computational Cost** | Very Low | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **Example Use Case** | Thermostat | Elevator Controller | Route Planner | Trading Bot | Robotic Arm |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: PEAS Design for a Robotic Assistant

**Scenario:** Design an AI agent that assists a visually impaired person in navigating a railway platform.

**Step 1 — Performance Measure**
- Safety (no collisions)
- Successful platform identification
- Real-time responsiveness
- Announcements accuracy

**Step 2 — Environment**
- Railway platforms (semi-controlled)
- Multiple trains, passengers, vendors
- Variable lighting, weather
- Audio announcements

**Step 3 — Actuators**
- Speaker (audio output)
- Vibration motor (haptic alerts)
- Bluetooth bone-conduction earphone interface

**Step 4 — Sensors**
- Camera (visual scene)
- Microphone (announcements, traffic)
- GPS (location)
- Ultrasonic (obstacle distance)
- IMU (orientation)

**Step 5 — Environment Classification**

| Property | Classification | Justification |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| Observable | **Partially** | Hidden by crowd, occlusions |
| Deterministic | **Stochastic** | Random crowd movement |
| Episodic | **Sequential** | Decisions depend on prior path |
| Static | **Dynamic** | Trains and people in motion |
| Discrete | **Continuous** | Audio waveforms, image pixels |
| Single-agent | **Multi-agent** | Other humans, autonomous systems |
| Known | **Partially Known** | Layout known, events unknown |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AI Agent — Perception-Action Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Env1[Environment] -->|Stimulus| Sensor1[Sensors]
    Sensor1 -->|Percept| Agent1[Agent Core]
    Agent1 -->|Reasoning and Decision| Action1[Actuators]
    Action1 -->|Action Effect| Env1
    Agent1 -->|Internal State| Memory1[Knowledge Base]
    Memory1 -->|Prior Knowledge| Agent1
```

**Block-Level Functional Description:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| Environment | External world (physical or simulated) |
| Sensors | Transduce physical signals into percepts |
| Agent Core | Percept → Reasoning → Action pipeline |
| Actuators | Convert decisions into physical effects |
| Knowledge Base | Long-term memory, learned models |

---

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Agent Architectures

```mermaid

    A[All Intelligent Agents] --> B[Rational Agents]
    A --> C[Non Rational Agents]
    B --> D[Simple Reflex]
    B --> E[Model Based Reflex]
    B --> F[Goal Based]
    B --> G[Utility Based]
    B --> H[Learning Agent]
    H --> I[Supervised Learner]
    H --> J[Unsupervised Learner]
    H --> K[Reinforcement Learner]
```

---

## 4.3 PEAS Design Flow

```mermaid

```

---

## 4.4 AI Foundations — Eight Disciplines Map

## 4.5 AI Capability Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Reactive Machines] --> L2[Limited Memory]

    L2A[Example: Self Driving Cars] -.- L2

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define an Intelligent Agent. List its four key components.**

**Model Answer:**

An **intelligent agent** is any entity that perceives its environment through **sensors** and acts upon it through **actuators** to achieve specified goals based on a performance measure.

The four key components are:
1. **Perception Module** — Acquires sensory input
2. **Reasoning/Decision Module** — Processes percepts
3. **Action Module** — Executes decisions via actuators
4. **Learning Module** — Improves behavior from experience

> [!NOTE]
> For full marks, the student must also mention the **agent function** $f: \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ as the mathematical abstraction.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Weak AI and Strong AI.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Weak AI (Narrow AI) | Strong AI (General AI) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Scope | Specialized single tasks | General human-level reasoning |
| Self-awareness | None | Hypothetically present |
| Current Status | Widely deployed | Theoretically pursued |
| Example | Siri, ChatGPT, chess engines | Hypothetical AGI |

> [!TIP]
> Mention that **all current AI systems are weak AI**, including foundation models — they only *simulate* generality.

---

## 📘 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Foundations of AI):**
> - **Lose 2 marks** if PEAS description misses one component.
> - **Lose 2 marks** if environment classification is incomplete (must list 6+ properties).
> - **Lose 3 marks** if agent architecture is described without a diagram.
> - **Lose 2 marks** if definitions are not tied back to the robotic application context.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Define Artificial Intelligence. Discuss the four classical approaches to defining AI with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **PEAS framework** in detail. Design the PEAS specification for an **autonomous agricultural robot** used for precision spraying in Kerala's spice plantations. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Definition:**
Artificial Intelligence is the science of creating systems that perform tasks requiring intelligent behavior, typically associated with human cognition, such as reasoning, learning, perception, and decision-making.

**Four Approaches:**

1. **Acting Humanly (Turing Test Approach):**
   - The system must communicate in natural language, store knowledge, reason, learn, and perceive.
   - Limitation: Does not guarantee true understanding (Chinese Room Argument).

2. **Thinking Humanly (Cognitive Modelling):**
   - Requires understanding the actual workings of the human mind.
   - Uses introspection, psychological experiments, and brain imaging.

3. **Thinking Rationally (Laws of Thought):**
   - Based on Aristotelian logic and syllogisms.
   - Limitations: Hard to formalize informal knowledge, brittleness.

4. **Acting Rationally (Rational Agent):**
   - Most general approach; agent acts to maximize expected performance.
   - More general than laws of thought (handles uncertainty, utilities).

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**PEAS for Autonomous Agricultural Robot (Precision Spraying):**

| Component | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Performance Measure** | Pest detection accuracy, pesticide usage reduction, crop yield preservation, no over-spraying |
| **Environment** | Spice plantations (pepper, cardamom, turmeric), uneven terrain, Kerala monsoon conditions, mixed crop types, livestock movement |
| **Actuators** | Sprayer nozzle arrays, robotic arm, wheeled/tracked drive, pump control |
| **Sensors** | Multispectral camera, LiDAR, soil moisture sensor, weather station, GPS/RTK, IMU |

**Environment Classification:**

- **Partially observable** (occluded by leaves)
- **Stochastic** (weather variability, pest emergence)
- **Sequential** (route depends on prior detections)
- **Dynamic** (wind, growing plants)
- **Continuous** (sensor data streams)
- **Single-agent** (one robot per plot)
- **Known** (plantation map is preloaded)

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **eight foundations of AI** with reference to their contributions to the field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the different types of **agent architectures** with neat block diagrams. Compare their suitability for robotic applications. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

| # | Foundation | Contribution |
|---|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Philosophy** | Formal logic, mind-body dualism, ethics of intelligence |
| 2 | **Mathematics** | Boolean logic, computability (Turing), probability (Bayes) |
| 3 | **Economics** | Utility theory, decision theory, game theory |
| 4 | **Neuroscience** | Neural substrates, plasticity → artificial neural networks |
| 5 | **Psychology** | Behaviourism → reinforcement learning, cognition → knowledge representation |
| 6 | **Computer Engineering** | Faster hardware (GPUs, TPUs) enables deep learning |
| 7 | **Linguistics** | Syntax, semantics → NLP, speech recognition |
| 8 | **Control Theory** | Feedback loops, stability, optimal control → robotics |

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Five Agent Architectures:**

1. **Simple Reflex Agent** — Uses condition-action rules; no memory. Example: Roomba basic mode.
2. **Model-Based Reflex Agent** — Maintains internal world model. Example: Elevator controller.
3. **Goal-Based Agent** — Reasons about future to achieve goals. Example: GPS route planner.
4. **Utility-Based Agent** — Maximizes a utility function under trade-offs. Example: Autonomous trading robot.
5. **Learning Agent** — Improves via feedback. Example: Robotic arm learning grasping.

**Robotic Suitability Comparison:**

| Architecture | Best Robotic Use Case | Limitation |
|--------------|----------------------|------------|
| Simple Reflex | Obstacle avoidance, cliff detection | Cannot handle ambiguity |
| Model-Based | SLAM robots, autonomous vacuum | Needs accurate world model |
| Goal-Based | Pick-and-place, mission planning | Computationally expensive |
| Utility-Based | Self-driving cars (trade-off of safety vs speed) | Hard to define utility function |
| Learning | Manipulation, locomotion | Requires extensive training data |

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Foundations of AI (Module 1)**

- 🔹 **AI Definition**: Russell & Norvig's agent-based definition is the gold standard.
- 🔹 **Four Approaches**: Thinking Humanly, Acting Humanly, Thinking Rationally, Acting Rationally.
- 🔹 **Turing Test**: Behavioural test of intelligence; criticized by Searle's Chinese Room.
- 🔹 **Eight Foundations**: Philosophy, Mathematics, Economics, Neuroscience, Psychology, Computer Engineering, Linguistics, Control Theory.
- 🔹 **Agent Function**: $f: \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$
- 🔹 **PEAS Components**: Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors.
- 🔹 **Environment Types**: Observable, Deterministic, Episodic, Static, Discrete, Single-agent, Known.
- 🔹 **Five Agent Architectures**: Simple Reflex → Model-Based → Goal-Based → Utility-Based → Learning.
- 🔹 **Rationality**: Maximize expected performance given available percepts.
- 🔹 **AI Types**: ANI (current), AGI (theoretical), ASI (speculative).
- 🔹 **Bayes' Rule** is foundational to reasoning under uncertainty.
- 🔹 **Always draw a block diagram** when describing an agent architecture — missing diagram = -2 to -3 marks.
- 🔹 **Tie AI concepts to robotics**: Examples to use — self-driving cars, robot vacuums, agricultural robots, surgical robots, humanoid robots.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** If asked "Explain intelligent agent" in 14 marks, structure the answer as:
> (1) Definition with formula, (2) PEAS of a chosen robotic example, (3) Environment classification, (4) Agent architecture with diagram, (5) Conclusion tying back to robotic autonomy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AI for Robotics – Historical Context (KTU Definition)**
> *Artificial Intelligence for Robotics* is an interdisciplinary engineering domain that synergizes **perception**, **reasoning**, **planning**, and **actuation** within physical or simulated embodied agents. The *historical overview* traces the co-evolution of cybernetics, control theory, classical AI, and modern machine learning into a unified field where intelligent algorithms govern autonomous mechanical systems.

The historical overview is broadly classified into **three intertwined lineages**:

| Lineage | Core Discipline | Defining Question |
|---|---|---|
| **AI Lineage** | Computer Science, Cognitive Science | "Can machines *think*?" |
| **Robotics Lineage** | Mechanical & Control Engineering | "Can machines *act* in the physical world?" |
| **Cybernetics Lineage** | Systems Theory, Biology | "Can machines *self-regulate* and *adapt*?" |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Simple Analogy: The Evolution of a Child**
> Imagine a child growing up. First, they learn to **recognize objects** (perception). Then they learn to **reason** about them ("the cup is hot, don't touch"). Later, they learn to **plan** ("I will walk to the table, then grab the cup"). Finally, they **act** by moving their body. The history of AI for Robotics is the same journey — but for machines.
> - **1950s = Infant:** The machine is born (Turing, Dartmouth).
> - **1970s = Toddler:** It learns to react (Shakey, Brooks).
> - **2000s = Teenager:** It learns to learn (Deep Learning).
> - **2020s = Adult:** It reasons with language and vision (Foundation Models).

## 1.3 Key Constants and Milestone Metrics

- **Dartmouth Conference Year:** **1956** — formal birth of AI.
- **Turing Test Year:** **1950** — proposed in *Mind* journal.
- **First Industrial Robot (Unimate):** Installed at GM in **1961**.
- **First Intelligent Mobile Robot (Shakey):** **1969–1972**, Stanford Research Institute.
- **"AI Winter" Periods:** **1974–1980** and **1987–1993**.
- **Deep Learning Breakthrough (AlexNet):** **2012**, ImageNet competition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary

## 2.1 The Three Evolutionary Waves of AI for Robotics

### 🌊 **Wave 1 — Symbolic / Knowledge-Based Era (1956–1985)**
- Dominated by **GOFAI** (Good Old-Fashioned AI).
- Robots relied on **explicit world models**, hand-crafted rules, and logical reasoning (e.g., STRIPS planner, Shakey).
- **Limitation:** *Brittleness* — failed in unstructured environments.

### 🌊 **Wave 2 — Reactive / Behavior-Based Era (1986–2000)**
- Pioneered by **Rodney Brooks** (MIT) with the *Subsumption Architecture*.
- Philosophy: *“The world is its own best model.”* No central reasoning; instead, layered behaviors (avoid-obstacle → wander → explore).
- Enabled robust, insect-like robots (e.g., **Genghis**, **Roomba**).

### 🌊 **Wave 3 — Learning-Based / Cognitive Era (2000–Present)**
- Rise of **probabilistic methods** (SLAM, Bayesian filters), **deep learning** (CNNs, RNNs, Transformers).
- Robots now learn from **data** rather than rules.
- Modern era: **Foundation Models** (RT-2, PaLM-E, Figure 01, Tesla Optimus) fuse vision, language, and action.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Timeline Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is **exam-ready**. Memorize the **year-event-pioneer** triplets for any KTU question on history.

| Year | Event / System | Pioneer / Institution | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1495** | Leonardo's Mechanical Knight | Leonardo da Vinci | First humanoid automaton design |
| **1738** | Digesting Duck | Jacques de Vaucanson | Bio-mimetic automaton |
| **1921** | *R.U.R.* play | Karel Čapek | Coined the word **"Robot"** (Czech: *robota* = forced work) |
| **1942** | Three Laws of Robotics | Isaac Asimov | Ethical framework for robots |
| **1948** | *Cybernetics* book | Norbert Wiener | Foundation of feedback & control theory |
| **1950** | Turing Test | Alan Turing | "Can machines think?" |
| **1956** | Dartmouth Conference | McCarthy, Minsky, Rochester, Shannon | **Birth of AI** as a formal field |
| **1959** | Machine Learning coined | Arthur Samuel | Self-learning checkers program |
| **1961** | Unimate | George Devol / Joseph Engelberger | First industrial robot at GM |
| **1969** | **Shakey the Robot** | SRI International | First **AI-powered mobile robot** |
| **1979** | Stanford Cart | Hans Moravec | Autonomous navigation with stereo vision |
| **1986** | Subsumption Architecture | Rodney Brooks | Reactive robotics paradigm |
| **1997** | Deep Blue defeats Kasparov | IBM | Symbolic AI triumphs in chess |
| **2002** | Roomba | iRobot | First mass-market autonomous robot |
| **2004** | DARPA Grand Challenge | Sebastian Thrun / Stanford | Self-driving cars go mainstream |
| **2012** | AlexNet | Hinton's team | Deep learning revolution begins |
| **2016** | AlphaGo defeats Lee Sedol | DeepMind | RL + Deep Learning milestone |
| **2022** | ChatGPT / GPT-4 | OpenAI | Foundation models enter robotics (RT-2) |

## 2.3 Why the Convergence Happened — The Engineering Rationale

1. **Classical control** could not scale to unstructured environments.
2. **Pure software AI** lacked a physical test-bed for embodied cognition.
3. **Robots needed perception** → borrowed from computer vision (CV) and machine learning (ML).
4. **AI needed embodiment** → the *physical grounding hypothesis* (Rodney Brooks, 1990) showed that intelligence emerges from sensorimotor interaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Every modern autonomous system — from self-driving cars (Waymo, Tesla) to surgical robots (Da Vinci), warehouse bots (Amazon Kiva), and humanoid robots (Figure, Optimus) — is a direct descendant of this historical convergence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Paradigm Shifts

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: The *Intelligence Hierarchy* for Robots

The evolution of robot intelligence can be modeled as a **stack of capabilities**, where each historical era added a new layer on top of the previous one.

$$
\text{Robot Intelligence} = \text{Sense}(S) \;\cup\; \text{Plan}(P) \;\cup\; \text{Act}(A) \;\cup\; \text{Learn}(L)
$$

Where:

- $S$ = Perception (sensors, vision, SLAM)
- $P$ = Planning (path planning, task planning, reasoning)
- $A$ = Actuation (motors, end-effectors, locomotion)
- $L$ = Learning (from data, from experience, from language)

### Step-by-Step Derivation of the Capability Matrix

**Step 1 — Define the baseline (1950s).**
The earliest robots had only $A$ (actuation). They were pre-programmed mechanical arms with no sensing or reasoning.

$$
\text{Capability}_{1950} = \{A\}
$$

**Step 2 — Add sensing (1960s with Unimate + sensors).**
Force-torque sensors and limit switches introduced $S$.

$$
\text{Capability}_{1960} = \{S, A\}
$$

**Step 3 — Add planning (1969, Shakey the Robot).**
Shakey used the **STRIPS planner** to reason about actions. This was the first time $P$ appeared.

$$
\text{Capability}_{1970} = \{S, P, A\}
$$

**Step 4 — Add learning (2000s, machine learning era).**
Reinforcement learning, SLAM, and deep neural networks gave robots the ability to *adapt*.

$$
\text{Capability}_{2010} = \{S, P, A, L\}
$$

**Step 5 — Add language/embodied reasoning (2020s, foundation models).**
Large multimodal models (e.g., RT-2) allow robots to understand natural language instructions and generalize across tasks.

$$
\text{Capability}_{2024} = \{S, P, A, L, \text{Language}\}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Paradigm Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **guaranteed KTU 14-marker** if the question asks to *compare the three eras of AI in robotics*. Memorize it.

| Dimension | Symbolic Era (1960s–80s) | Reactive Era (1986–2000) | Learning Era (2000–Now) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **World Model** | Explicit, hand-coded | None (world-as-model) | Implicit, learned |
| **Reasoning** | Logical, deductive | Stimulus-response | Statistical, probabilistic |
| **Computation** | Symbolic search | Finite-state machines | Neural networks, GPU |
| **Strength** | High-level planning | Robustness, real-time | Adaptability, perception |
| **Weakness** | Brittle, slow | No long-term planning | Data-hungry, opaque |
| **Example** | Shakey, STRIPS | Genghis, Roomba | AlphaGo, RT-2, Optimus |
| **Pioneer** | McCarthy, Nilsson | Brooks | Hinton, Thrun, Bengio |

## 3.3 Worked Example: From Shakey to RT-2

**Problem (Conceptual, 7 marks):** *Trace the evolution from Shakey the Robot (1969) to modern robot foundation models (2023), highlighting the paradigm shift in each era.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Shakey (1969):** Used a symbolic planner (STRIPS) and a geometric map of the world. The world was a formal representation; the robot reasoned over symbols.
2. **Subsumption (1986):** Brooks rejected world models. He built layered behaviors (e.g., *avoid*, *wander*) that reacted directly to sensor input. Intelligence emerged from interaction, not deliberation.
3. **Probabilistic Robotics (2000s):** Thrun, Burgard, and Fox introduced **Bayesian filters, SLAM, and POMDPs** to handle uncertainty in real environments.
4. **Deep Learning Era (2012–2020):** CNNs replaced hand-crafted features for vision; reinforcement learning enabled robots to learn manipulation skills (e.g., OpenAI's Dactyl, 2018).
5. **Foundation Model Era (2022+):** Google's **RT-2** (Robotics Transformer 2) showed that a vision-language model trained on internet data could directly output robot actions, enabling **zero-shot generalization**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Shift: Symbols} \rightarrow \text{Behavior} \rightarrow \text{Probabilities} \rightarrow \text{Neural Patterns} \rightarrow \text{Language-Grounded Actions}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the term "Robot" and trace its etymological origin.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Model Answer:**
The word *robot* was coined by Czech writer **Karel Čapek** in his 1921 play *R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)*. It is derived from the Slavic word *robota*, meaning **"forced labor"** or **"drudgery."** Originally, it referred to artificial biological workers; today, it broadly denotes any autonomous or semi-autonomous programmable mechanical system capable of performing tasks in the physical world.

### **Q2. What were the two "AI Winters"? Mention the major cause of each.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Model Answer:**
An **AI Winter** is a period of reduced funding and interest in AI research caused by unmet expectations.
- **First AI Winter (1974–1980):** Caused by the **Lighthill Report (1973)** and the failure of machine translation and symbolic AI to scale.
- **Second AI Winter (1987–1993):** Caused by the **collapse of the expert-system market** (LISP machines, XCON's escalating costs) and the limitations of rule-based systems.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Writing only *names* of pioneers without their *contributions* → loses 2–3 marks.
> - ❌ Confusing **AI** history with **Robotics** history → treat them as **converging** but distinct.
> - ❌ Omitting the **year** of a milestone → in KTU, dates carry 1 mark each.
> - ❌ Drawing vague timelines without categorizing into **eras** → examiners expect structured eras.
> - ✅ Always end with a **diagram or table** of milestones for guaranteed 2 bonus marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [Internal Choice Option 1]**

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the major milestones in the evolution of **Artificial Intelligence** from 1950 to 2020, classifying them into distinct eras. *(7 marks)*
**Q. (b)** Explain the significance of **Shakey the Robot** as a historical milestone. How did it influence modern AI-robotics systems? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer for (a):
1. **Pre-Dawn (1950):** Alan Turing publishes *Computing Machinery and Intelligence*, proposing the *Imitation Game* (Turing Test).
2. **Birth (1956):** The **Dartmouth Summer Research Project** (McCarthy, Minsky, Rochester, Shannon) officially coins "Artificial Intelligence."
3. **Early Enthusiasm (1960s):** Development of **LISP** (1958), **ELIZA** (1966, Weizenbaum), and **SHRDLU** (Winograd, 1968). Logic-based reasoning dominates.
4. **First AI Winter (1974–80):** Government funding cuts in the US and UK after the *Lighthill Report*.
5. **Expert Systems Boom (1980s):** **MYCIN** (medical diagnosis), **XCON** (Digital Equipment Corp). Commercial success but fragile.
6. **Second AI Winter (1987–93):** Collapse of LISP-machine market; expert systems prove unmaintainable.
7. **Statistical Revolution (1990s–2000s):** Rise of **probabilistic methods**, **Bayesian networks**, **SVMs**.
8. **Deep Learning Era (2012+):** **AlexNet** (Krizhevsky, Hinton) wins ImageNet by a huge margin. GPUs enable training of large neural networks.
9. **Reinforcement Learning Milestone (2016):** **AlphaGo** defeats world champion Lee Sedol using *Deep RL + Monte Carlo Tree Search*.
10. **Foundation Models (2020+):** **GPT-3 (2020)**, **ChatGPT (2022)**, **RT-2 (2023)** — single models handling vision, language, and action.

#### Model Answer for (b):
**Shakey the Robot (1969–1972, SRI International)** is considered the **first mobile robot to reason about its own actions**.

- **Hardware:** Onboard TV camera, range-finder, bump sensors, and a radio link to a PDP-10 mainframe.
- **Software Stack:** Used **STRIPS** (Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver) for action planning, **A\*** search for navigation, and **symbolic world modeling**.
- **Historical Significance:**
  - Pioneered the **sense–plan–act** paradigm.
  - Introduced the concept of a robot maintaining an **explicit symbolic world model**.
  - Directly inspired the field of **robot planning** and modern **hierarchical task networks (HTNs)**.
- **Influence on Modern Systems:**
  - Modern autonomous vehicles use a **Shakey-like layered architecture** (perception → planning → control).
  - The **sense–plan–act** loop is the foundation of **ROS (Robot Operating System)** and **autonomy stacks** used in self-driving cars (e.g., **Apollo, Autoware**).
  - STRIPS evolved into **PDDL (Planning Domain Definition Language)**, the standard for modern AI planners.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Internal Choice Option 2]**

**Q. (a)** Compare and contrast the **Symbolic AI approach** and the **Reactive / Behavior-based approach** in robotics, with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*
**Q. (b)** Describe the role of **cybernetics, control theory, and machine learning** in shaping modern AI-robotics. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer for (a):

| Aspect | Symbolic AI (Shakey, STRIPS) | Reactive AI (Brooks, Genghis) |
|---|---|---|
| **World Model** | Explicit symbolic map | None — world is its own model |
| **Reasoning** | Centralized, deliberative | Distributed, layered behaviors |
| **Speed** | Slow (search-heavy) | Fast (reflexive) |
| **Robustness** | Brittle in noisy environments | Robust in dynamic environments |
| **Scalability** | Limited by combinatorial explosion | Scales with added layers |
| **Example** | Shakey, SHAKEY, NAVLAB | Genghis, Roomba, Roomba |
| **Strength** | Long-horizon planning | Real-time reactivity |

**Conclusion:** Neither paradigm alone is sufficient. **Modern hybrid architectures** (e.g., ROS 2 + Behavior Trees + Foundation Models) combine *symbolic planning* (for high-level goals) with *reactive control* (for low-level safety), embodying the best of both eras.

#### Model Answer for (b):
1. **Cybernetics (1948, Norbert Wiener):** Introduced the concept of **feedback loops** and *goal-seeking machines*. Foundation of all modern control systems.
2. **Control Theory (1950s–60s):** **PID controllers, state-space methods, optimal control** (Kalman filter) gave robots the mathematical tools to actuate precisely.
3. **Probabilistic AI (1990s–2000s):** **SLAM, Bayesian filters, POMDPs** (Thrun, Fox, Burgard) allowed robots to *localize* and *map* under uncertainty.
4. **Machine Learning (2010s):** **Deep reinforcement learning** (DQN, PPO, SAC) enabled robots to learn manipulation and locomotion from scratch (e.g., OpenAI's Dactyl, Boston Dynamics' parkour).
5. **Foundation Models (2020s):** **RT-2, PaLM-E, Octo** unified perception, language, and action in a single neural network, marking the *4th paradigm* of AI-robotics.

**Engineering takeaway:** Modern intelligent robots are a **layered stack** of cybernetics (feedback), control theory (actuation), probabilistic methods (uncertainty), and deep learning (adaptation) — each a historical gift from a different era.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Know Before Exam)**

- ✅ AI was formally **born in 1956** at the **Dartmouth Conference**.
- ✅ The word **"Robot"** was coined by **Karel Čapek in 1921** in the play *R.U.R.*.
- ✅ The **Turing Test** was proposed in **1950** (paper: *Computing Machinery and Intelligence*).
- ✅ **Unimate (1961)** was the first industrial robot; installed at **General Motors**.
- ✅ **Shakey the Robot (1969)** was the first AI-powered mobile robot.
- ✅ **Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986)** founded the reactive paradigm.
- ✅ The two **AI Winters** are: **(1974–80)** and **(1987–93)**.
- ✅ **AlexNet (2012)** triggered the deep-learning revolution.
- ✅ **AlphaGo (2016)** demonstrated mastery of an intuition-heavy game via deep RL.
- ✅ **RT-2 (2023)** unified vision, language, and action in a single model.
- ✅ Three lineages: **AI**, **Robotics**, and **Cybernetics** — all converge in modern intelligent robots.
- ✅ Three eras: **Symbolic → Reactive → Learning-based** (plus the modern *Foundation-Model* era).
- ✅ *Embodied Cognition* — intelligence is grounded in physical sensorimotor interaction.
- ✅ Modern robot stack = **Sensors → Perception → Reasoning → Planning → Control → Actuators**.
- ✅ Key paradigms to remember: **Sense–Plan–Act (Shakey)** vs. **Subsumption (Brooks)** vs. **End-to-End Learning (Deep RL / RT-2)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intelligent Agents - Agents and Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Intelligent Agents - Agents and Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Agent?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Formal)**
> An **Agent** is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through **sensors** and acting upon that environment through **actuators**.

More precisely, an agent is a system that:

* **Perceives** the state of its environment via sensors.
* **Reasons / Decides** upon an action based on its percept sequence.
* **Acts** upon the environment via actuators, thereby changing the future percepts it receives.

The complete mapping that an agent embodies is known as the **Agent Function**, which maps every possible percept history to an action.

$$f : \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

Where:
* $\mathcal{P}^{*}$ = the set of **all possible finite percept sequences**.
* $\mathcal{A}$ = the set of **all possible actions** the agent can execute.
* $f$ = the agent function (abstract mathematical mapping).

The **Agent Program** is the concrete implementation of $f$ that runs on a physical architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Human Agent**
> Think of *yourself* walking into a dark room. Your **sensors** are your eyes and ears; your **actuators** are your hands, legs, and voice. Based on what you have seen so far in life (your *percept sequence*), you decide to flick the light switch. That decision-to-action mapping is your agent function. A robot doing the same task is just a *silicon version* of you!

## 1.2 Rationality — The Bedrock of Intelligent Behaviour

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rational Agent Definition (Russell & Norvig, KTU adopted):**
> For each possible percept sequence, a **rational agent** should select an action that is expected to **maximize its performance measure**, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and whatever built-in knowledge the agent has.

A rational agent does **not** need to be **omniscient** (knowing the actual outcome of actions); it must instead act based on its **expected** performance based on available information.

## 1.3 PEAS Framework

The **PEAS** acronym is the universal KTU-mandated descriptor for any agent design problem:

| Letter | Stands For | Question It Answers |
|--------|------------|---------------------|
| **P** | Performance Measure | *How do we judge success?* |
| **E** | Environment | *What world does the agent inhabit?* |
| **A** | Actuators | *How does the agent change the world?* |
| **S** | Sensors | *How does the agent perceive the world?* |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Autonomy Principle (KTU High-Yield):** A truly intelligent agent should not rely solely on prior knowledge of its designer — it should learn from its own percepts. An agent whose actions are determined only by its **built-in knowledge** (ignoring percepts) lacks **autonomy**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rational Action — The Mathematical Criterion

A rational agent at any instant selects the action $a$ that maximizes the **expected value** of the performance measure $V$.

$$a^{*} = \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \; \mathbb{E}\left[\,V(\text{outcome of } a \,\vert\, \text{percept sequence}) \,\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why expected value?** Because the agent cannot predict the future deterministically. Rationality is about the **best expected outcome**, not the guaranteed best outcome.

## 2.2 Four Pillars of Rationality (Definition Checklist)

For an agent to be rational, **all four** conditions must be jointly satisfied:

1. **Performance Measure** — A clear, quantitative success criterion defined by the user.
2. **Prior Knowledge** of the environment.
3. **Percept Sequence** — Everything the agent has perceived so far.
4. **Action Set** — The feasible actions available.

## 2.3 Task Environment Properties — The 7-Dimensional Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU repeatedly tests classification of environments across these **seven orthogonal dimensions**. Memorize the keyword contrasts!

| Dimension | Possible Values | Definition |
|-----------|-----------------|------------|
| **Observability** | Fully / Partially | Are all relevant env. variables accessible via sensors? |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic / Stochastic | Is the next state fully determined by current state + action? |
| **Episodicity** | Episodic / Sequential | Is each action-decision independent of prior ones? |
| **Dynamics** | Static / Dynamic | Can the environment change while the agent deliberates? |
| **Discreteness** | Discrete / Continuous | Finite or infinite state / action / time values? |
| **Agent Count** | Single / Multi-Agent | How many agents are operating and interacting? |
| **Knowledge** | Known / Unknown | Does the agent know the rules/outcomes a-priori? |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *O-D-E-D-D-A-K* → **"Our Drivers Eat During Dark And Kold"** — for the property order.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Type |
|---------|-----------------|--------------|
| Agent Function | $f : \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Set mapping |
| Rational Action | $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[V \vert \text{percept}]$ | Decision rule |
| Performance Metric | $V = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^{t} \cdot r_{t}$ | Scalar reward sum |
| Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ | Dimensionless |
| Reflex Mapping | $\text{Action} = \text{Rule}(\text{Current Percept})$ | Stateless rule |
| Expected Utility | $EU(a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$ | Probability-weighted |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule:** In markdown tables, replace $\vert$ or $\mid$ symbols inside expressions with the LaTeX commands themselves (already in plain math) — never the literal `|` character, which breaks table rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Robotics

In **PERUT521**, these definitions translate directly to:

* **Autonomous Mobile Robots** (e.g., warehouse AMRs from Amazon/Kiva).
* **Self-Driving Cars** — partially observable, stochastic, multi-agent, dynamic.
* **Vacuum-Cleaning Robots** (Roomba) — episodic when small rooms, sequential when large.
* **Industrial Manipulators** — fully observable, deterministic, single-agent, static.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Theorem (from KTU Board Examiner perspective):**
> *Rationality ≠ Omniscience ≠ Perfection.* A rational agent can fail if its sensors are inadequate or its action set is incomplete — yet it still remains *rational* under its informational constraints.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Expected Performance of a Rational Agent

### Goal
Show how the rational action $a^{*}$ is computed when the environment is **stochastic** (i.e., the same action can lead to multiple outcomes with different probabilities).

### Step 1: Define the Percept History

Let $h_{t}$ denote the sequence of percepts observed up to time step $t$:

$$h_{t} = (p_{1}, p_{2}, p_{3}, \ldots, p_{t})$$

This is the **memory** of the agent. An agent that forgets past percepts is called **stateless** or **reflexive**.

### Step 2: Define the Transition Model of the Environment

The environment, given current state $s$ and action $a$, transitions to state $s'$ with probability:

$$T(s, a, s') = P(s' \mid s, a)$$

This is the **stochastic transition kernel**.

### Step 3: Define the Performance (Reward) Function

The agent receives a scalar reward $r_{t}$ at each time step. The **cumulative discounted reward** is:

$$V = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{t} \cdot r_{t}$$

where:
* $r_{t}$ = reward at time $t$.
* $\gamma$ = discount factor $\in [0,1]$. When $\gamma = 0$, only immediate reward matters. When $\gamma \to 1$, future rewards are valued almost equally.

### Step 4: Compute Expected Utility of an Action

The expected utility of choosing action $a$ in state $s$ is:

$$EU(a \mid s) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$$

where $U(s')$ is the utility (long-term value) of the resulting state.

### Step 5: Select the Rational Action

The rational action is the one with the **highest expected utility**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a^{*} &= \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \; EU(a \mid s) \\
      &= \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \; \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Interpretation

* If $P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$ for exactly one $s'$ (deterministic case), the sum collapses to a single term:

$$a^{*} = \underset{a}{\operatorname{argmax}} \; U(s'(s,a))$$

* If $U(s') = V$ is approximated by the immediate reward $r$, we recover a **greedy policy**.
* If we substitute the optimal action back recursively, we obtain the **Bellman Optimality Equation** (foundation of dynamic programming and reinforcement learning — covered in later modules).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Derivation:** The rational-action rule derived above is the universal blueprint for every decision-making module you'll see in this course — from reflex agents (where $U$ is trivial) to learning agents (where $P$ and $U$ are themselves learned).

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Matrix — PEAS for a Medical Diagnosis Robot

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asks:** *"Design the PEAS specification for an autonomous medical diagnosis system."* Here is the canonical answer.

| Component | Specification |
|-----------|----------------|
| **P**erformance | Correct diagnosis rate, false-negative minimization, patient wait time. |
| **E**nvironment | Hospital ward, patients, medical records, doctors. |
| **A**ctuators | Display screen, prescription printer, alarm, robotic arm for sample handling. |
| **S**ensors | Keyboard, microphone, camera, MRI/CT image input, electronic health record API. |

Environment Classification: **Partially Observable, Stochastic, Sequential, Dynamic, Continuous, Single-Agent, Known.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[Environment]
    SENS[Sensors]
    PERC[Percept]
    PROG[Agent Program]
    ACT[Action]
    ACTR[Actuators]

    ENV -- "state" --> SENS
    SENS -- "raw signals" --> PERC
    PERC -- "percept sequence" --> PROG
    PROG -- "decision" --> ACT
    ACT -- "control signal" --> ACTR
    ACTR -- "physical change" --> ENV

    style ENV fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a4262c
    style SENS fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0364b3
    style PERC fill:#fff4ce,stroke:#946800
    style PROG fill:#d1f7d1,stroke:#2e7d32
    style ACT fill:#fff4ce,stroke:#946800
    style ACTR fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0364b3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Loop:** Time flows clockwise. The *Environment* changes; *Sensors* capture this change as a *percept*; the *Agent Program* reasons over it; an *action* is emitted; *Actuators* apply it to the world; the cycle repeats.

## 4.2 The Five Canonical Agent Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1["(1) Simple Reflex Agent"]
        S1[Sensors] --> W1[Condition-Action Rules]
        W1 --> AC1[Actuators]
    end

    subgraph A2["(2) Model-Based Reflex Agent"]
        S2[Sensors] --> ST[State / World Model]
        ST --> HOW[How the world evolves]
        HOW --> W2[Condition-Action Rules]
        W2 --> AC2[Actuators]
    end

    subgraph A3["(3) Goal-Based Agent"]
        S3[Sensors] --> ST3[State]
        ST3 --> GOAL[Goal Information]
        GOAL --> PL[Planning / Search]
        PL --> AC3[Actuators]
    end

    subgraph A4["(4) Utility-Based Agent"]
        S4[Sensors] --> ST4[State]
        ST4 --> UT[Utility Function]
        UT --> OPT[Maximize Expected Utility]
        OPT --> AC4[Actuators]
    end

    subgraph A5["(5) Learning Agent"]
        S5[Sensors] --> CR[Critic]
        CR --> LP[Learning Element]
        LP --> PE[Performance Element]
        PE --> PG[Problem Generator]
        PG --> AC5[Actuators]
    end

    style A2 fill:#fff0d6
    style A3 fill:#e3f5d6
    style A4 fill:#d6e8f5
    style A5 fill:#ead6f5
```

```mermaid

```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked *"Which agent type suits environment X?"* always match the agent's capability to the environment's complexity. Reflex agents fail in **partially observable** worlds because they have no memory of unobserved state.

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a rational agent. How does it differ from an omniscient agent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **rational agent** is one that, for every possible percept sequence, selects the action that is expected to **maximize its performance measure**, given the percept evidence and its prior knowledge. (2 marks)

An **omniscient agent** knows the actual outcome of its actions and always picks the truly optimal action. Rationality is weaker: a rational agent acts optimally *with respect to its current information*, so it can still fail when its percepts are inadequate. (1 mark)

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Split:** Definition (2) + Rationality ≠ Omniscience distinction (1).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any FOUR properties used to classify task environments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Observability** – Fully vs. Partially observable, depending on whether sensors access all relevant state. (0.75)
2. **Determinism** – Deterministic vs. Stochastic, depending on whether the next state is uniquely determined. (0.75)
3. **Episodicity** – Episodic vs. Sequential, depending on whether each decision depends on prior ones. (0.75)
4. **Dynamics** – Static vs. Dynamic, depending on whether the world changes during deliberation. (0.75)

---

## Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Format:** Each question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.** Solve any **ONE** of the two alternatives provided.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the structure of any **TWO** of the following agents: *(7 marks)*
  (i) Simple Reflex Agent
  (ii) Model-Based Reflex Agent

**(b)** Define the **PEAS framework**. Design the complete PEAS specification for an **autonomous taxi driver**. Classify its environment across all seven properties. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks)

**(i) Simple Reflex Agent (3.5 marks)**

A simple reflex agent selects actions purely on the basis of the **current percept**, ignoring the rest of the percept history.

```
Sensors → Condition-Action Rules → Actuators
```

* Example rule: `IF car-in-front-is-braking THEN initiate-braking`.
* **Limitation:** Will fail in partially observable environments because the same current percept can arise from different unobserved histories (e.g., a stop sign hidden behind a tree).

**(ii) Model-Based Reflex Agent (3.5 marks)**

This agent maintains an **internal state** that depends on the percept history and thereby reflects at least some of the unobserved aspects of the current state.

```
Sensors → State Update → How the World Works (Transition Model) → Rules → Actuators
```

* Two key knowledge components: **(a)** how the world evolves independent of the agent, and **(b)** how the agent's own actions affect the world.
* This internal model enables the agent to handle **partial observability**.

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 marks)

**PEAS Definition (1 mark):** PEAS = **Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors** — the universal descriptor for any rational agent design.

**PEAS for Autonomous Taxi Driver (4 marks):**

| Component | Specification |
|-----------|----------------|
| Performance | Safety, speed, legality, comfort, profit |
| Environment | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather, signals, customers |
| Actuators | Steering, accelerator, brake, signal lights, horn, display |
| Sensors | Cameras, LiDAR, GPS, speedometer, accelerometer, microphone |

**Environment Classification (2 marks):**

| Property | Class |
|----------|-------|
| Observability | Partially |
| Determinism | Stochastic |
| Episodicity | Sequential |
| Dynamics | Dynamic |
| Discreteness | Continuous |
| Agent Count | Multi-Agent |
| Knowledge | Known |

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the mathematical condition for a **rational action** in a stochastic environment, starting from the agent function. Clearly state each assumption. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the **five canonical agent architectures** in a tabular form. For each, give one real-world application scenario. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the agent function mapping.

$$f : \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Introduce the stochastic transition model $P(s' \mid s, a)$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Define the utility of a state $U(s')$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute expected utility of action $a$:

$$EU(a \mid s) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State the rational-action selection rule:

$$a^{*} = \underset{a}{\operatorname{argmax}} \; EU(a \mid s)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Discuss limiting case — deterministic environments reduce the sum to a single term, recovering $\arg\max_{a} U(s'(s,a))$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption to explicitly mention:** The agent knows the transition model $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and the utility function $U(s')$. In **unknown** environments, these must be *learned*, leading naturally to the **learning agent** architecture.

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 marks)

| Agent Type | Internal State? | Goal/Utility? | Learning? | Real-World Example |
|------------|-----------------|---------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Simple Reflex | No | No | No | Thermostat, automatic door |
| Model-Based Reflex | Yes | No | No | Robot navigating a maze using SLAM |
| Goal-Based | Yes | Yes (goal) | No | GPS route planner |
| Utility-Based | Yes | Yes (utility) | No | Stock-trading bot with risk-reward |
| Learning | Yes | Yes | Yes | AlphaGo, autonomous driving AI |

*Each row carries ~1.4 marks.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing rational with omniscient** → 1 mark lost per occurrence. Always emphasize "expected" performance.
> 2. **Forgetting to state assumptions** during derivations (e.g., that the agent knows $P$ and $U$) → 1–2 marks lost.
> 3. **Incomplete PEAS tables** — missing *Performance* measure is the most common omission. Performance is the most critical column because it defines "what success means."
> 4. **Misclassifying environments** — e.g., calling a self-driving car environment "fully observable" or "deterministic." Both are wrong: it's partially observable and stochastic.
> 5. **Diagrams missing arrows/labels** — KTU deducts 0.5–1 mark per unlabeled diagram arrow.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Master These Before Exam Day:**

- [ ] **Agent** = Sensors + Actuators + Decision Logic; perceives and acts.
- [ ] **Agent Function** $f : \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ — abstract mapping.
- [ ] **Agent Program** = concrete implementation of $f$.
- [ ] **Rational Agent** = maximizes **expected** performance, not actual.
- [ ] **Rationality $\neq$ Omniscience** — agents act on available info only.
- [ ] **Autonomy** = ability to compensate for unknown environment via learning.
- [ ] **PEAS** = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors — required for *every* agent design.
- [ ] **Seven Environment Properties** = Observability, Determinism, Episodicity, Dynamics, Discreteness, Agent Count, Knowledge.
- [ ] **Five Agent Architectures** = Simple Reflex → Model-Based Reflex → Goal-Based → Utility-Based → Learning Agent.
- [ ] **Expected Utility Rule**: $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$.
- [ ] **Discounted Reward**: $V = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{t} r_{t}$, $\gamma \in [0,1]$.
- [ ] **Reflex agents fail** in partially observable worlds (no memory).
- [ ] **Learning agents** are required when $P$ and $U$ are unknown.
- [ ] **Bellman Optimality** is the recursive foundation for utility-based and learning agents.
- [ ] In PEAS tables for KTU, **always quantify the performance measure** with concrete metrics.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Memorize the 7 environment properties + 5 agent types in tabular form.
> 2. Practice deriving the rational-action rule at least 3 times.
> 3. Always draw **labeled** diagrams (arrows + node labels) — KTU rewards visual clarity.
> 4. In long answers, *explicitly state assumptions* before derivations.
> 5. End every PEAS answer with a 7-row environment classification table.

topics.title: Good Behaviour - The Concept of Rationality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Good Behaviour - The Concept of Rationality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the formal language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** and the canonical AI textbook framework (Russell & Norvig, *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach*), a **rational agent** is defined as an entity that, for every possible percept sequence, selects an action that is **expected to maximize its performance measure**, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and whatever built-in knowledge the agent possesses.

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition
> **Rational Agent:** *"For each possible percept sequence, a rational agent should select an action that is expected to maximize its performance measure, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and whatever built-in knowledge the agent has."*

Formally, if we let:
- $P$ = the set of all possible percept sequences
- $A$ = the set of all actions the agent can perform
- The **performance measure** evaluates any given sequence of environment states

Then a **rational agent function** $f: P^{*} \rightarrow A$ maps any percept history to the action that maximizes expected performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Self-Driving Car in Kerala Rain

Imagine a **self-driving car** navigating the chaotic traffic of Thiruvananthapuram during a sudden monsoon downpour. The "good behaviour" we expect is **not** that the car magically knows every driver's future move (that would be *omniscience*). Instead, we expect the car to:

1. **Perceive** the slippery road, brake lights ahead, and a pedestrian stepping off the footpath.
2. **Reason** about the situation: "If I brake hard, the biker behind may skid into me."
3. **Act** by gently reducing speed and steering slightly left — the action that, based on available evidence, **maximizes the expected safety outcome**.

This is **rationality** — making the *best expected decision* given what is *known*, not what is *known to exist*. The car is **rational**, not **omniscient**.

## 1.3 Rationality vs. Omniscience vs. Success

> [!NOTE] Three Distinct Concepts
> - **Omniscience** = knowing the *actual* outcome of every action. Impossible in practice.
> - **Rationality** = choosing the action with the *maximum expected performance*, based on current knowledge.
> - **Success / Perfection** = actually achieving the desired outcome. Sometimes rationality fails due to stochastic environments, but the agent was still *rational* in its choice.

## 1.4 The Four Pillars Defining Rational Behaviour

A rational agent's behaviour is fully specified once we define the following:

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Performance Measure** | The yardstick of success (e.g., fuel efficiency + safety + time). |
| **Prior Knowledge** | What the agent knows about the environment before acting. |
| **Possible Actions** | The set $A$ of actions the agent can execute. |
| **Percept Sequence** | Everything the agent has perceived up to the current time step. |

> [!IMPORTANT] Key Insight
> Rationality is **not** the same as **reflex behaviour**, **stochastic action**, or **mimicking human cognition**. It is a purely mathematical optimization over expected outcomes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing Rationality — The Operational Logic

The concept of rationality can be broken down into **five sequential reasoning steps** that any KTU examiner expects a student to articulate:

1. **Step 1 — Goal Articulation:** The designer specifies a *performance measure* (a scalar reward/utility) over environment states.
2. **Step 2 — Percept Processing:** The agent's sensors gather a percept, appending it to the *percept history*.
3. **Step 3 — Belief Update:** The agent updates its internal *belief state* using Bayesian-style inference (see Section 3 for the formal derivation).
4. **Step 4 — Expected Utility Computation:** For every candidate action $a \in A$, the agent computes the **expected performance measure** over all possible environment transitions.
5. **Step 5 — Action Selection:** The agent executes the action $a^{*}$ that maximizes the expected value. Formally:

$$a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in A} \ E[\,U \mid \text{percept sequence}, a\,]$$

> [!NOTE] Why Each Step Matters
> - Skipping Step 1 → the agent has no notion of "good."
> - Skipping Step 2 → the agent is blind to reality.
> - Skipping Step 3 → the agent acts on stale beliefs.
> - Skipping Step 4 → the agent cannot trade off risk vs. reward.
> - Skipping Step 5 → the agent deliberates but never acts (paralysis).

## 2.2 Information Gathering as Rational Action

A critical high-yield sub-concept: **Information gathering is itself a rational action**. A rational agent may choose to perform an exploratory action (e.g., sonar scan, query a sensor) *even when that action has no immediate reward* — because the information gained improves future expected performance.

The formal trigger for information gathering is when:

$$\max_{a \in A} \ E[\,U \mid \text{percept sequence}, a\,] \;<\; \max_{a \in A} \ E[\,U \mid \text{percept sequence}, a, \text{new info}\,]$$

## 2.3 Autonomy — The Limitation of Built-In Knowledge

A **fully autonomous agent** is one whose behaviour is determined by its *own percepts and learning experience*, not solely by its *initial designer-provided knowledge*.

Let $K_0$ be the initial (built-in) knowledge and $K_t$ be the knowledge at time $t$ after $t$ percepts. **Autonomy** is the limit:

$$\text{Autonomy} = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{\vert K_t \setminus K_0 \vert}{\vert K_t \vert}$$

An agent with high autonomy can adapt to novel environments (essential for robotics in unstructured settings like disaster zones).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rational Action** | $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in A} \mathbb{E}[U \mid \text{percepts}, a]$ | Robot decision-making under uncertainty |
| 2 | **Performance Measure** | $V(s) \in \mathbb{R}$ — utility of state $s$ | Objective function for AI design |
| 3 | **Percept Sequence** | $\vec{p} = \langle p_1, p_2, \dots, p_t \rangle$ | Sensor log of a robot |
| 4 | **Agent Function** | $f: P^{*} \rightarrow A$ | Maps full history to action |
| 5 | **Agent Program** | Realization of $f$ on hardware | The actual running code on a robot |
| 6 | **Expected Utility** | $\mathbb{E}[U] = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$ | MDP-style planning |
| 7 | **Information Gain Criterion** | $a_{\text{info}} = \arg\max_{a} \ \mathbb{E}[U \mid a, \text{new evidence}]$ | Active perception, SLAM |
| 8 | **Autonomy Ratio** | $\text{Autonomy} = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{\vert K_t \setminus K_0 \vert}{\vert K_t \vert}$ | Quantifies learning dependence |

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU High-Yield Note
> In valuation, students who confuse the **agent function** (abstract mathematical mapping) with the **agent program** (its concrete implementation) almost always lose 2–3 marks. Remember: *function = mathematical ideal; program = physical realization.*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Performance measure = safety + speed + comfort. Rationality selects the steering angle that maximizes expected safety.
- **Industrial Robotic Arms:** Performance measure = throughput − defect rate. Rationality chooses motion primitives that minimize expected defects.
- **Service Robots (ASIMO, Pepper):** Performance measure = user satisfaction + task completion. Rationality balances verbal acknowledgment with task progress.
- **Drone Swarms:** Performance measure = coverage area − energy spent. Rationality distributes sensing based on marginal information gain.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Performance Measure to Rational Action

We will now rigorously derive the **principle of maximum expected utility (MEU)** from the foundational definition of a rational agent.

### Step 1 — Define the Environment State Space
Let the environment be characterized by a state $s \in S$. The state evolves over time steps $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ according to a transition model.

### Step 2 — Define the Performance Measure
A **performance measure** $U: S \times S \times \dots \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ evaluates any sequence of states. For simplicity, we use a per-step reward $R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})$ that the environment emits.

### Step 3 — Define the Percept Sequence
At time $t$, the agent has observed:

$$\vec{p}_t = \langle p_1, p_2, \dots, p_t \rangle$$

This induces a posterior belief over the current state $s_t$ via Bayes' rule:

$$P(s_t \mid \vec{p}_t) = \frac{P(\vec{p}_t \mid s_t) \cdot P(s_t)}{P(\vec{p}_t)}$$

This step converts raw percepts into a probabilistic belief about the world.

### Step 4 — Compute the Expected Utility of Each Action
For a candidate action $a \in A$, the expected immediate utility is:

$$\mathbb{E}[U_t \mid \vec{p}_t, a] = \sum_{s_t \in S} P(s_t \mid \vec{p}_t) \cdot \sum_{s_{t+1} \in S} P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a) \cdot R(s_t, a, s_{t+1})$$

Substituting the Bayes-rule posterior from Step 3:

$$\mathbb{E}[U_t \mid \vec{p}_t, a] = \sum_{s_t} \sum_{s_{t+1}} \frac{P(\vec{p}_t \mid s_t) P(s_t)}{P(\vec{p}_t)} \cdot P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a) \cdot R(s_t, a, s_{t+1})$$

### Step 5 — Select the Maximizing Action
The rational action is the argmax over all candidate actions:

$$a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in A} \ \mathbb{E}[U_t \mid \vec{p}_t, a]$$

This is the **Principle of Maximum Expected Utility (MEU)**.

### Step 6 — Generalize to Long-Horizon Tasks
If the task has horizon $H$ (number of future steps), the rational agent maximizes the **discounted cumulative expected utility**:

$$\mathbb{E}[U] = \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{H-1} \gamma^{k} R(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}, s_{t+k+1}) \right]$$

where $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the **discount factor**. The rational action is then:

$$a^{*} = \arg\max_{a_{t:t+H-1}} \ \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{H-1} \gamma^{k} R(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}, s_{t+k+1}) \ \Big|\ \vec{p}_t \right]$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping KTU Concepts to Equations

| Concept | Verbal Statement | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Rationality** | "Choose the action with the highest expected performance." | $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[U]$ |
| **Autonomy** | "Knowledge from learning dominates built-in knowledge." | $\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{\vert K_t \setminus K_0 \vert}{\vert K_t \vert} \to 1$ |
| **Information Gathering** | "Explore when exploration raises expected future utility." | $\exists a : \mathbb{E}[U \mid a, \text{info}] > \mathbb{E}[U \mid a]$ |
| **Percept-to-Belief** | "Convert raw sensor data to a probability over states." | $P(s \mid \vec{p}) = \frac{P(\vec{p} \mid s) P(s)}{P(\vec{p})}$ |
| **Long-Horizon Rationality** | "Plan a sequence, not just one step." | $\max_{a_{0:H-1}} \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{k=0}^{H-1} \gamma^{k} R_k\right]$ |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A cleaning robot operates in a 2-cell world. Cell A is dirty (state $s_A$); Cell B is clean (state $s_B$). The performance measure is $-1$ per step, plus $+10$ when the world is fully clean. The robot can *Clean* (utility $+8$, transitions to clean) or *Move* (utility $-1$, transitions to other cell). Current state: $s_A$, dirty. Compute the rational action for a 2-step horizon with $\gamma = 0.9$.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

Step 1: Action = *Clean*.

$$\text{Path: } s_A^{\text{dirty}} \xrightarrow{\text{Clean}} s_A^{\text{clean}} \xrightarrow{\text{Move}} s_B^{\text{clean}}$$

Cumulative reward:

$$U_{\text{Clean}} = (+8) + 0.9 \cdot (+10) = 8 + 9 = 17$$

Step 2: Action = *Move*.
