$$\text{Path: } s_A^{\text{dirty}} \xrightarrow{\text{Move}} s_B^{\text{dirty}} \xrightarrow{\text{Clean}} s_B^{\text{clean}}$$

Cumulative reward:

$$U_{\text{Move}} = (-1) + 0.9 \cdot (-1 + 8) = -1 + 0.9 \cdot 7 = -1 + 6.3 = 5.3$$

Step 3: Compare.

$$a^{*} = \arg\max\{17, \; 5.3\} = \text{Clean}$$

**Conclusion:** The rational action is **Clean** (expected utility $17 > 5.3$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Rational Agent — Architectural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Env[Environment] -->|Percept| Sens[Sensors]
    Sens -->|Raw Data| PercSeq[Percept Sequence]
    PercSeq -->|Bayes Update| Belief[Belief State]
    Belief -->|Probabilities| Planner[Planner / Reasoner]
    Planner -->|Expected Utility| Dec[Action Selector]
    Dec -->|Action a-star| Act[Actuators]
    Act -->|Effect| Env
    PM[Performance Measure] -.->|Defines| Planner
    PK[Prior Knowledge] -.->|Initializes| Belief
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Solid arrows = data flow at runtime.
- Dashed arrows = design-time injection of goals and priors.
- The **Planner** is the seat of rationality — it converts beliefs + goals into the maximizing action.

## 4.2 The PEAS Specification of a Task Environment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Task[Rational Agent Task] --> P[Performance Measure]
    Task --> E[Environment]
    Task --> A[Actuators]
    Task --> S[Sensors]
    P --> P1[Example: safety + time + fuel]
    E --> E1[Example: Kerala highway traffic]
    A --> A1[Example: steering, brake, accelerator]
    S --> S1[Example: camera, LIDAR, GPS]
```

> [!NOTE] PEAS Mnemonic
> **P**erformance, **E**nvironment, **A**ctuators, **S**ensors — the four-part specification that fully defines a rational agent's design problem.

## 4.3 Information Gathering as Rational Action — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Current Belief State] --> Q1{Known enough\nfor safe action?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Act[Execute best action a-star]
    Q1 -- No --> Explore[Perform info-gathering action]
    Explore --> Sens[Sense the environment]
    Sens --> Update[Update belief via Bayes rule]
    Update --> Q1
    Act --> Done[Environment responds]
```

**Interpretation:** The loop on the right (Explore → Sens → Update) embodies **active perception** — the agent is not merely reacting; it is **rationally choosing to learn** when ignorance is costlier than sensing.

## 4.4 Rationality vs. Omniscience — Conceptual Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KNOWN[Known Universe of the Agent]
        A1[Sensed percepts] --> K[Belief State]
        B1[Built-in prior] --> K
        K --> R[Rational Choice]
    end
    subgraph UNK[Unknown to the Agent]
        OS[True future state] -.->|unobservable| K
    end
    R -->|Selected| OUT[Action a-star]
    OUT --> REAL[Real World Outcome]
    REAL -->|Feedback| A1
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Notice the *dashed* arrow from `True future state` to `K` — this represents the *fundamental epistemic gap* that distinguishes a rational agent (who acts on expected outcomes) from an *omniscient* agent (who acts on true outcomes).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
> **Define a rational agent. How is rationality different from omniscience?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **rational agent** is one that, for every possible percept sequence, selects an action that *is expected to maximize its performance measure*, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and the agent's built-in knowledge.

**Rationality vs. Omniscience:**

- *Rationality* concerns **expected** performance based on current information — it is a property of the *decision*, not the outcome.
- *Omniscience* is the impossible ability to know the *actual* result of every action and the *true* state of the world.

An agent may be rational yet fail (e.g., an autonomous car that brakes correctly for a child but the child still gets hurt due to physics), whereas an omniscient agent would have foreseen the outcome. Hence, **omniscience is a stronger (and unattainable) property** than rationality. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **List and briefly explain the four components that fully specify a rational agent's design problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four components — collectively the **PEAS specification** — are:

1. **Performance Measure:** The criterion defining success (e.g., safety, speed, fuel economy for a self-driving car).
2. **Environment:** The external world in which the agent operates (e.g., Kerala urban traffic).
3. **Actuators:** The means by which the agent affects the environment (e.g., steering, brakes, accelerator).
4. **Sensors:** The means by which the agent perceives the environment (e.g., LIDAR, cameras, GPS, IMU).

These four together fully define the design problem of any rational robotic agent. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]*

> **(a)** [7 marks] Define the concept of **rationality** in artificial intelligence. Explain the principle of **maximum expected utility (MEU)** with the help of a suitable mathematical formulation. Why is information gathering considered a rational action?
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A warehouse robot must decide between two actions in a stochastic environment. Action *Pick* yields reward $+10$ with probability $0.8$ and $-2$ with probability $0.2$. Action *Scan* yields reward $+1$ with certainty, but also reveals new information that updates the belief state. After *Scan*, the expected reward of *Pick* rises to $+12$. With a **discount factor $\gamma = 1$** and a **one-step horizon**, determine the rational action. Justify with full computation.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

Rationality, as defined by Russell & Norvig, is the property whereby an agent, for every possible percept sequence, selects the action that is **expected to maximize its performance measure**, given its current knowledge.

**Mathematical Formulation of MEU:**

$$a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in A} \ \mathbb{E}[\,U \mid \vec{p}_t, a\,]$$

For a discrete environment, this expands to:

$$\mathbb{E}[\,U \mid \vec{p}_t, a\,] = \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid \vec{p}_t, a) \cdot U(s')$$

where $P(s' \mid \vec{p}_t, a)$ is the posterior probability of reaching state $s'$ after taking action $a$, given the percept history $\vec{p}_t$.

**Information Gathering as Rational Action:**

Information gathering is rational when the *expected future utility* of acquiring information exceeds the *immediate expected utility* of acting without that information. Formally:

$$a_{\text{info}} \text{ is rational if } \mathbb{E}[U \mid a_{\text{info}}, \text{new evidence}] > \max_{a \in A} \mathbb{E}[U \mid a]$$

This justifies exploratory actions, active perception, and Bayesian sensing in robotics. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Compute the expected utility of *Pick* without scanning.

$$\mathbb{E}[U_{\text{Pick}}] = (0.8)(+10) + (0.2)(-2) = 8.0 - 0.4 = 7.6$$

**Step 2:** Compute the expected utility of *Scan*.

Since *Scan* gives reward $+1$ with certainty and the *future* action *Pick* now yields $+12$:

$$\mathbb{E}[U_{\text{Scan}}] = 1.0 + (1.0)(+12) = 13.0$$

**Step 3:** Compare.

$$\mathbb{E}[U_{\text{Scan}}] = 13.0 > \mathbb{E}[U_{\text{Pick}}] = 7.6$$

**Step 4:** Conclude.

The **rational action is *Scan***, because its expected utility ($13.0$) exceeds that of *Pick* ($7.6$). This also demonstrates that **information gathering is a rational action** — the agent willingly sacrifices immediate reward to obtain information that dramatically improves downstream decisions. **(7 marks)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]*

> **(a)** [7 marks] Differentiate between the **agent function** and the **agent program**. Why is a rational agent not necessarily omniscient? Discuss with an example from robotics.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Explain the concept of **autonomy** in rational agents. A robot initially has $K_0 = 100$ units of built-in knowledge. After $t$ time steps, it has $K_t = 100 + 50 \log(t+1)$ units total. Compute the **autonomy ratio at $t = 99$** and interpret the result.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Agent Function vs. Agent Program:**

- The **agent function** $f: P^{*} \rightarrow A$ is an *abstract mathematical mapping* that specifies, for every possible percept sequence, the action the agent should take. It is an idealized specification.
- The **agent program** is the *concrete implementation* of that function on a physical computational platform. The program runs on the robot's hardware and produces the actual outputs.

The distinction is crucial: the agent function is the *what*, while the agent program is the *how*.

**Why Rationality ≠ Omniscience:**

Rationality requires only that the agent make the *best expected decision* given its current knowledge. It does *not* require the agent to know the true future state. For example, an autonomous car on a highway may *rationally* decide to brake when it perceives a sudden obstacle — but if the road is mysteriously covered in black ice (an unobserved fact), the car may still skid. The decision was rational; the outcome was simply unlucky.

Hence, **rationality is a property of the decision-making process, not of the outcome**, and is fundamentally weaker than omniscience. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Concept of Autonomy:**

Autonomy is the degree to which an agent's behaviour is determined by its own percepts and learning experience, rather than solely by its initial built-in knowledge. A fully autonomous agent improves over time via experience.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$\text{Autonomy}(t) = \frac{\vert K_t \setminus K_0 \vert}{\vert K_t \vert}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $K_t$ at $t = 99$.

$$K_{99} = 100 + 50 \log(99 + 1) = 100 + 50 \log(100) = 100 + 50 \cdot 2 = 200$$

**Step 2:** Compute the learned (acquired) knowledge.

$$\vert K_{99} \setminus K_0 \vert = 200 - 100 = 100$$

**Step 3:** Compute the autonomy ratio.

$$\text{Autonomy}(99) = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5 = 50\%$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

At $t = 99$ time steps, **50% of the robot's total knowledge** is *learned from experience* rather than built-in. As $t \to \infty$, the $\log(t+1)$ term grows unboundedly, so the autonomy ratio approaches 1 — meaning the robot eventually becomes almost entirely *self-taught*, exhibiting true autonomy. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing agent function with agent program** → lose 2 marks. Remember: function = abstract math; program = concrete code.
> 2. **Stating "rationality = perfection"** → lose 1–2 marks. Rationality is about *expected* performance, not actual outcomes.
> 3. **Forgetting the role of performance measure** in PEAS → lose 1 mark. Always begin any rationality discussion by stating the performance measure.
> 4. **Mixing up autonomy with omniscience** → lose 2 marks. Autonomy is about *learning*, omniscience is about *knowing everything in advance*.
> 5. **Skipping the Bayes' rule derivation in long answers** → lose up to 2 marks. Examiners reward explicit percept-to-belief transitions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Rational Agent Definition:** Selects the action that *maximizes expected performance measure* for every percept sequence.
- **Rationality ≠ Omniscience:** Rationality is about decisions given current knowledge; omniscience is impossible perfect foresight.
- **Rationality ≠ Success:** A rational agent can still fail due to stochastic environments; rationality is judged by the decision quality, not the outcome.
- **MEU Principle:** $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in A} \mathbb{E}[U \mid \vec{p}_t, a]$ — the cornerstone equation.
- **PEAS Specification:** Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors — the four-part design framework.
- **Information Gathering is Rational** when the expected future utility post-information exceeds the current best action's expected utility.
- **Autonomy Ratio:** $\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{\vert K_t \setminus K_0 \vert}{\vert K_t \vert} \to 1$ for a fully learning agent.
- **Agent Function vs. Agent Program:** Function = abstract mathematical mapping; Program = its physical implementation on the robot.
- **Bayes' Rule is the Engine of Rationality:** It converts percept sequences into posterior beliefs that drive action selection.
- **Discounted Cumulative Utility:** For long-horizon tasks, use $\sum_{k=0}^{H-1} \gamma^{k} R_k$ with $\gamma \in [0,1]$.
- **Performance Measure is the Moral Compass:** Always ask "according to *what* measure is the agent rational?" — this determines everything.
- **KTU Hot Words to Use in Answers:** "expected," "argmax," "performance measure," "percept sequence," "belief state," "autonomy."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nature of Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nature of Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Artificial Intelligence for Robotics**, an **Environment** is the external physical or simulated world within which an intelligent agent (robot) operates, perceives sensory inputs, and executes actions to achieve its predefined goals. The **Nature of Environments** refers to the formal classification of operating worlds based on observable properties such as observability, determinism, dynamics, and the number of co-existing agents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students must be able to classify any given robotic/AI problem into the correct environment type. Board examiners frequently test the ability to *justify* the classification using real-world examples (vacuum cleaner, self-driving car, chess engine, etc.).

The classical framework used in AI literature (Russell & Norvig) defines the environment through the **PEAS** descriptor:
- **P** — Performance Measure
- **E** — Environment (the topic of our discussion)
- **A** — Actuators
- **S** — Sensors

The nature of the **E** in PEAS governs the *entire* design of the agent architecture (reactive, deliberative, hybrid, learning-based).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robot in a Dark Warehouse"

Imagine two robots dropped into a logistics warehouse:

- **Robot A** receives perfect GPS coordinates, a complete map, and all obstacles are stationary. Every action produces a *guaranteed* outcome.
- **Robot B** operates with a foggy camera, slippery floors, moving workers, and unpredictable network latency.

Both robots have identical hardware, yet **Robot B is exponentially harder to program**. *Why?* Because the **Nature of the Environment** dictates the complexity of the agent's reasoning engine. Environment classification is the first step an AI engineer performs before writing a single line of code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The environment is *external* to the agent. It is the "universe" the agent must reason about. The agent does not control the environment; it only **perceives** and **acts** upon it.

---

### 1.3 The Eight Canonical Properties of an Environment

The KTU 2024 syllabus, aligned with standard AI curriculum, expects students to classify environments along **eight** orthogonal axes. A single environment may be a combination (e.g., *partially observable, stochastic, dynamic, continuous, multi-agent*).

The eight properties are:

1. **Fully Observable vs. Partially Observable**
2. **Deterministic vs. Stochastic**
3. **Episodic vs. Sequential**
4. **Static vs. Dynamic**
5. **Discrete vs. Continuous**
6. **Single-Agent vs. Multi-Agent**
7. **Known vs. Unknown**
8. **Accessible vs. Inaccessible**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical constants/metrics associated with environments:**
> - **Frame Rate (Hz):** Sensor refresh frequency for dynamic environments (typical range **10 Hz – 100 Hz**).
> - **State Space Cardinality:** The total number of distinct configurations an environment can assume. A chessboard has $\vert S \vert = 10^{47}$ states, while a tic-tac-toe board has only $\vert S \vert = 5{,}478$ states.

---

### 1.4 Quick-Reference Summary Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Recap:**
> - **Observable:** Can the agent see the *complete* state via sensors?
> - **Deterministic:** Is the next state a *certain* function of the current state and action?
> - **Episodic:** Does the current decision depend *only* on the current episode?
> - **Static:** Does the world change while the agent deliberates?
> - **Discrete:** Are perceptions/actions drawn from a *finite* set?
> - **Single-Agent:** Is the agent operating *alone*, or competing/cooperating with others?

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Environments (Operational Breakdown)

Below is an exhaustive, structured breakdown of each environment property, its operational meaning, the agent design it demands, and a canonical robotic/AI example.

---

#### **A. Observability Axis**

| Aspect | Fully Observable | Partially Observable |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Agent's sensors give it access to the *complete* state of the world at every time step. | Critical state variables are *hidden* (sensor noise, occlusion, range limits). |
| **Mathematical Form** | $\text{Percept}_t = S_t$ (percept equals the true state) | $\text{Percept}_t \subset S_t$ (percept is a proper subset) |
| **Agent Design** | Simple reflex / model-based reflex agents are sufficient. | Requires **belief states**, **probabilistic reasoning**, or **memory** (POMDP framework). |
| **Example** | Chess engine with full board access. | Poker game (opponent's cards hidden). |
| **Robotic Example** | Industrial arm inside a controlled cage. | Autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) in murky water. |

---

#### **B. Determinism Axis**

| Aspect | Deterministic | Stochastic |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Next state is fully determined by the current state and action. | Next state is governed by a *probability distribution*. |
| **Mathematical Form** | $S_{t+1} = f(S_t, A_t)$ | $P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t)$ — a conditional probability distribution. |
| **Agent Design** | Search/planning algorithms (A*, Dijkstra) work. | Requires **Markov Decision Processes (MDP)**, reinforcement learning. |
| **Example** | Solved maze. | Self-driving car on a public road. |

---

#### **C. Episodicity Axis**

| Aspect | Episodic | Sequential |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Each episode (percept-action pair) is *independent*. | Current decision affects all future decisions. |
| **Mathematical Form** | $\text{Utility} = \sum_i U_i(\text{episode}_i)$ | $U = U(s_0, a_0, s_1, a_1, \dots, s_n)$ |
| **Agent Design** | Stateless classifiers are acceptable. | Requires planning, lookahead, memory. |
| **Example** | Defect detection on an assembly line. | Long-horizon household cleaning robot. |

---

#### **D. Static vs. Dynamic Axis**

| Aspect | Static | Dynamic | Semidynamic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | World is unchanged while the agent decides. | World evolves *continuously*. | World itself is static, but the agent's **performance score** changes with time. |
| **Implication** | Agent may "pause to think". | Real-time constraint $t_{\text{decide}} < t_{\text{state-change}}$. | Latency optimization required. |
| **Example** | Crossword puzzle. | RoboCup soccer. | Chess with a game clock. |

---

#### **E. Continuity Axis**

| Aspect | Discrete | Continuous |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A finite (or countably infinite) number of distinct states/actions. | Infinite, uncountable state space (real numbers). |
| **Mathematical Form** | $S \in \mathbb{Z}^n$ | $S \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| **Example** | Rubik's cube moves. | Robotic arm joint angles in radians. |

---

#### **F. Agent Multiplicity Axis**

| Aspect | Single-Agent | Multi-Agent (Competitive) | Multi-Agent (Cooperative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Only one decision-maker. | Agents have *conflicting* goals (zero-sum). | Agents share a common utility. |
| **Example** | Solitaire. | Adversarial chess. | Warehouse fleet coordination. |

---

#### **G. Known vs. Unknown**

| Aspect | Known | Unknown |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The agent *knows* the rules of the environment a priori. | The agent must *learn* the transition probabilities. |
| **Note** | A *known* environment may still be *partially observable*. | Pure exploration problem (model-based RL). |

---

#### **H. Accessibility**

| Aspect | Accessible | Inaccessible |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | All relevant state information can be retrieved from percepts. | Some state variables are fundamentally unreachable. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Knowing the environment classification upfront allows the AI engineer to **pre-select** the right algorithm family — search (deterministic), MDP (stochastic), RL (unknown), POMDP (partially observable). This avoids costly mid-development architectural pivots.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Observability:}\quad & \text{Full} \iff \text{Percept}_t = S_t \\
\textbf{Determinism:}\quad & \text{Det} \iff P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t) = 1 \\
\textbf{Static Constraint:}\quad & t_{\text{decide}} < \Delta t_{\text{world-change}} \\
\textbf{Discrete:}\quad & \vert S \vert < \infty \\
\textbf{Continuous:}\quad & S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n \\
\textbf{Single-Agent Utility:}\quad & U = R(s, a) \\
\textbf{Multi-Agent Zero-Sum:}\quad & U_{\text{agent}} = -U_{\text{opponent}} \\
\textbf{Multi-Agent Coop:}\quad & U_{\text{team}} = \sum_{i} U_i
\end{aligned}
$$

| Property | Binary Value 0 | Binary Value 1 | Robotic Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Observability | Partially Observable | Fully Observable | Memory needed? |
| Determinism | Stochastic | Deterministic | Need MDP? |
| Episodicity | Sequential | Episodic | Need planning? |
| Dynamics | Dynamic | Static | Need real-time OS? |
| Continuity | Continuous | Discrete | Need calculus? |
| Multiplicity | Multi-agent | Single-agent | Need game theory? |
| Knowledge | Unknown | Known | Need exploration? |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Formal State-Space Formulation

To rigorously classify an environment, we model it as a **tuple** $\langle S, A, T, R, \Omega, O \rangle$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1 — Define the State Space } S: \quad & S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\} \text{ is the set of all possible world configurations.} \\
\textbf{Step 2 — Define the Action Set } A: \quad & A = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_m\} \text{ is the set of actions available to the agent.} \\
\textbf{Step 3 — Transition Function } T: \quad & T: S \times A \rightarrow P(S) \text{ (Stochastic) OR } T: S \times A \rightarrow S \text{ (Deterministic).} \\
\textbf{Step 4 — Reward Function } R: \quad & R: S \times A \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \\
\textbf{Step 5 — Observation Set } \Omega: \quad & \Omega = \{o_1, \dots, o_k\} \\
\textbf{Step 6 — Observation Function } O: \quad & O: S \times A \rightarrow P(\Omega)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Explanation (Step 3):**
> In a **deterministic** environment, applying action $a$ in state $s$ *always* produces the *same* next state $s'$. Hence $T$ is a function, not a probability distribution. In a **stochastic** environment, $T$ returns an entire *probability distribution* over next states.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Classifying a "Roomba Vacuum Cleaner"

We now rigorously classify a domestic robotic vacuum cleaner.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{State } s: \quad & s = \langle x, y, \theta, \text{battery}, \text{dirt-map} \rangle \\
\textbf{Action } a: \quad & a \in \{\text{move-forward}, \text{turn-left}, \text{turn-right}, \text{stop}\} \\
\textbf{Observation } o: \quad & o = \langle \text{IR-sensor-readings}, \text{cliff-sensor-readings}, \text{bumper-state} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification (with justification):**

1. **Observability → Partially Observable.**
   The robot cannot see the *entire* floor plan at once; it only knows the regions it has visited. $\text{Percept}_t \subset S_t$.

2. **Determinism → Stochastic.**
   Wheel slippage on rugs and unpredictable dirt distribution make next-state outcomes probabilistic.

3. **Episodicity → Sequential.**
   The decision to recharge the battery affects *all* future cleaning decisions.

4. **Dynamics → Dynamic.**
   The world changes (dust settles, chairs move, pets walk) while the agent computes.

5. **Continuity → Continuous.**
   Position $(x,y)$ is in $\mathbb{R}^2$, heading $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ — uncountable.

6. **Multiplicity → Single-Agent.**
   No other intelligent agent is competing or cooperating (ignoring pets as agents).

7. **Knowledge → Known.**
   The manufacturer hard-codes the room geometry and sensor model.

> **Conversion Logic:** Each binary property has been evaluated against the formal tuple $\langle S, A, T, R, \Omega, O \rangle$. The Roomba qualifies as a **partially-observable, stochastic, sequential, dynamic, continuous, single-agent, known** environment — one of the *hardest* realistic robotic settings.

---

### 3.3 Decision Matrix — "Which Environment Class Am I In?"

Use this analytical checklist when solving exam questions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Check 1 — Observability:} \quad & \text{Can ALL state variables be read from sensors?} \\
& \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Fully Observable.} \\
& \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Partially Observable.} \\
\textbf{Check 2 — Determinism:} \quad & \text{Does the same (s,a) ALWAYS yield the same s'?} \\
& \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Deterministic.} \\
& \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Stochastic.} \\
\textbf{Check 3 — Episodicity:} \quad & \text{Does the current decision depend on past decisions?} \\
& \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Sequential.} \\
& \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Episodic.} \\
\textbf{Check 4 — Dynamics:} \quad & \text{Does the world change during deliberation?} \\
& \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Dynamic.} \\
& \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Static.} \\
\textbf{Check 5 — Continuity:} \quad & \text{Are states/actions from a countable set?} \\
& \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Discrete.} \\
& \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Continuous.} \\
\textbf{Check 6 — Multiplicity:} \quad & \text{Are there other goal-driven entities?} \\
& \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Single-Agent.} \\
& \text{Yes, adversarial} \rightarrow \text{Multi-Agent Competitive.} \\
& \text{Yes, allied} \rightarrow \text{Multi-Agent Cooperative.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Exercise — State-Space Cardinality

For a chess environment:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert S \vert_{\text{chess}} & = \text{(Pieces)} \times \text{(Squares)}^N \\
& = 32 \times 64^{32} / \text{(illegal states)} \\
& \approx 10^{47} \quad \text{(Shannon's estimate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a tic-tac-toe environment:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert S \vert_{\text{ttt}} & = 3^9 \quad \text{(each cell: empty, X, O)} \\
& = 19{,}683 \text{ raw states} \\
& \approx 5{,}478 \text{ legal states}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Chess state-space cardinality ($10^{47}$) is approximately $10^{43}$ times larger than tic-tac-toe ($5{,}478$). This numerical disparity is *why* Deep Blue can brute-force portions of chess but a hand-coded tic-tac-toe AI solves it trivially.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

This diagram illustrates the canonical **perception–cognition–action** cycle in which the agent engages with the environment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Environment E"] -->|Percept via Sensors| nodeB["Agent Perception Module"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Belief State Update"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Decision Policy π"]
    nodeD -->|Action| nodeE["Actuator Interface"]
    nodeE --> nodeA
    nodeA -.->|Reward / Penalty| nodeD
```

> **Reading the loop:** The environment issues a percept to the agent, the agent updates its belief state, evaluates its policy, and dispatches an action through its actuators, which modifies the environment. This loop repeats at a frequency bounded by the **dynamics** of the environment.

---

### 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Environment Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Any Robotic AI Environment"]
    nodeStart --> nodeA1["Observability Axis"]
    nodeStart --> nodeA2["Determinism Axis"]
    nodeStart --> nodeA3["Dynamics Axis"]
    nodeStart --> nodeA4["Agent Count Axis"]

    nodeA1 --> nodeB1["Fully Observable"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB2["Partially Observable"]

    nodeA2 --> nodeB3["Deterministic"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB4["Stochastic"]

    nodeA3 --> nodeB5["Static"]
    nodeA3 --> nodeB6["Dynamic"]
    nodeA3 --> nodeB7["Semidynamic"]

    nodeA4 --> nodeB8["Single Agent"]
    nodeA4 --> nodeB9["Multi-Agent Competitive"]
    nodeA4 --> nodeB10["Multi-Agent Cooperative"]

    nodeB1 --> nodeC1["Example: Chess Engine"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2["Example: Autonomous Car"]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC3["Example: Path Planner in Known Grid"]
    nodeB4 --> nodeC4["Example: Stock Trading Bot"]
    nodeB5 --> nodeC5["Example: Crossword Puzzle"]
    nodeB6 --> nodeC6["Example: Humanoid Robot"]
    nodeB7 --> nodeC7["Example: Real Time Strategy Game with Clock"]
    nodeB8 --> nodeC8["Example: Pick and Place Arm"]
    nodeB9 --> nodeC9["Example: RoboCup Soccer"]
    nodeB10 --> nodeC10["Example: Swarm Robotics"]
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mapping Environment to Agent Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeEnv["Environment Type Detected"]
    nodeEnv --> nodeDec1{"Fully Observable?"}
    nodeDec1 -->|Yes| nodeArch1["Reflex Agent"]
    nodeDec1 -->|No| nodeArch2["Belief-Based POMDP Agent"]

    nodeEnv --> nodeDec2{"Deterministic?"}
    nodeDec2 -->|Yes| nodeArch3["A Star Search Planner"]
    nodeDec2 -->|No| nodeArch4["MDP / Reinforcement Learning"]

    nodeEnv --> nodeDec3{"Single Agent?"}
    nodeDec3 -->|Yes| nodeArch5["Solo Decision Logic"]
    nodeDec3 -->|No| nodeArch6["Game Theoretic Module"]
```

> **Functional Mapping Insight:** The block diagram illustrates how each detected environment property triggers a different architectural submodule within the robot's software stack. This is the *practical translation* of environment classification into engineering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`
**Define an "environment" in the context of Artificial Intelligence for Robotics. Differentiate between a fully observable and a partially observable environment with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **environment** is the external world (physical or simulated) in which an AI/robotic agent operates by perceiving through sensors and acting through actuators to maximize its performance measure (3 marks — full credit for PEAS-aware definition).

**Differentiation:**

| Fully Observable | Partially Observable |
|---|---|
| Sensors reveal the *complete* state of the world. | Critical state variables are hidden. |
| Example: A chess engine sees the whole board. | Example: A poker agent cannot see opponent cards. |

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the difference between a deterministic and a stochastic environment? State one robotic example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Deterministic environment:** The next state is uniquely determined by the current state and the agent's action. $S_{t+1} = f(S_t, A_t)$.
- **Stochastic environment:** The next state is governed by a probability distribution. $S_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \mid S_t, A_t)$.

**Robotic examples:**

- **Deterministic:** A pick-and-place arm operating in a structured bin with calibrated coordinates (1 mark).
- **Stochastic:** A Mars rover traversing sandy terrain where wheel slippage introduces uncertainty (1 mark).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **ONE** full question. Each question has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each.

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Classify the following environments along the **first four** properties (observability, determinism, episodicity, dynamics). Justify each classification.

1. An autonomous self-driving car in city traffic.
2. A chess engine competing against a human.
3. An industrial robotic arm spot-welding car bodies on an assembly line.

**(b)** Explain the **PEAS** descriptor. For the same three scenarios in part (a), construct the PEAS table.

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Scenario 1: Autonomous Self-Driving Car in City Traffic**

| Property | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Observability | **Partially Observable** | Sensors (cameras, LiDAR) suffer from occlusion, blind spots, weather noise. |
| Determinism | **Stochastic** | Pedestrians, other drivers, road conditions introduce probabilistic outcomes. |
| Episodicity | **Sequential** | A braking decision affects all subsequent driving maneuvers. |
| Dynamics | **Dynamic** | The world evolves continuously; reaction time is bounded. |

**Scenario 2: Chess Engine vs Human**

| Property | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Observability | **Fully Observable** | Both players see all 64 squares and all 32 pieces. |
| Determinism | **Deterministic** | A move has exactly one legal outcome. |
| Episodicity | **Sequential** | Each move constrains future options. |
| Dynamics | **Semidynamic** | Board is static, but the chess clock penalizes slow decisions. |

**Scenario 3: Industrial Welding Arm**

| Property | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Observability | **Fully Observable** | Car body positions are pre-calibrated. |
| Determinism | **Deterministic** | Joint angles map to exact XYZ positions. |
| Episodicity | **Episodic** | Each weld is an independent task (no inter-weld dependency). |
| Dynamics | **Static** | The car body does not move during the weld (though the arm itself moves). |

> *Award full 7 marks: ~1.5 marks per scenario, ~0.4 per cell of justification.*

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 marks]

**PEAS = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors.**

**PEAS Table:**

| Agent | Performance (P) | Environment (E) | Actuators (A) | Sensors (S) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Self-Driving Car | Safety, speed, comfort, fuel economy | Roads, traffic, weather, pedestrians | Steering, accelerator, brake, signals, horn | Cameras, LiDAR, GPS, IMU, radar, ultrasonic |
| Chess Engine | Win/draw/loss ratio | 8x8 board, opponent moves | Move generator, evaluation output | Board-state encoder (digital) |
| Welding Arm | Weld quality, cycle time | Car body on conveyor, fixtures | Servo motors at 6 joints, weld torch | Encoders, force sensors, position switches |

> *Award 7 marks: 1 mark for PEAS definition + ~0.7 marks per cell across the 12 cells (3 scenarios x 4 columns).*

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the **agent–environment interaction loop**. Define the formal tuple $\langle S, A, T, R, \Omega, O \rangle$ and explain how each symbol contributes to environment classification.

**(b)** Discuss how the **nature of the environment** dictates the choice of agent architecture. Provide at least two contrasting examples (e.g., reflex vs. learning-based).

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Agent–Environment Interaction Loop (refer to Section 4.1 diagram).**

The loop proceeds as follows:
1. The environment is in state $S_t$.
2. The agent receives an observation $O_t$ via its sensors.
3. The agent updates its internal belief state and selects an action $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid \text{belief})$.
4. The actuator dispatches $A_t$, transitioning the environment to $S_{t+1}$ via $T$.
5. The agent receives reward $R_{t+1}$.

**Tuple Definition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S & : \text{Set of all world states.} \\
A & : \text{Set of all legal actions.} \\
T : S \times A & \rightarrow P(S) \text{ — Transition model.} \\
R : S \times A & \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \text{ — Reward function.} \\
\Omega & : \text{Set of all possible percepts.} \\
O : S \times A & \rightarrow P(\Omega) \text{ — Observation model.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**How each symbol aids classification:**

- If $T$ is deterministic (single outcome), environment is **deterministic**; else **stochastic**.
- If $O(s,a) = s$, environment is **fully observable**; else **partially observable**.
- If $S$ is countable, environment is **discrete**; else **continuous**.

> *Award 7 marks: 2 marks loop explanation, 3 marks tuple, 2 marks classification linkage.*

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 marks]

The nature of the environment constrains the agent design as follows:

**Example 1 — Reflex Agent (Thermostat):**
- **Environment:** Fully observable, deterministic, episodic, static, discrete, single-agent.
- **Architecture:** Condition–action rules (IF temperature > 25°C THEN turn OFF).
- **Reasoning:** No need for memory or planning — percept alone is sufficient.

**Example 2 — Reinforcement Learning Agent (Backgammon AI):**
- **Environment:** Fully observable, stochastic, sequential, static, discrete, multi-agent competitive.
- **Architecture:** Deep Q-Network / TD-learning with value function approximation.
- **Reasoning:** Stochastic opponent + sequential decisions demand learning a value function over states.

**Example 3 — POMDP Agent (Autonomous Vacuum):**
- **Environment:** Partially observable, stochastic, sequential, dynamic, continuous, single-agent.
- **Architecture:** Belief-based state estimator + policy over belief states.
- **Reasoning:** Sensor uncertainty mandates a maintained belief distribution.

> *Award 7 marks: 1 mark introduction + 2 marks each for two fully-justified examples.*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing "known" with "observable".** A chess environment is *known* (rules are known) AND *fully observable*. A poker environment is *known* (rules are known) BUT *partially observable*. Do **not** use these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Confusing "static" with "deterministic".** Static refers to *time*, deterministic refers to *causation*. A crossword is static and deterministic; a robot arm is dynamic and deterministic.
> 3. **Forgetting the multi-agent axis.** If a robot operates in a space with humans, the environment is multi-agent — examiners *expect* this annotation.
> 4. **In PEAS questions, omitting Actuators and Sensors.** Many students write only "Performance + Environment" and lose 2 marks per scenario.
> 5. **Writing vague justifications** like *"the environment is dynamic because it's complex"*. Use crisp engineering language: *"the environment is dynamic because pedestrians modify the state space within a sub-second interval."*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Nature of Environments:**
>
> - **Environment =** the external world an AI/robotic agent must perceive and act upon.
> - **PEAS Descriptor =** Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors.
> - **Eight Classification Axes:** Observability, Determinism, Episodicity, Dynamics, Continuity, Multiplicity, Knowledge, Accessibility.
> - **Fully Observable:** $O_t = S_t$.
> - **Partially Observable:** $O_t \subsetneq S_t$ — requires POMDP.
> - **Deterministic:** $S_{t+1} = f(S_t, A_t)$.
> - **Stochastic:** $S_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \mid S_t, A_t)$ — requires MDP.
> - **Episodic:** Each task is independent — no memory needed.
> - **Sequential:** Decisions affect future states — needs planning.
> - **Static:** World does not change during deliberation.
> - **Dynamic:** World evolves in real-time — bounded reaction time.
> - **Semidynamic:** World is static, but performance decays with time.
> - **Discrete:** Countable state-action space.
> - **Continuous:** Real-valued state-action space (e.g., joint angles in $\mathbb{R}^n$).
> - **Single-Agent:** One decision-maker.
> - **Multi-Agent Competitive:** Zero-sum or general-sum game theory.
> - **Multi-Agent Cooperative:** Shared utility function.
> - **Agent–Environment Loop:** Percept → Belief Update → Policy → Action → Environment Transition → Reward.
> - **Chess:** $10^{47}$ states; **Tic-Tac-Toe:** $5{,}478$ legal states.
> - **Architecture Selection Rule:** Reflex (simple envs), Model-based (observable stochastic), POMDP (partial obs), RL (unknown envs).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure of Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Structure of Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Artificial Intelligence for Robotics**, an **Agent** is formally defined as any entity that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through **sensors** and acting upon that environment through **actuators** (or effectors). The discipline of designing, analyzing, and implementing such entities is known as **Agentic AI** or **Intelligent Agent Design**.

The **Structure of an Agent** refers to the internal architecture that maps the agent's **perceptual history** (the complete sequence of all sensory inputs up to the present time) to a corresponding **action**. Mathematically, this is an abstracted function $f$:

$$f: P^* \rightarrow A$$

Where:
- $P^*$ denotes the set of all possible finite sequences of percepts.
- $A$ denotes the set of all possible actions available to the agent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> The course PERUT521 explicitly requires students to understand **agent architectures**, distinguish between **rational vs. omniscient agents**, classify **environment types** (fully vs. partially observable, deterministic vs. stochastic, etc.), and analyze the **four foundational agent programs**: Simple Reflex, Model-Based Reflex, Goal-Based, and Utility-Based.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chess player** sitting in front of a board.

- Your **eyes** act as *sensors*, perceiving the positions of all pieces, the threats, and opportunities on the board (the **percept**).
- Your **brain** processes this entire perceptual history (every move made so far) to decide the best next move.
- Your **hand moving a piece** is the *actuator* executing the **action**.

You do not have access to your opponent's strategy in their head (partial observability), and you cannot see the future (lack of omniscience). Yet, you try to make the *best possible move* given what you have perceived. **That is precisely the role of a rational agent.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rational ≠ Omniscient**
> A *rational agent* maximizes its **expected performance** given its percept sequence. An *omniscient agent* knows the actual outcome of its actions. In robotics, we always design for **rationality**, because omniscience is physically and computationally impossible.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Performance Measure | $PM$ | Criterion defining success (e.g., distance traveled, accuracy). |
| Percept | $p_t$ | Sensory input at discrete time $t$. |
| Percept Sequence | $\langle p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_t \rangle$ | Complete history observed by the agent. |
| Action Set | $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$ | Finite/discrete actions available. |
| Environment State | $E_t$ | The world configuration at time $t$. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Agent Function in Detail

The **agent function** is a mathematical idealization. For any given percept sequence, the agent function dictates the action. The **agent program** is the concrete implementation of this function running on the physical architecture.

For a **vacuum-cleaner world** with two locations $A$ and $B$, the percept could be:
$$p_t = [Location, Status]$$
where $Location \in \{A, B\}$ and $Status \in \{Clean, Dirty\}$.

The mapping could be specified as a lookup table (idealized function) or computed through a program (real implementation).

### 2.2 Rationality — The Core PEAE Framework

An agent is **rational** if and only if it selects an action that is expected to maximize its **performance measure**, based on:
1. Its **percept sequence** (evidence of the environment's state).
2. Its **prior built-in knowledge** of the environment.
3. The **available actions** it can perform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A rational agent is not static. It **learns from percepts** and updates its internal state, making it **autonomous** to the maximum extent possible.

### 2.3 The PEAS Specification (High-Yield)

Every KTU robot design problem begins with a **PEAS** description:

- **P** — Performance Measure
- **E** — Environment
- **A** — Actuators
- **S** — Sensors

**Example — Autonomous Taxi:**
- **P:** Safety, speed, legality, comfort, profit.
- **E:** Roads, traffic, weather, pedestrians.
- **A:** Steering wheel, accelerator, brake, signal, horn.
- **S:** Cameras, GPS, sonar, speedometer, accelerometer.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Expression / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Agent Function | $f: P^* \rightarrow A$ | Maps history to action |
| 2 | Percept at time $t$ | $p_t \in P$ | Single sensory input |
| 3 | Percept Sequence | $P^* = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_t\}$ | All inputs up to time $t$ |
| 4 | Rationality | $\max \mathbb{E}[U(s_{t+1}) \mid p_1, \ldots, p_t]$ | Expected utility maximization |
| 5 | Performance Measure | $PM = \sum_{t=0}^{T} R(s_t, a_t)$ | Cumulative reward, dimensionless |
| 6 | Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ | Used in MDP/utility agents |
| 7 | Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Long-horizon objective |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In autonomous vehicles and robotic surgery arms, the agent function is often replaced with a **deep neural network policy** $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$, where $\theta$ are learned parameters. The PEAS framework remains the universal specification language.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Percept Sequence to Action (Vacuum World)

**Problem:** A two-cell vacuum world has a rational agent. The agent starts in an unknown cell. Available actions are $Left$, $Right$, and $Suck$. Percepts are $[Location, Status]$. Construct the agent function table for the *idealized rational agent*.

**Step 1 — Define the Percept Space**

Each percept is a tuple of two elements: location and dirt status.

$$p_t = (loc_t, dirt_t), \quad loc_t \in \{A, B\}, \quad dirt_t \in \{Clean, Dirty\}$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate all possible percepts**

There are exactly $2 \times 2 = 4$ unique percepts:

$$P = \{(A, Clean), (A, Dirty), (B, Clean), (B, Dirty)\}$$

**Step 3 — Define the Action Set**

The agent can perform one of three discrete actions:

$$A = \{Left, Right, Suck\}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Rationality Principle**

A rational agent must *suck* when the current cell is dirty (direct performance benefit), and must move to the *other* cell when both cells are clean and visited, or when the current cell is clean but the other is unknown/dirty.

**Step 5 — Construct the Tabular Agent Function**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\langle (A, Clean) \rangle) &= Right \\
f(\langle (A, Dirty) \rangle) &= Suck \\
f(\langle (B, Clean) \rangle) &= Left \\
f(\langle (B, Dirty) \rangle) &= Suck
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Generalize to History-Dependent Function**

For more complex environments, the agent must retain internal state. Let the internal state be $I_t$. The update rule is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{t+1} &= Update(I_t, p_t) \\
a_t &= Action(I_t, p_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

This two-stage formulation is the **core of every agent architecture**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Classification of Environments

| Environment Property | Definition | Vacuum World | Taxi Driver | Chess | Image Classifier |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Observable** | Full state accessible via sensors | Fully | Partially | Fully | Fully |
| **Deterministic** | Next state is fully determined by current state + action | Deterministic | Stochastic | Deterministic | Deterministic |
| **Episodic** | Experience divided into atomic episodes | Episodic | Sequential | Sequential | Episodic |
| **Static** | Environment unchanged while agent deliberates | Static | Dynamic | Static | Static |
| **Discrete** | Finite number of actions/percepts | Discrete | Continuous | Discrete | Discrete |
| **Single-Agent** | Only one agent operating | Single | Multi | Multi | Single |

### 3.3 Derivation: Expected Utility for a Utility-Based Agent

Given a sequence of rewards $R_{t+1}, R_{t+2}, \ldots$, the **expected discounted return** the agent tries to maximize is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t &\triangleq R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots \\
G_t &= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The agent chooses the action that maximizes the **expected value** of $G_t$:

$$a_t^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} \, \mathbb{E}\left[ G_t \,\vert\, s_t = s, a_t = a \right]$$

Where $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the discount factor. As $\gamma \to 0$, the agent becomes *myopic* (only immediate reward matters). As $\gamma \to 1$, the agent becomes *far-sighted*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The 14-mark derivations on agent structure usually expect students to explicitly state the **update rule** $I_{t+1} = Update(I_t, p_t)$ and **action selection rule** $a_t = Action(I_t, p_t)$ before mapping them to a specific agent architecture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Agent Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensIn["Sensors"] --> percept["Percept p_t"]
    percept --> cond["Condition-Action Rules"]
    cond --> effOut["Effectors"]
    effOut --> actOut["Action a_t"]
    env1["Environment"] -.feedback.-> sensIn
    actOut -.-> env1
```

**Interpretation:** The **Simple Reflex Agent** directly maps percepts to actions via a *Condition-Action* rule table. It has no memory.

### 4.2 Model-Based Reflex Agent (Internal State)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensA["Sensors"] --> stateA["Percept p_t"]
    stateA --> updA["Update Internal State"]
    histA["How World Evolves"] --> updA
    obsA["How World is Observed"] --> updA
    updA --> ruleA["Condition-Action Rules"]
    ruleA --> actA["Action a_t"]
    actA --> effA["Effectors"]
    envA["Environment"] -.feedback.-> sensA
```

**Interpretation:** The model-based agent maintains an internal state that captures the *unobserved* aspects of the world, using two models: **(a)** the *transition model* (how the world evolves) and **(b)** the *sensor model* (how the world is observed).

### 4.3 Goal-Based Agent (Search/Planning Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sB["Sensors"] --> pB["Percept p_t"]
    pB --> sUp["State Update"]
    gB["Goal Information"] --> planner["Search and Planning"]
    sUp --> planner
    planner --> aB["Action a_t"]
    aB --> eB["Effectors"]
    envB["Environment"] -.feedback.-> sB
```

**Interpretation:** The goal-based agent uses a **goal description** that limits the actions considered by the planner. It looks into the future (planning) before acting.

### 4.4 Utility-Based Agent (Decision Under Uncertainty)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sC["Sensors"] --> pC["Percept p_t"]
    pC --> sUpC["State Update"]
    sUpC --> util["Utility Function U(s)"]
    utilC["Utility of States"] --> util
    util --> decC["Decision Maximizing Expected Utility"]
    decC --> aC["Action a_t"]
    aC --> eC["Effectors"]
    envC["Environment"] -.feedback.-> sC
```

**Interpretation:** When multiple goals conflict or the probability of success is uncertain, a **utility function** $U: S \to \mathbb{R}$ orders world states by desirability. The agent selects the action that maximizes the **expected utility**.

### 4.5 General Learning Agent (Top-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    perfE["Performance Element"] --> probG["Problem Generator"]
    learnE["Learning Element"] --> perfE
    criticE["Critic"] --> learnE
    sensE["Sensors"] --> perfE
    perfE --> actE["Actuators"]
    criticE -.feedback.-> actE
    problemGen["Problem Generator"] -.-> sensE
```

**Interpretation:** The **Learning Agent** is the most general architecture. It consists of four conceptual components:
- **Performance Element** — the existing agent program.
- **Critic** — feedback on how the agent is doing relative to a fixed performance standard.
- **Learning Element** — modifies the performance element to improve.
- **Problem Generator** — suggests exploratory actions leading to new informative experiences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `sensIn`, `cond`, `util`), and labels contain only clean uppercase/lowercase text without markdown tags, in strict compliance with the KTU Engine V10 protocol.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a rational agent. How is rationality different from omniscience? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A *rational agent* is one that, for every possible percept sequence, selects an action that is expected to maximize its **performance measure**, based on the evidence provided by the percept sequence and any built-in knowledge the agent possesses.

**Rationality vs. Omniscience:**
- An *omniscient* agent knows the **actual outcome** of its actions in the environment.
- A *rational* agent acts on the basis of the **expected outcome** given its percepts.
- In real-world robotics, omniscience is impossible due to partial observability, stochastic dynamics, and sensor noise. Hence, KTU designs mandate **rationality**, not omniscience.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse *rational* with *all-knowing*. Always state that rationality is about *expected* performance based on the percept sequence, not guaranteed outcomes.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any four properties used to classify task environments in AI. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four primary environment classification properties are:

1. **Fully Observable vs. Partially Observable** — Whether the agent's sensors give it access to the complete state of the environment at each point in time.
2. **Deterministic vs. Stochastic** — Whether the next state of the environment is completely determined by the current state and the action executed by the agent.
3. **Episodic vs. Sequential** — Whether the agent's experience can be divided into atomic, independent episodes (episodic) or whether earlier actions affect later decisions (sequential).
4. **Static vs. Dynamic** — Whether the environment can change while the agent is deliberating.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the **PEAS specification** for designing an intelligent agent. Use the example of a **medical diagnosis system** to illustrate your answer. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compare and contrast the four basic types of agent programs: **Simple Reflex, Model-Based Reflex, Goal-Based, and Utility-Based** agents. Use a unified diagram to justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q3A(a):

The **PEAS** acronym stands for **Performance Measure, Environment, Actuators, and Sensors**. It is the first formal step in designing any intelligent agent.

**For a Medical Diagnosis System:**

- **Performance Measure:** Correct diagnosis rate, patient recovery rate, minimization of diagnostic cost, patient comfort, and number of lives saved.
- **Environment:** Patients, hospital staff, medical records, pathology labs, time-varying symptoms, evolving disease progression.
- **Actuators:** Display screens (questions to patient), report printers, alert systems to doctors, prescription generation, database update mechanisms.
- **Sensors:** Keyboard input (patient history), touchscreen, microphone (voice symptoms), blood test reports, X-ray and MRI scanners, electronic health records (EHR).

**Justification:** The PEAS framework forces the designer to explicitly enumerate the goals, surroundings, capabilities, and observational channels *before* selecting an architecture. Without this specification, the agent's rationality cannot be evaluated.

#### Model Solution to Q3A(b):

| Agent Type | Internal State | Decision Basis | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Reflex** | None (stateless) | Current percept only via condition-action rules | Thermostat, basic obstacle avoidance |
| **Model-Based Reflex** | Maintains internal state of the world | Current percept + world model | Mobile robot in unknown terrain |
| **Goal-Based** | State + goal description | Future consequence of actions (planning) | Chess engine, path planning |
| **Utility-Based** | State + utility function | Maximizes expected utility | Autonomous trading, medical diagnosis |

**Unified Justification:**

- The **Simple Reflex agent** is computationally cheap but breaks in partially observable environments because two identical percepts may demand different actions.
- The **Model-Based Reflex agent** solves this by tracking how the world evolves and what is observable, enabling correct behavior in partially observable worlds.
- The **Goal-Based agent** extends the model-based agent by injecting a *goal* description; the agent plans a sequence of actions to reach the goal. This is essential for **sequential decision problems**.
- The **Utility-Based agent** is the most general; it uses a *utility function* to compare states when goals are insufficient (e.g., multiple conflicting goals, uncertainty in outcomes).

**Architectural Justification (Textual Diagram):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Simple Reflex} &\subset \text{Model-Based Reflex} \\
\text{Model-Based Reflex} &\subset \text{Goal-Based} \\
\text{Goal-Based} &\subset \text{Utility-Based} \\
\text{Utility-Based} &\subset \text{Learning Agent}
\end{aligned}
$$

The inclusion is strict; each subsequent architecture adds expressiveness at the cost of computational complexity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners explicitly look for the **internal state update equation** $I_{t+1} = Update(I_t, p_t)$ and the **action selection equation** $a_t = Action(I_t, p_t)$ in the answer. Omitting these equations typically costs **2 marks** out of 7.

---

**OR**

---

**Q3B.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Define the **agent function** and **agent program**. Show how an idealized rational agent's behavior can be specified as a **lookup table** for a $2 \times 2$ vacuum world. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the architecture of a **Learning Agent** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of each of its four components in detail. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q3B(a):

**Definitions:**

- **Agent Function:** The abstract mathematical mapping $f: P^* \rightarrow A$ from percept histories to actions.
- **Agent Program:** The concrete implementation of the agent function that runs on the physical architecture of the agent.

**Lookup Table for the 2x2 Vacuum World:**

The vacuum world has two cells, $A$ and $B$. The agent can perceive its location and the cleanliness of that cell. There are 4 possible percepts, and the action set is $\{Left, Right, Suck\}$.

| Percept Sequence | Action |
|---|---|
| $\langle (A, Clean) \rangle$ | Right |
| $\langle (A, Dirty) \rangle$ | Suck |
| $\langle (B, Clean) \rangle$ | Left |
| $\langle (B, Dirty) \rangle$ | Suck |

**Justification:** The rational agent must:
- *Suck* whenever the current square is dirty (immediate performance gain).
- *Move* to the other square when the current square is clean (to keep exploring and cleaning).

For percept sequences longer than 1 (e.g., after moving), the same table is queried on the **most recent** percept (simple reflex model) or on a derived internal state (model-based model).

#### Model Solution to Q3B(b):

A **Learning Agent** is the most general agent architecture. It can improve its performance over time by learning from its interactions with the environment. It has four conceptual components:

1. **Performance Element:** This is the existing agent program that selects actions given the current percept. It is equivalent to the entire agent in the architectures discussed previously.

2. **Critic:** This component takes as input the percepts from the environment and outputs a *feedback signal* indicating how well the agent is doing relative to a fixed performance standard. The critic is essential because the percepts themselves do not directly indicate the agent's success.

3. **Learning Element:** This component uses the feedback from the critic along with information about the agent's past actions to modify the **Performance Element** so that future actions lead to better performance.

4. **Problem Generator:** This component is responsible for suggesting **exploratory actions** that lead to new and informative experiences. By exploring, the agent can discover better strategies that pure exploitation would never reveal. This addresses the fundamental **exploration–exploitation trade-off**.

**Information Flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sensors} &\rightarrow \text{Performance Element} \rightarrow \text{Actuators} \\
\text{Environment} &\rightarrow \text{Critic} \rightarrow \text{Learning Element} \rightarrow \text{Performance Element} \\
\text{Performance Element} &\rightarrow \text{Problem Generator} \rightarrow \text{Performance Element}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often forget the **Problem Generator** in the diagram. This is the *distinguishing* component of a learning agent versus a regular agent. A diagram without the Problem Generator is marked incomplete.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- ✅ An **agent** is anything that perceives its environment via *sensors* and acts upon it via *actuators*.
- ✅ The **agent function** is $f: P^* \rightarrow A$, abstract and possibly non-implementable.
- ✅ The **agent program** is the concrete, executable realization of the agent function.
- ✅ **Rationality** = maximum expected performance; **Omniscience** = knowledge of actual outcomes (impossible in practice).
- ✅ The **PEAS** framework must always be the *first* design step.
- ✅ **Environment classification** is along 6 dimensions: observability, determinism, episodicity, static/dynamic, discreteness, and number of agents.
- ✅ The four basic agent programs, in increasing order of capability:
  1. **Simple Reflex** (no state, condition-action rules)
  2. **Model-Based Reflex** (internal state + world model)
  3. **Goal-Based** (goal description + planning)
  4. **Utility-Based** (utility function + decision-theoretic action selection)
- ✅ The **Learning Agent** augments any base agent with three additional components: **Critic, Learning Element, and Problem Generator**.
- ✅ The **utility function** $U: S \to \mathbb{R}$ orders states by desirability, and the agent maximizes **expected** utility (not actual utility).
- ✅ The **discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ governs the *horizon* of agent reasoning: low $\gamma$ is myopic, high $\gamma$ is far-sighted.
- ✅ **Always state** the update rule $I_{t+1} = Update(I_t, p_t)$ and action rule $a_t = Action(I_t, p_t)$ in 14-mark answers to demonstrate command over agent structure.
- ✅ **Memorize** the four components of a learning agent and their data flow — examiners frequently ask them as a 7-mark standalone question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Expert Systems - Basic Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Expert Systems - Basic Overview # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Expert System (ES)** is a branch of applied Artificial Intelligence (AI) that emulates the decision-making and problem-solving capabilities of a human expert in a narrow, well-defined domain of knowledge. It is a computer program that uses a curated **Knowledge Base** of domain facts and heuristics, combined with an **Inference Engine**, to derive intelligent conclusions and provide expert-level advice, diagnosis, or recommendations. In the context of **AI for Robotics** (PERUT521), an Expert System acts as the high-level "brain" or cognitive layer of a robotic agent, allowing it to reason about sensor inputs, plan actions, and make autonomous decisions in environments where hard-coded rules alone are insufficient but full machine learning is overkill. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Expert Systems are classified under *Knowledge-Based Systems* and are a foundational topic in Module 1. Students must understand their architecture, components, and inference mechanisms, as questions on these directly map to **CO1 (Apply AI techniques in robotic systems)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Specialist Doctor" Intuition Imagine a highly experienced doctor in a remote village clinic. Over **30 years**, she has memorized thousands of symptom–disease relationships. When a new patient arrives: 1. She **asks questions** (the **User Interface**). 2. She **recalls medical facts** from her memory (the **Knowledge Base**). 3. She **reasons** through the symptoms step by step to identify the disease (the **Inference Engine**). 4. She **explains** to the patient *why* she prescribed a certain medicine (the **Explanation Facility**). An Expert System is essentially a **digital clone** of this doctor. The "30 years of experience" is replaced by a curated database of rules given by human experts, and the "reasoning" is replaced by a software engine that applies logical rules to the facts. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** Unlike traditional programs that follow rigid algorithms (if A then B), Expert Systems handle *uncertainty*, use *heuristics* (rules of thumb), and can explain *how* they reached a conclusion. ## 1.3 Core Characteristics of Expert Systems | Characteristic | Description | |---|---| | **High Performance** | Operates at a human expert level in its narrow domain. | | **Domain Specificity** | Expertise is limited to a specific problem area (e.g., medical diagnosis, mineral prospecting). | | **Reasoning** | Uses symbolic logic (rules, facts) rather than numeric computation. | | **Explainability** | Can justify its reasoning path to the user. | | **Knowledge Separation** | Domain knowledge is kept *separate* from the inference mechanism. | | **Uncertainty Handling** | Deals with incomplete or probabilistic data using confidence factors. | ## 1.4 Why Expert Systems Matter in Robotics In robotics, Expert Systems bridge the gap between **low-level control** (motors, sensors) and **high-level cognition** (planning, decision-making). A mobile robot navigating a cluttered warehouse uses an expert system to interpret sensor data and decide *which* obstacle-avoidance strategy to deploy based on environmental context. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of an Expert System — The 5 Building Blocks An Expert System is not a single monolithic program. It is a collection of **interacting modules**, each with a specific responsibility. Understanding this architecture is the single most important topic for KTU exams on this subject. ### Block 1: Knowledge Base (KB) - The **heart** of the expert system. - Contains domain-specific knowledge in two forms: - **Facts:** Objective truths (e.g., "A temperature above 100°C causes water to boil"). - **Heuristics / Rules:** Subjective rules of thumb from human experts, typically expressed as **IF-THEN** (production) rules. - Example rule: `IF (robot_battery < 20%) AND (task_priority = HIGH) THEN (return_to_dock = TRUE)` ### Block 2: Inference Engine (IE) - The **"brain" or processor** of the ES. - Matches the current facts in the **Working Memory** against the rules in the Knowledge Base. - Applies logical reasoning strategies: - **Forward Chaining** (Data-Driven): Starts from known facts and fires rules to derive new facts until a goal is reached. Used in *monitoring and control* (e.g., a robot monitoring factory floor sensors). - **Backward Chaining** (Goal-Driven): Starts from a hypothesis/goal and works backward to find supporting facts. Used in *diagnosis* (e.g., "Why is the robot arm vibrating?"). ### Block 3: Working Memory (Database) - A **dynamic, short-term memory** that holds the current problem state — i.e., the facts known about the current session. - Updated continuously as the inference engine fires rules. ### Block 4: User Interface - The **communication bridge** between the human user and the system. - Accepts queries, presents questions, and displays conclusions in a natural, readable format. ### Block 5: Explanation Facility - A unique and powerful module that answers **"WHY?"** and **"HOW?"** questions. - "WHY did you ask this question?" → traces the rule currently being evaluated. - "HOW did you reach this conclusion?" → lists the chain of rules that fired. - This transparency is what makes Expert Systems trustworthy in critical applications like medical robotics. ## 2.2 Knowledge Representation in Expert Systems Knowledge in the KB is encoded using formal representation schemes. The most common is the **Production Rule** system: $$\text{IF } \langle \text{antecedent/condition} \rangle \text{ THEN } \langle \text{consequent/action} \rangle$$ Each rule also carries an optional **Certainty Factor (CF)** to handle uncertainty: $$CF = MB - MD$$ Where: - $MB$ = Measure of Belief (how strongly the evidence supports the hypothesis) - $MD$ = Measure of Disbelief (how strongly the evidence refutes the hypothesis) - $CF \in [-1, +1]$, where $+1$ means absolute certainty and $-1$ means absolute disbelief. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Certainty Factors (CF) were introduced in the **MYCIN** medical expert system. The formula above is the original *Shortliffe & Buchanan (1975)* formulation and is frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Element | Formula / Rule | Application / Unit | |---|---|---| | Production Rule | $\text{IF } A \text{ THEN } B$ | Knowledge Base encoding | | Certainty Factor (CF) | $CF = MB - MD$ | Uncertainty handling, range $[-1, +1]$ | | CF Combination (conjunction) | $CF(A \text{ AND } B) = \min(CF(A), CF(B))$ | Combining two evidence sources | | CF Combination (disjunction) | $CF(A \text{ OR } B) = \max(CF(A), CF(B))$ | Alternative evidence paths | | CF Propagation Rule | $CF(B) = CF(A) \times CF(\text{rule})$ | Forward chaining uncertainty update | | Forward Chaining Trigger | New fact added to WM | Data-driven reasoning | | Backward Chaining Trigger | Goal hypothesis set | Goal-driven reasoning | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In autonomous robotics, these CF formulas allow a robot to weigh conflicting sensor inputs. For example, if a vision system says "obstacle ahead, CF = 0.9" but an ultrasonic sensor says "clear path, CF = 0.6," the robot can logically fuse the two into a single, defensible decision. ## 2.4 The Two Inference Strategies — Operational Flow **Forward Chaining (Data-Driven):** 1. Start with known facts in Working Memory. 2. Scan all rules in the KB. 3. Fire any rule whose conditions are satisfied. 4. Add the rule's conclusion as a new fact to WM. 5. Repeat steps 2–4 until no more rules can fire OR the goal is reached. **Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven):** 1. Start with a hypothesis (goal) to prove. 2. Find rules whose conclusion matches the goal. 3. Treat the rule's conditions as new sub-goals. 4. Recursively try to prove each sub-goal using known facts. 5. If all sub-goals are satisfied, the hypothesis is confirmed. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for KTU:** "**For**ward = **F**acts first" (data-driven). "**Back**ward = **B**egin with goal" (goal-driven). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Certainty Factor Propagation This is the most common numerical problem in KTU exams on Expert Systems. Let us work through it completely. **Problem Setup:** A robot diagnostic expert system has the following rule chain: $$\text{Rule 1: } \text{IF} \; (\text{voltage} < 5V) \; \text{THEN} \; (\text{power_fault}) \quad [CF_{\text{rule1}} = 0.8]$$ $$\text{Rule 2: } \text{IF} \; (\text{power_fault}) \; \text{THEN} \; (\text{robot_failure}) \quad [CF_{\text{rule2}} = 0.9]$$ A sensor reports `voltage = 4V` with an initial certainty $CF(\text{voltage} < 5V) = 0.7$. Calculate the final certainty that there is a **robot failure**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Identify the initial fact certainty.** The sensor provides the antecedent with a certainty of $0.7$. This is the *Measure of Belief* in the condition. $$CF(\text{antecedent}) = CF(\text{voltage} < 5V) = 0.7$$ **Step 2: Apply the CF propagation formula for Rule 1.** When a rule fires, the certainty of its conclusion is the product of the antecedent's certainty and the rule's own certainty factor. $$\begin{aligned} CF(\text{power\_fault}) &= CF(\text{antecedent}) \times CF_{\text{rule1}} \\ &= 0.7 \times 0.8 \\ &= 0.56 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Use the derived fact as the new antecedent for Rule 2.** The newly established fact `power_fault` becomes the input to Rule 2. $$CF(\text{antecedent for Rule 2}) = CF(\text{power\_fault}) = 0.56$$ **Step 4: Apply the CF propagation formula for Rule 2.** $$\begin{aligned} CF(\text{robot\_failure}) &= CF(\text{power\_fault}) \times CF_{\text{rule2}} \\ &= 0.56 \times 0.9 \\ &= 0.504 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Interpret the final result.** The expert system concludes there is a **robot failure** with a certainty of **0.504** (approximately 50.4%). The system can present this as a probabilistic recommendation to the human operator. > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The final certainty (0.504) is lower than the initial sensor certainty (0.7) because uncertainty compounds as the inference chain grows. This is a fundamental limitation of rule-based reasoning and a popular KTU exam question. ## 3.2 Combined Evidence — Conjunction and Disjunction **Scenario:** Two independent sensors provide evidence about a robot's wheel slip: - Sensor A: $CF(\text{slip}) = 0.6$ - Sensor B: $CF(\text{slip}) = 0.8$ **Case 1: Both sensors must agree (AND / Conjunction):** We take the *minimum*, because the weakest link governs. $$\begin{aligned} CF(\text{slip, combined}) &= \min(CF_A, CF_B) \\ &= \min(0.6, 0.8) \\ &= 0.6 \end{aligned}$$ **Case 2: Either sensor triggering is enough (OR / Disjunction):** We take the *maximum*, because the strongest evidence dominates. $$\begin{aligned} CF(\text{slip, combined}) &= \max(CF_A, CF_B) \\ &= \max(0.6, 0.8) \\ &= 0.8 \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Inference Strategy | Problem Type | Recommended Strategy | Robotic Example | |---|---|---| | Monitoring / Real-time data streams | **Forward Chaining** | Continuously reading factory sensors to detect anomalies | | Diagnosis / Fault isolation | **Backward Chaining** | "Why did the conveyor belt stop? Test each hypothesis." | | Planning / Configuration | **Forward Chaining** | Assembling a sequence of robot actions from initial state | | Hypothesis verification | **Backward Chaining** | "Is this obstacle a human? Check thermal, motion, shape data." | | Small rule base (< 100 rules) | **Backward Chaining** | Efficient, focused search | | Large rule base (> 100 rules) | **Forward Chaining** | Avoids combinatorial explosion of sub-goals | ## 3.4 Historical & Classical Expert Systems — KTU Reference Table | System | Domain | Year | Key Innovation | |---|---|---|---| | **DENDRAL** | Chemistry (molecular structure) | 1965 | First successful ES; used by chemists to identify unknown compounds | | **MYCIN** | Medical (bacterial infection) | 1972 | Introduced Certainty Factors and backward chaining for diagnosis | | **XCON/R1** | Computer configuration | 1980 | First commercial ES; saved DEC millions in VAX system sales | | **PROSPECTOR** | Geology (mineral exploration) | 1974 | Combined ES with Bayesian probability for mining assessments | | **CLIPS** | General-purpose shell | 1985 | NASA-developed ES shell, widely used in robotics research | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an Expert System The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the five core components of an Expert System interact during a typical consultation session (e.g., a robot diagnostic check). ```mermaid flowchart TD userA[Human User] -->|Queries / Sensor Data| uiA[User Interface] uiA -->|Parsed Input| wmA[Working Memory] wmA -->|Current Facts| ieA[Inference Engine] kbA[Knowledge Base
