# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define a robot as per the Robot Institute of America (RIA). List any two distinguishing features.

**Model Answer:**
According to the **Robot Institute of America (RIA)**, a robot is *"a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks."*

Two distinguishing features:
1. **Reprogrammability:** The task can be altered by changing software, not hardware.
2. **Multifunctionality:** A single robot can perform several different operations (welding, painting, assembly) by changing the end-effector or program.

*(3 marks – 1 for definition, 1 each for the two features)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Differentiate between a **Revolute joint** and a **Prismatic joint**. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Revolute Joint (R) | Prismatic Joint (P) |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Type of motion | Rotation about a fixed axis | Linear translation along an axis |
| Joint variable | Angle $\theta$ | Displacement $d$ |
| Real-world example | Human elbow / robot shoulder | Hydraulic cylinder / gantry slide |

*(3 marks – clear distinction earns full marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Industrial Robot Architecture Focus*

> **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model Paper Style]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **five major subsystems of an industrial robot** and explain the function of each. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Classify robots by coordinate system. Explain any **three** configurations in detail with neat sketches. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

The five major subsystems of an industrial robot are:

1. **Manipulator / Mechanical Structure:** The "body" of the robot, consisting of rigid links connected by joints (revolute or prismatic). It provides the physical reach and dexterity. Example: A 6R articulated arm with aluminum/steel links.

2. **Actuators:** Convert stored energy into mechanical motion. Types include:
    - **Electric motors** (DC, stepper, servo) – most common in modern robots.
    - **Hydraulic actuators** – high payload, used in heavy industry.
    - **Pneumatic actuators** – simple, low-cost, used for pick-and-place.

3. **Sensors:** Provide feedback about internal state and external environment.
    - **Internal (Proprioceptive):** Encoders, resolvers, tachometers.
    - **External (Exteroceptive):** Cameras, LIDAR, force/torque sensors.

4. **Controller:** The "brain" of the robot. Houses the CPU, memory, and AI software. Executes the user program, performs trajectory interpolation, closes the feedback loop, and in modern systems runs **AI algorithms (SLAM, RL, CNN perception)**.

5. **Power Supply:** Provides regulated energy — typically 230 V AC mains for industrial robots, 48 V DC for collaborative robots, and Li-ion batteries for mobile platforms.

*(Award 7 marks: 1 for block diagram + 1.2 per subsystem with examples.)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

Classification of robots by coordinate system (workspace geometry):

1. **Cartesian (PPP) Robot:** Three prismatic joints along orthogonal $x$, $y$, $z$ axes. Workspace is a rectangular cuboid. *Application:* CNC machines, 3D printers, gantry loaders. *Advantage:* Simple kinematics, high accuracy. *Disadvantage:* Large footprint, poor dexterity.

2. **Cylindrical (RPP) Robot:** One revolute joint at the base + two prismatic joints. Workspace is a hollow cylinder. *Application:* Machine loading/unloading, spot welding. *Advantage:* Compact footprint, good reach. *Disadvantage:* Limited to cylindrical work envelope.

3. **Articulated (RRR) Robot:** Three or more revolute joints arranged like a human arm (waist, shoulder, elbow). Workspace is an irregular sphere. *Application:* Automotive welding, painting, sealing. *Advantage:* Maximum dexterity, full 3D reach. *Disadvantage:* Complex kinematics, requires advanced controllers.

*(7 marks: 3 each for any three configurations + 1 for overall classification table.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Degrees of Freedom & Kinematics Focus*

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** in robotics. State the Kutzbach criterion and apply it to verify a **6R industrial manipulator** with 7 links. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Define **workspace**. Distinguish between **reachable workspace** and **dexterous workspace**. Derive the workspace volume of a Cartesian robot with $L_x = 2$ m, $L_y = 1.5$ m, $L_z = 1$ m. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

**Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** is defined as the number of independent motions (translational or rotational) a robot can perform. A rigid body in 3D space possesses a maximum of **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations).

**Kutzbach–Grübler Criterion (Mobility Equation):**

$$
M = 6(n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

**Application to 6R Manipulator:**
- Number of links: $n = 7$ (1 base + 6 moving links)
- Number of joints: $j = 6$ (all revolute)
- Each joint DOF: $f_i = 1$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(7 - 1 - 6) + (1 \times 6) \\
&= 6(0) + 6 \\
M &= 6 \;\text{DOF} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the 6R manipulator achieves full spatial dexterity.

*(7 marks: 2 for DOF concept, 2 for criterion, 3 for numerical verification.)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

**Workspace ($W$)** is the total volume of space that the end-effector of a robot can reach.

- **Reachable Workspace:** The set of all points the end-effector can reach with **at least one orientation**.
- **Dexterous Workspace:** The subset of reachable points where the end-effector can achieve **all possible orientations**.

**Derivation of Cartesian Workspace Volume:**

The three prismatic joints of a Cartesian robot move independently along orthogonal axes. The total swept volume is a **rectangular cuboid**.

$$
V = L_x \times L_y \times L_z
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 2 \times 1.5 \times 1 \\
V &= 3 \;\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The robot's end-effector can reach any point within a **3 m³ cuboid** workspace. Since a Cartesian robot maintains a fixed end-effector orientation, its dexterous workspace coincides exactly with its reachable workspace.

*(7 marks: 1 for definitions, 3 for distinction, 3 for derivation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing accuracy with repeatability** – *Accuracy* = closeness to a *commanded* point; *Repeatability* = closeness to a *previously taught* point. Always state both when defining performance.
> 2. **Misapplying Kutzbach** – Use $6(n-1-j)$ for spatial (3D) and $3(n-1-j)$ for planar (2D). Forgetting the $-1$ (ground link) is a 1-mark error.
> 3. **Workspace confusion** – Dexterous ⊂ Reachable. The dexterous workspace can never be *larger* than the reachable one.
> 4. **Unit errors** – Reach is in meters (m), payload in kg, resolution in rad/°/arc-sec — never mix up.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition (RIA, 1979):** A reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator. *(High-frequency one-liner for KTU.)*
- **ISO 8373:** Defines robot as having **3 or more programmable axes**.
- **Two joint types only:** **Revolute (R)** = rotation; **Prismatic (P)** = translation.
- **DOF of a rigid body in 3D = 6** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- **Kutzbach criterion (spatial):** $M = 6(n-1-j) + \sum f_i$.
- **6R manipulator** = 7 links, 6 joints → 6 DOF.
- **Coordinate systems:** Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), Articulated (RRR), SCARA (RRP), Parallel (Delta).
- **Workspace:** *Reachable* = ≥1 orientation; *Dexterous* = all orientations. Dexterous ⊂ Reachable.
- **Cartesian workspace volume:** $V = L_x L_y L_z$ (cuboid).
- **Spherical workspace volume:** $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi(r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3)$ (hemispherical shell).
- **Encoder resolution:** $\Delta\theta = 2\pi/2^N$ (for revolute joints).
- **Five robot subsystems:** Manipulator, Actuators, Sensors, Controller, Power Supply.
- **AI integration points:** Perception (CNN), Planning (A*/RRT), Decision (RL), SLAM.
- **Asimov's Laws:** Three original + one Zeroth Law. Often a 2-mark short note question.
- **Sense → Plan → Act** is the classical AI-robotics paradigm. Modern systems add **Learning** → *Sense → Plan → Act → Learn → Loop back*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervised Learning Algorithms - Decision Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supervised Learning Algorithms - Decision Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Tree (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *Decision Tree* is a non-parametric, **supervised learning algorithm** used for both **classification** (Decision Tree Classifier) and **regression** (Decision Tree Regressor) tasks. It models decisions and their possible consequences as a recursive, tree-structured hierarchy where:
> - **Internal Nodes** represent a *test* on a feature attribute.
> - **Branches** represent the *outcome* of that test.
> - **Leaf Nodes (Terminal Nodes)** represent a *class label* (classification) or a *continuous value* (regression).
> - **Root Node** is the topmost node representing the *best predictor* (highest Information Gain or lowest Gini Index).

From the perspective of **Information Theory**, a decision tree is a greedy, top-down recursive partitioning scheme that splits the feature space $\mathcal{X}$ into mutually exclusive, exhaustive rectangular regions $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_K$ such that the *impurity* within each region is minimized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "20 Questions" Analogy:** Imagine you are a doctor diagnosing a patient. You do not ask all 100 questions at once. Instead, you ask **one question at a time** — the question whose answer most *reduces your uncertainty*. "Does the patient have a fever?" splits the patient pool faster than "What is the patient's eye color?". Each question *branches* the population into smaller, more homogeneous groups, until you reach a diagnosis (a leaf). A **Decision Tree** is precisely this — an automated, mathematically optimized game of *20 Questions* learned from labeled training data.

Another everyday analogy: think of the **"Guess Who?"** board game. Each yes/no question you ask is a *split*, and the final character you guess is the *class label*.

## 1.3 Why Decision Trees are Critical in Robotics

In the context of **PERUT521 (AI for Robotics)**, decision trees serve as lightweight, interpretable **behavior arbitration policies** — for instance:
- A mobile robot deciding *turn-left / move-forward / stop* based on LiDAR sensor thresholds.
- A manipulator choosing *grasp / reject* based on object weight and shape.
- A drone selecting flight mode based on wind speed, battery, and altitude.

Their **interpretability**, **fast inference** ($O(\text{depth})$), and **no need for feature scaling** make them ideal for embedded robotic platforms.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Root Node** | Topmost node; the entire dataset $S$ resides here initially. |
| **Internal Node** | A decision node with both incoming edge(s) and outgoing edges. |
| **Leaf Node** | Terminal node carrying the final prediction. |
| **Splitting** | Partitioning a node into two or more child nodes. |
| **Pruning** | Removing subtrees to combat overfitting. |
| **Impurity** | Measure of disorder/heterogeneity at a node. |
| **Entropy** | Information-theoretic impurity measure $H(S)$. |
| **Gini Index** | Probabilistic impurity measure $G(S)$. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition: How Does the ID3 Algorithm Work?

The classical **ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** algorithm, proposed by *Ross Quinlan (1986)*, builds the tree in a **greedy, top-down recursive** manner. Each step performs:

1. **Compute the impurity** of the current node $S$ using Entropy or Gini.
2. **For every feature** $A \in \mathcal{A}$, evaluate the *quality of the split* by computing the weighted average impurity of the resulting child subsets.
3. **Select the feature with the maximum Information Gain** (or minimum weighted impurity).
4. **Recurse** on each child subset using the remaining features.
5. **Stop** when:
   - All samples in a node belong to one class (pure node), **OR**
   - No remaining features provide positive Information Gain, **OR**
   - The node has fewer samples than a predefined threshold $\min\_samples\_leaf$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Greedy Selection:** At each node we choose the split that *locally* maximizes purity reduction. This is a heuristic — it does *not* guarantee a globally optimal tree, but it is computationally tractable (finding the globally optimal decision tree is **NP-complete**).

## 2.2 The Three Classical Algorithms

| Algorithm | Splitting Criterion | Year | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ID3** | Information Gain | 1986 | Categorical features only; multi-way splits. |
| **C4.5** | Gain Ratio | 1993 | Handles continuous attributes, missing values, pruning. |
| **CART** | Gini Index | 1984 | Binary splits only; foundation of Random Forests. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these four formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU exam question on Decision Trees.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \, \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = proportion of class $i$ in $S$; $c$ = number of classes. $H=0$ means pure; $H=1$ means maximum disorder (binary). |
| 2 | **Conditional Entropy** | $H(S \mid A) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \, H(S_v)$ | Weighted average entropy of subsets after splitting on $A$. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for set cardinality. |
| 3 | **Information Gain (ID3)** | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | Higher is better. **Biased toward many-valued features.** |
| 4 | **Gain Ratio (C4.5)** | $GR(S, A) = \dfrac{IG(S, A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S, A)}$ | $\text{SplitInfo}(S,A) = -\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$. Corrects ID3's bias. |
| 5 | **Gini Index (CART)** | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$ | Ranges $[0, 1-\tfrac{1}{c}]$. Computationally cheaper (no $\log$). |
| 6 | **Gini Impurity of Split** | $\text{Gini}_{\text{split}}(S, A) = \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \text{Gini}(S_v)$ | Weighted Gini after split. |

## 2.4 Handling Continuous-Valued Features (C4.5 / CART Trick)

For a continuous attribute $A$ with values $\{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n\}$, the algorithm:
1. Sorts the values in ascending order.
2. Considers every midpoint $t_k = \tfrac{a_k + a_{k+1}}{2}$ as a candidate threshold.
3. Performs a binary test "$A \leq t_k$ ?" and evaluates Information Gain.
4. Selects the threshold $t^*$ with maximum IG.

This converts a continuous attribute into a discrete binary split, increasing the search space to $O(n \log n)$ per attribute.

## 2.5 Overfitting and the Role of Pruning

A tree grown to full depth can perfectly classify the training set but fail on unseen data — a classic case of **high variance / overfitting**. Two remedial strategies:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Pruning (Early Stopping):** Halt tree growth when:
> - Information Gain falls below a threshold $\epsilon$.
> - Node contains fewer than $\min\_samples\_split$ samples.
> - Tree depth exceeds $\max\_depth$.
>
> **Post-Pruning (Reduced Error Pruning, Cost-Complexity Pruning):** Grow the full tree, then *replace* a subtree with a leaf if the validation error decreases. The CART algorithm uses a regularization parameter $\alpha$ to penalize tree size:
> $$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \, \vert T \vert$$
> where $R(T)$ is the misclassification rate and $\vert T \vert$ is the number of leaf nodes.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **autonomous robotics**, decision trees power:
- **Behavior trees** in ROS (Robot Operating System) for mission planning.
- **Obstacle avoidance classifiers** distinguishing traversable vs. non-traversable terrain from LiDAR point clouds.
- **Fault diagnosis systems** in industrial manipulators, where sensor readings are mapped to fault codes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example: The "Play Tennis" Dataset

To make every algebraic transition explicit, we work through Quinlan's classical *Play Tennis* dataset (14 instances). The goal is to predict whether to play tennis (`Yes` / `No`) given weather conditions.

**Dataset Summary:**

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play? |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|-------|
| 1–5 (counts) | Sunny: 5 | Hot: 4 | High: 7 | Weak: 8 | **Yes: 9** |
| | Overcast: 4 | Mild: 6 | Normal: 7 | Strong: 6 | **No: 5** |
| | Rain: 5 | Cool: 4 | | | |

Total samples: $\vert S \vert = 14$; Positive class ($p = \text{Yes}$) = $9/14$; Negative class ($n = \text{No}$) = $5/14$.

---

### Step 1: Compute Entropy of the Root Node $S$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i \\
&= -\left[\, p_{\text{yes}} \log_2 p_{\text{yes}} \;+\; p_{\text{no}} \log_2 p_{\text{no}} \,\right] \\
&= -\left[\, \frac{9}{14} \log_2 \frac{9}{14} \;+\; \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \frac{5}{14} \,\right] \\
&= -\left[\, (0.6429)(-0.6374) \;+\; (0.3571)(-1.4854) \,\right] \\
&= -[\, -0.4098 \;+\; -0.5305 \,] \\
&= -[\, -0.9403 \,] \\
&= 0.940 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The root dataset has an impurity of **0.940 bits** out of a maximum of 1 bit (binary), indicating substantial disorder.

---

### Step 2: Compute Information Gain for Each Feature

We repeat the procedure for all four features. To avoid repetition, we showcase the *Outlook* calculation in full and tabulate the rest.

#### 2(a) Information Gain for **Outlook**

The attribute *Outlook* has three values: Sunny (5), Overcast (4), Rain (5).

**Subset $S_{\text{Sunny}}$:** 2 Yes, 3 No.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) &= -\left[\, \frac{2}{5} \log_2 \frac{2}{5} \;+\; \frac{3}{5} \log_2 \frac{3}{5} \,\right] \\
&= -[\, (0.4)(-1.3219) + (0.6)(-0.7370) \,] \\
&= -[\, -0.5288 - 0.4422 \,] \\
&= 0.971 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Subset $S_{\text{Overcast}}$:** 4 Yes, 0 No → **Pure node.**

$$
H(S_{\text{Overcast}}) = -\left[\, \frac{4}{4} \log_2 \frac{4}{4} \;+\; \frac{0}{4} \log_2 \frac{0}{4} \,\right] = 0 \text{ bits}
$$

**Subset $S_{\text{Rain}}$:** 3 Yes, 2 No (symmetric to Sunny).

$$
H(S_{\text{Rain}}) = H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = 0.971 \text{ bits}
$$

**Conditional Entropy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) &= \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v) \\
&= \frac{5}{14}(0.971) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.971) \\
&= 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 \\
&= 0.694 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Information Gain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IG(S, \text{Outlook}) &= H(S) - H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) \\
&= 0.940 - 0.694 \\
&= 0.246 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### 2(b) Compact Calculations for Remaining Features

For brevity, all remaining intermediate entropies are tabulated:

| Feature | Subset $v$ | $\vert S_v \vert$ | Class Split (Y, N) | $H(S_v)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Hot | 4 | 2, 2 | 1.000 |
| | Mild | 6 | 4, 2 | 0.918 |
| | Cool | 4 | 3, 1 | 0.811 |
| **Humidity** | High | 7 | 3, 4 | 0.985 |
| | Normal | 7 | 6, 1 | 0.592 |
| **Wind** | Weak | 8 | 6, 2 | 0.811 |
| | Strong | 6 | 3, 3 | 1.000 |

**Conditional Entropy and Information Gain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Temp}) &= \tfrac{4}{14}(1.000) + \tfrac{6}{14}(0.918) + \tfrac{4}{14}(0.811) \\
&= 0.286 + 0.394 + 0.232 = 0.911 \text{ bits} \\
IG(S, \text{Temp}) &= 0.940 - 0.911 = 0.029 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Humidity}) &= \tfrac{7}{14}(0.985) + \tfrac{7}{14}(0.592) \\
&= 0.493 + 0.296 = 0.789 \text{ bits} \\
IG(S, \text{Humidity}) &= 0.940 - 0.789 = 0.151 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Wind}) &= \tfrac{8}{14}(0.811) + \tfrac{6}{14}(1.000) \\
&= 0.463 + 0.429 = 0.892 \text{ bits} \\
IG(S, \text{Wind}) &= 0.940 - 0.892 = 0.048 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3: Rank Features by Information Gain (Decision at Root)

| Rank | Feature | $IG(S, A)$ | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Outlook** | **0.246** | ✅ Selected as Root |
| 2 | Humidity | 0.151 | — |
| 3 | Wind | 0.048 | — |
| 4 | Temperature | 0.029 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Outcome:** *Outlook* is selected as the root split. The branch *Outlook = Overcast* becomes a pure leaf (Play = Yes). The remaining branches (*Sunny*, *Rain*) are recursed upon with the remaining features — for instance, the *Sunny* subset splits further on *Humidity*.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Criteria

| Property | Information Gain (ID3) | Gain Ratio (C4.5) | Gini Index (CART) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | $\dfrac{IG}{\text{SplitInfo}}$ | $1 - \sum p_i^2$ |
| **Range** | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ |
| **Bias** | Favors many-valued features | Corrects bias via normalization | Favors balanced splits |
| **Computation** | Requires $\log$ calls | Requires $\log$ calls | Cheaper (no $\log$) |
| **Speed** | Slower | Slower | Faster |
| **Tree type** | Multi-way | Multi-way | Binary |

---

## 3.3 Variance / Bias Decomposition in Decision Trees

The expected prediction error can be decomposed as:

$$
E[\text{Error}] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}
$$

| Tree Depth | Bias | Variance | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shallow (Underfit) | High | Low | Underfitting |
| Full depth (No pruning) | Low | High | Overfitting |
| Pruned (Optimal) | Moderate | Moderate | Best Generalization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Start with a *deep* tree and apply **post-pruning** using cross-validation. This empirically outperforms pre-pruning in most KTU lab experiments.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Induced Decision Tree for "Play Tennis"

The following Mermaid block visualizes the tree induced from our analytical derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Decision Tree. List any two advantages and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Decision Tree* is a supervised, non-parametric machine learning model that recursively partitions the feature space into rectangular decision regions, represented as a tree of decision (internal) and outcome (leaf) nodes. The model is learned by greedy maximization of Information Gain (ID3) or minimization of Gini impurity (CART).

**Advantages:**
1. Highly **interpretable** — the induced rules can be expressed as IF-THEN statements.
2. Requires **no feature scaling** (normalization/standardization).
3. Handles both **numerical and categorical** data.

**Disadvantage:**
1. Prone to **overfitting** if not pruned; small data perturbations can yield very different trees (high variance).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Entropy and Gini Index as splitting criteria.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Entropy | Gini Index |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $-\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $1 - \sum p_i^2$ |
| Computational Cost | Requires $\log_2$ operation | Cheaper (squared terms only) |
| Range (binary) | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, 0.5]$ |
| Curve Shape | Logarithmic | Parabolic |
| Used By | ID3, C4.5 | CART |

Both measure node impurity and yield similar splits in practice; Gini is preferred in large datasets due to its computational efficiency.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** Common mistakes costing 2–3 marks:
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in entropy — entropy is always $\geq 0$.
> 2. **Confusing conditional entropy notation** $H(S \mid A)$ with joint entropy $H(S, A)$.
> 3. **Skipping the units** ("bits") in entropy answers — KTU awards half-marks for correct units.
> 4. **Not recursing** the algorithm — students often compute IG only at the root and stop.
> 5. **Writing $p \log p$ instead of $-p \log p$** in entropy — a classic one-mark deduction.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2]`
**With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Decision Tree. Compute the Information Gain for splitting the following dataset on the attribute "Weather":**
- *Sunny → Play = {Yes, No, Yes, No, No}*
- *Cloudy → Play = {Yes, Yes, Yes, Yes}*
- *Rainy → Play = {No, Yes, No}*

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (already covered in SECTION 4) — describe:** Root node → branches for each attribute value → recursive subtrees → leaf nodes.

**Computation:**

Total: 12 samples. Yes = 7, No = 5.

$$
H(S) = -\left[\tfrac{7}{12}\log_2\tfrac{7}{12} + \tfrac{5}{12}\log_2\tfrac{5}{12}\right] = 0.980 \text{ bits}
$$

For *Sunny* (5 samples: 2 Yes, 3 No):
$$
H(\text{Sunny}) = -\left[\tfrac{2}{5}\log_2\tfrac{2}{5} + \tfrac{3}{5}\log_2\tfrac{3}{5}\right] = 0.971 \text{ bits}
$$

For *Cloudy* (4 samples: 4 Yes, 0 No) — pure:
$$
H(\text{Cloudy}) = 0 \text{ bits}
$$

For *Rainy* (3 samples: 1 Yes, 2 No):
$$
H(\text{Rainy}) = -\left[\tfrac{1}{3}\log_2\tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{2}{3}\log_2\tfrac{2}{3}\right] = 0.918 \text{ bits}
$$

Conditional Entropy:
$$
H(S \mid \text{Weather}) = \tfrac{5}{12}(0.971) + \tfrac{4}{12}(0) + \tfrac{3}{12}(0.918) = 0.404 + 0 + 0.230 = 0.634 \text{ bits}
$$

Information Gain:
$$
\boxed{IG(S, \text{Weather}) = 0.980 - 0.634 = 0.346 \text{ bits}}
$$

#### Part (b) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Explain overfitting in Decision Trees. Compare Pre-Pruning and Post-Pruning with suitable examples.**

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Overfitting:** A tree grown to full depth perfectly memorizes the training set (training error $\approx 0$) but exhibits poor test accuracy. This happens because the algorithm greedily splits on noise patterns.

**Pre-Pruning (Early Stopping):** Halts growth before perfect classification. Examples:
- $\max\_depth \leq 5$
- $\min\_samples\_split \geq 10$
- $IG < \epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 0.001$)

**Post-Pruning (Reduced-Error / Cost-Complexity Pruning):** Grows the full tree first, then replaces subtrees with leaves if validation accuracy improves. CART uses:
$$
R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert
$$

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Pre-Pruning | Post-Pruning |
|---|---|---|
| When applied | During construction | After full tree is built |
| Speed | Faster (avoids extra splits) | Slower |
| Risk | Underfitting (premature stop) | Safer (sees full structure) |
| KTU Recommendation | Use only with clear thresholds | **Preferred** in academic projects |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2]`
**Derive the formula for Gini Index. Compute the Gini impurity for the following binary split and comment on its quality:**
- *Node $P$: 30 positives, 70 negatives*
- *Node $Q$: 60 positives, 40 negatives*

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** For a node with class probabilities $p_i$, the Gini impurity measures the probability of misclassification if we randomly label according to class distribution:

$$
\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2
$$

For two-class case with $p$ and $(1-p)$:
$$
\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - [p^2 + (1-p)^2] = 2p(1-p)
$$

**Computation for Node $P$ (30 Pos, 70 Neg, $n = 100$):**
$$
p_+ = 0.3, \quad p_- = 0.7
$$
$$
\text{Gini}(P) = 1 - [(0.3)^2 + (0.7)^2] = 1 - [0.09 + 0.49] = 0.42
$$

**Computation for Node $Q$ (60 Pos, 40 Neg, $n = 100$):**
$$
p_+ = 0.6, \quad p_- = 0.4
$$
$$
\text{Gini}(Q) = 1 - [(0.6)^2 + (0.4)^2] = 1 - [0.36 + 0.16] = 0.48
$$

**Weighted Gini of split:**
$$
\text{Gini}_{\text{split}} = \tfrac{100}{200}(0.42) + \tfrac{100}{200}(0.48) = 0.45
$$

**Comment:** The split has Gini $= 0.45$ out of a parent Gini. Compare with parent node (90 Pos, 110 Neg, $n = 200$):
$$
\text{Gini}_{\text{parent}} = 1 - [(0.45)^2 + (0.55)^2] = 1 - 0.505 = 0.495
$$
Reduction $= 0.495 - 0.45 = 0.045$. Modest improvement — the split is **acceptable but not highly informative**.

#### Part (b) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Explain the C4.5 algorithm. How does it improve upon ID3? Discuss its handling of continuous attributes and missing values.**

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**C4.5 Algorithm (Quinlan, 1993):** Successor to ID3, addressing three key limitations:

| Limitation in ID3 | C4.5 Solution |
|---|---|
| Biased toward features with many values | Uses **Gain Ratio** instead of raw IG |
| Cannot handle continuous attributes | **Discretization** via binary threshold search |
| Cannot handle missing values | **Probabilistic splitting** — distribute fractional samples across all branches |

**Gain Ratio:**
$$
GR(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S, A)}
$$
where $\text{SplitInfo}(S, A) = -\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ is the intrinsic information of the split.

**Handling Continuous Attributes:**
For each continuous feature $A$, sort values and consider each midpoint $t$ as a candidate binary split "$A \leq t$?". The threshold with maximum Gain Ratio is selected. This expands the hypothesis space to include axis-aligned thresholds.

**Handling Missing Values:**
- During training, instances with missing $A$ are *fractionally* distributed: a sample with weight $w$ is split into weights $w \cdot p_v$ assigned to each branch $v$ of $A$.
- During inference, missing $A$ is handled by traversing *all* branches and aggregating weighted predictions.

**Pruning in C4.5:** Uses *error-based post-pruning* with confidence intervals (pessimistic error estimation), rather than a separate validation set.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2 — Decision Trees):**

- ✅ **Decision Tree** = supervised, non-parametric, hierarchical model for classification and regression.
- ✅ Three structural elements: **Root Node**, **Internal (Decision) Nodes**, **Leaf (Terminal) Nodes**.
- ✅ **ID3** uses Information Gain; **C4.5** uses Gain Ratio; **CART** uses Gini Index and builds only binary trees.
- ✅ **Entropy** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ — measures disorder in **bits**.
- ✅ **Information Gain** $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ — *higher* is better; **biased toward many-valued features**.
- ✅ **Gain Ratio** $GR = IG / \text{SplitInfo}$ — *normalizes* IG; corrects the bias of ID3.
- ✅ **Gini Index** $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ — cheaper than entropy; preferred in CART.
- ✅ **Conditional Entropy** $H(S \mid A) = \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ — weighted average of child entropies.
- ✅ **Overfitting** in deep trees is combated by **pre-pruning** (early stopping) or **post-pruning** (reduced-error / cost-complexity).
- ✅ **Continuous attributes** are handled by binary threshold search at midpoints.
- ✅ **Missing values** are handled in C4.5 via probabilistic fractional splitting.
- ✅ **Advantages**: interpretability, no feature scaling, handles mixed data types, fast inference $O(\text{depth})$.
- ✅ **Disadvantages**: prone to overfitting, high variance, greedy splitting is locally but not globally optimal.
- ✅ **Robotics use cases**: behavior trees, fault diagnosis, terrain classification, grasp planning.
- ✅ **Always carry the negative sign** and the unit "bits" in entropy answers — KTU marks are deducted otherwise.
- ✅ **Recurse fully** — after selecting the root feature, repeat IG computation on each child subset with the remaining features.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logistic Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Logistic Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logistic Regression** is a **supervised machine learning classification algorithm** used to predict the probability of a categorical (typically binary) dependent output variable from a set of independent predictor variables. Despite its name containing "Regression," it is fundamentally a **classification** method that models the log-odds of the probability of an event as a linear combination of the input features.

Mathematically, Logistic Regression estimates the parameters of a logistic model — a form of the **Generalized Linear Model (GLM)** — in which the link function is the **logit** (natural log of the odds). The output is bounded between **0** and **1**, making it a probability estimator.

For a binary classification task, given an input feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the model predicts:

$$
\hat{y} = P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}, b) = \sigma(z) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **weight vector**
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **bias term** (intercept)
- $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the **sigmoid (logistic) activation function**
- $z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ is the **logit** or linear predictor

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Doctor's Diagnosis Threshold"**
> Imagine a doctor trying to diagnose whether a patient has a particular disease based on a set of symptoms (fever, blood pressure, age, cholesterol). The doctor doesn't simply add up symptoms like a linear regression model would. Instead, the doctor asks: *"What is the probability that this patient is sick, given the cumulative evidence?"*
> - Small symptoms → low probability (e.g., 0.05) → **No disease**
> - Moderate symptoms → moderate probability (e.g., 0.5) → **Uncertain**
> - Strong symptoms → high probability (e.g., 0.95) → **Disease confirmed**
>
> The doctor then applies a **threshold** (commonly **0.5**) to convert this probability into a binary decision. **Logistic Regression** mathematically formalizes this exact reasoning process — it transforms a linear combination of features into a probability using the **sigmoid curve**.

## 1.3 Why Not Linear Regression for Classification?

| Issue with Linear Regression | How Logistic Regression Solves It |
|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Output can be any real number ($- \infty$ to $+\infty$) | Output is bounded to $[0, 1]$ via sigmoid |
| Predictions can exceed probability bounds | Probability interpretation is valid |
| Highly sensitive to outliers | Robust due to sigmoid saturation |
| Squared error loss is non-convex for classification | Log loss is convex — unique global minimum |

## 1.4 Types of Logistic Regression

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Binary Logistic Regression** — Two outcomes (e.g., Spam/Not Spam, Pass/Fail)
> 2. **Multinomial Logistic Regression** — Three or more unordered categories (e.g., Digit 0–9 classification)
> 3. **Ordinal Logistic Regression** — Three or more ordered categories (e.g., Low/Medium/High rating)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sigmoid (Logistic) Function

The cornerstone of Logistic Regression is the **sigmoid function**, which squashes any real-valued number into the interval $(0, 1)$.

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

**Key Properties of $\sigma(z)$:**
- **Range:** $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ for all $z \in \mathbb{R}$
- **Monotonically increasing**
- **Symmetry:** $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$
- **Inflection point at** $z = 0$, where $\sigma(0) = 0.5$
- **Asymptotes:** $\sigma(z) \to 1$ as $z \to +\infty$; $\sigma(z) \to 0$ as $z \to -\infty$
- **Derivative (Self-Referential Property):**

$$
\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot (1 - \sigma(z))
$$

This beautiful self-referential derivative makes gradient computations extremely efficient.

## 2.2 The Logit Function and Log-Odds

The **logit function** is the inverse of the sigmoid and represents the natural logarithm of the odds:

$$
\text{logit}(p) = \ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)
$$

The **odds** of an event are defined as:

$$
\text{Odds} = \frac{p}{1-p}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Log-Odds?** The logarithm of odds maps the constrained probability space $p \in (0, 1)$ to an unconstrained real space $\mathbb{R}$. This allows a linear model to operate without violating probability constraints. The relationship is:
> - $p \to 0 \Rightarrow \text{logit}(p) \to -\infty$
> - $p = 0.5 \Rightarrow \text{logit}(p) = 0$
> - $p \to 1 \Rightarrow \text{logit}(p) \to +\infty$

## 2.3 Hypothesis Representation

The complete hypothesis for Logistic Regression is:

$$
h_{\mathbf{w}, b}(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)}}
$$

**Decision Rule:**

$$
\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } h_{\mathbf{w}, b}(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{if } h_{\mathbf{w}, b}(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5 \end{cases}
$$

This means $\hat{y} = 1$ when $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b \geq 0$.

## 2.4 The Cost Function — Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Mean Squared Error** (MSE) used in Linear Regression is **non-convex** when applied to the sigmoid output, leading to many local minima. Therefore, Logistic Regression uses the **Log Loss** (also called **Binary Cross-Entropy**), which is convex and yields a unique global minimum.

**For a single training example $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$:**

$$
\mathcal{L}\big(h_{\mathbf{w}, b}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)}\big) = -\Big[y^{(i)} \log\big(h_{\mathbf{w}, b}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})\big) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log\big(1 - h_{\mathbf{w}, b}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})\big)\Big]
$$

**For $m$ training examples (Cost Function):**

$$
J(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}\big(h_{\mathbf{w}, b}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)}\big)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition behind Log Loss:**
> - If $y^{(i)} = 1$ and the model predicts $h \to 1$ (correct), the loss $\to 0$.
> - If $y^{(i)} = 1$ but the model predicts $h \to 0$ (wrong), the loss $\to \infty$ (heavily penalized).
> - The negative log ensures minimization of cost corresponds to maximizing likelihood.

## 2.5 Gradient Descent Update Rules

The partial derivatives of the cost function are remarkably elegant:

$$
\frac{\partial J(\mathbf{w}, b)}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \big(h_{\mathbf{w}, b}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big) x_j^{(i)}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial J(\mathbf{w}, b)}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \big(h_{\mathbf{w}, b}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big)
$$

**Vectorized Form:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \frac{1}{m} \mathbf{X}^T (\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{y})
$$

**Update Rule (simultaneous update):**

$$
w_j := w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}, \quad b := b - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial b}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Logistic Regression

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Sigmoid Function | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Output range $(0,1)$ |
| 2 | Sigmoid Derivative | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | Self-referential |
| 3 | Logit (Log-Odds) | $\ln\!\big(\tfrac{p}{1-p}\big)$ | Inverse of sigmoid |
| 4 | Hypothesis | $h(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}+b)$ | Probability estimate |
| 5 | Single Loss | $-[y\log h + (1-y)\log(1-h)]$ | Log loss for one example |
| 6 | Cost Function | $J(\mathbf{w},b) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\mathcal{L}^{(i)}$ | Mean over $m$ samples |
| 7 | Gradient w.r.t $w_j$ | $\frac{1}{m}\sum(h^{(i)}-y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$ | Same form as linear regression |
| 8 | Gradient w.r.t $b$ | $\frac{1}{m}\sum(h^{(i)}-y^{(i)})$ | Scalar bias update |
| 9 | Decision Boundary | $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Where $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0.5$ |
| 10 | Odds Ratio | $e^{w_j}$ | Change in odds per unit $x_j$ |
| 11 | L2 Regularization | $J_{\text{reg}} = J + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum w_j^2$ | Prevents overfitting |
| 12 | Multinomial (Softmax) | $P(y=k\mid\mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}_k^T\mathbf{x}}}{\sum_j e^{\mathbf{w}_j^T\mathbf{x}}}$ | Multi-class extension |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of Logistic Regression

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Predicting disease presence/absence from patient biomarkers
- **Spam Filtering:** Classifying emails as spam or ham
- **Credit Scoring:** Estimating probability of loan default
- **Robotics:** Obstacle detection, gesture recognition, terrain classification
- **Manufacturing:** Defect classification in quality control
- **Marketing:** Customer churn prediction, conversion probability

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sigmoid Function from Probability Bounds

**Starting Point:** We require a function $f(z)$ such that:
- $f: \mathbb{R} \to (0, 1)$
- $f$ is differentiable and monotonically increasing
- $f(0) = 0.5$ (symmetry)
- $f$ has horizontal asymptotes at $0$ and $1$

Consider the differential equation governing logistic growth in population dynamics:

$$
\frac{df}{dz} = f(z)(1 - f(z))
$$

This separable ODE is justified because the rate of change must:
- Proportional to current value $f$ (more population → more growth)
- Saturate as $f \to 1$ (resource constraints)

**Solving the ODE:**

$$
\frac{df}{f(1-f)} = dz
$$

Applying partial fractions:

$$
\frac{1}{f(1-f)} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{1-f}
$$

Integrating both sides:

$$
\int \left(\frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{1-f}\right) df = \int dz
$$

$$
\ln\vert f \vert - \ln\vert 1 - f \vert = z + C
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{f}{1-f}\right) = z + C
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
\frac{f}{1-f} = e^{z+C} = e^C \cdot e^z
$$

Let $A = e^C$:

$$
\frac{f}{1-f} = A e^z
$$

Solving for $f$:

$$
f = (1-f) A e^z \Rightarrow f(1 + A e^z) = A e^z
$$

$$
f(z) = \frac{A e^z}{1 + A e^z} = \frac{A}{A + e^{-z}}
$$

Applying boundary condition $f(0) = 0.5$:

$$
0.5 = \frac{A}{A + 1} \Rightarrow 0.5(A+1) = A \Rightarrow 0.5 = 0.5A \Rightarrow A = 1
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cost Function via Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

**Assumption:** The training samples are **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)**, and labels $y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}$.

For a single training example, the model output represents:

$$
P(y = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}, b) = h_{\mathbf{w}, b}(\mathbf{x})
$$

$$
P(y = 0 \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}, b) = 1 - h_{\mathbf{w}, b}(\mathbf{x})
$$

**Compact Form (both cases):**

$$
P(y \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}, b) = h(\mathbf{x})^y \cdot (1 - h(\mathbf{x}))^{1-y}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formulation elegantly handles both classes: if $y=1$, only the first term survives; if $y=0$, only the second term survives.

**Likelihood Function over $m$ i.i.d. samples:**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \Big[h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})\Big]^{y^{(i)}} \Big[1 - h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})\Big]^{1 - y^{(i)}}
$$

**Log-Likelihood (to convert product into sum):**

$$
\ell(\mathbf{w}, b) = \log \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Big[ y^{(i)} \log h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})) \Big]
$$

**To minimize** cost, we negate and normalize:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{m} \ell(\mathbf{w}, b)
$$

$$
\boxed{J(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Big[ y^{(i)} \log h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})) \Big]}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Gradient Descent Update Rules

We need to compute $\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$ and $\frac{\partial J}{\partial b}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Chain Rule to Single Sample Loss:**

Let $\hat{y}^{(i)} = h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = \sigma(z^{(i)})$ where $z^{(i)} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b$.

For a single sample:

$$
\mathcal{L}^{(i)} = -\Big[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \Big]
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}} = -\left[ \frac{y^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}} - \frac{1 - y^{(i)}}{1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}} \right]
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}} = \frac{\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})}
$$

**Step 2 — Derivative of Sigmoid:**

$$
\frac{d\sigma(z)}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z)) = \hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})
$$

**Step 3 — Chain Rule (Chain Three Derivatives):**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{d\hat{y}^{(i)}}{dz^{(i)}} = \frac{\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})} \cdot \hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) = \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $\hat{y}^{(i)}(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})$ terms **cancel beautifully**, yielding the extremely clean result: $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}$.

**Step 4 — Derivative with respect to $w_j$:**

Since $z^{(i)} = w_1 x_1^{(i)} + w_2 x_2^{(i)} + \cdots + w_j x_j^{(i)} + \cdots + b$, we have $\frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = x_j^{(i)}$.

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 5 — Average over all $m$ samples:**

$$
\frac{\partial J(\mathbf{w}, b)}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 6 — Derivative with respect to $b$:**

Since $\frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial b} = 1$:

$$
\frac{\partial J(\mathbf{w}, b)}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Linear vs Logistic Regression

| Parameter | Linear Regression | Logistic Regression |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Target Variable** | Continuous (Real-valued) | Categorical (Binary/Multi-class) |
| **Hypothesis** | $h = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b$ | $h = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b)$ |
| **Output Range** | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | $(0, 1)$ |
| **Loss Function** | Squared Error | Log Loss (Cross-Entropy) |
| **Cost Function** | $J = \frac{1}{2m}\sum(h-y)^2$ | $J = -\frac{1}{m}\sum[y\log h + (1-y)\log(1-h)]$ |
| **Gradient Form** | $\frac{1}{m}\sum(h-y)x_j$ | $\frac{1}{m}\sum(h-y)x_j$ (same!) |
| **Decision Mechanism** | Threshold on continuous value | Threshold on probability |
| **Convexity of $J$** | Always convex | Convex (unlike MSE on sigmoid) |
| **Evaluation Metrics** | MSE, MAE, $R^2$ | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC |
