# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the sigmoid function. Why is it preferred over a step function in Logistic Regression?**

**Model Answer:**
The sigmoid (logistic) function is defined as:

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

It maps any real-valued input $z \in \mathbb{R}$ to the bounded output $(0, 1)$.

**Why preferred over the step function:**
1. **Differentiability:** The sigmoid is smooth and continuously differentiable, whereas the step function has a discontinuity at $z = 0$ where its derivative is undefined (zero everywhere else). This differentiability is essential for **gradient-based optimization**.
2. **Gradient Information:** The sigmoid provides meaningful gradients even in regions far from the decision boundary, allowing learning to proceed smoothly.
3. **Probabilistic Interpretation:** It yields a true probability estimate, unlike the binary $\{0, 1\}$ output of the step function.

The self-referential derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ makes gradient computations computationally cheap. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Explain the role of the logit function in Logistic Regression.**

**Model Answer:**
The **logit function** is defined as $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\big(\tfrac{p}{1-p}\big)$ and serves as the **link function** in Logistic Regression.

**Roles:**
1. **Probability to Real Line Mapping:** It maps probabilities $p \in (0, 1)$ to the unconstrained real line $\mathbb{R}$, enabling a linear combination of features.
2. **Inverse of Sigmoid:** It is the mathematical inverse of the sigmoid function, connecting the linear predictor to the probability.
3. **Log-Odds Interpretation:** It represents the logarithm of the odds ratio, providing an intuitive measure: $\ln(\text{odds})$.
4. **Enables Linear Modeling:** By modeling $\ln\!\big(\tfrac{p}{1-p}\big) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$, we obtain the logistic hypothesis when solved for $p$.

The slope coefficient $w_j$ is interpreted as the change in log-odds per unit increase in $x_j$. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the cost function (log loss) for Logistic Regression using the principle of **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given a training set with 3 samples: $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = (1, 2), y^{(1)} = 1$; $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = (2, 1), y^{(2)} = 0$; $\mathbf{x}^{(3)} = (3, 3), y^{(3)} = 1$. With initial parameters $\mathbf{w} = (0.1, 0.1)^T$, $b = 0$, and learning rate $\alpha = 0.5$, perform **one iteration** of gradient descent and compute the updated parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Log Loss via MLE

**Assumptions:**
1. Training samples are **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)**.
2. Labels follow a **Bernoulli distribution** conditioned on input features.
3. The model is parameterized by $(\mathbf{w}, b)$.

**Step 1 — Probability of a Single Observation:**

For $y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}$, the model assigns:

$$
P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \mathbf{w}, b) = h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})^{y^{(i)}} \cdot \big(1 - h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})\big)^{1-y^{(i)}}
$$

where $h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)$.

**Step 2 — Likelihood Function:**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})^{y^{(i)}} \cdot \big(1 - h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})\big)^{1-y^{(i)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Log-Likelihood:**

$$
\ell(\mathbf{w}, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Big[ y^{(i)} \log h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})) \Big]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Cost by Negating:**

$$
J(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{m} \ell(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Big[ y^{(i)} \log h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})) \Big]
$$

This is the **Binary Cross-Entropy / Log Loss**. We minimize $J$ because minimizing the negative log-likelihood is equivalent to maximizing the likelihood. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — One Iteration of Gradient Descent

**Given:** $\mathbf{w} = (0.1, 0.1)^T$, $b = 0$, $\alpha = 0.5$, $m = 3$.

**Step 1 — Compute $z^{(i)}$ and $h^{(i)}$ for each sample:**

**Sample 1:** $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = (1, 2), y^{(1)} = 1$

$$
z^{(1)} = (0.1)(1) + (0.1)(2) + 0 = 0.3
$$

$$
h^{(1)} = \sigma(0.3) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.3}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.7408} = \frac{1}{1.7408} \approx 0.5744
$$

**Sample 2:** $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = (2, 1), y^{(2)} = 0$

$$
z^{(2)} = (0.1)(2) + (0.1)(1) + 0 = 0.3
$$

$$
h^{(2)} = \sigma(0.3) \approx 0.5744
$$

**Sample 3:** $\mathbf{x}^{(3)} = (3, 3), y^{(3)} = 1$

$$
z^{(3)} = (0.1)(3) + (0.1)(3) + 0 = 0.6
$$

$$
h^{(3)} = \sigma(0.6) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.6}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.5488} = \frac{1}{1.5488} \approx 0.6457
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Errors $(h^{(i)} - y^{(i)})$:**

$$
e^{(1)} = 0.5744 - 1 = -0.4256
$$

$$
e^{(2)} = 0.5744 - 0 = 0.5744
$$

$$
e^{(3)} = 0.6457 - 1 = -0.3543
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Gradients:**

For $w_1$:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_1} = \frac{1}{3}\Big[(-0.4256)(1) + (0.5744)(2) + (-0.3543)(3)\Big]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{3}\Big[-0.4256 + 1.1488 - 1.0629\Big] = \frac{-0.3397}{3} \approx -0.1132
$$

For $w_2$:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_2} = \frac{1}{3}\Big[(-0.4256)(2) + (0.5744)(1) + (-0.3543)(3)\Big]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{3}\Big[-0.8512 + 0.5744 - 1.0629\Big] = \frac{-1.3397}{3} \approx -0.4466
$$

For $b$:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{3}\Big[-0.4256 + 0.5744 - 0.3543\Big] = \frac{-0.2055}{3} \approx -0.0685
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Update Rule:**

$$
w_1^{\text{new}} = 0.1 - (0.5)(-0.1132) = 0.1 + 0.0566 = 0.1566
$$

$$
w_2^{\text{new}} = 0.1 - (0.5)(-0.4466) = 0.1 + 0.2233 = 0.3233
$$

$$
b^{\text{new}} = 0 - (0.5)(-0.0685) = 0 + 0.0343 = 0.0343
$$

**Updated Parameters:**

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{w}^{\text{new}} \approx (0.1566,\ 0.3233)^T, \quad b^{\text{new}} \approx 0.0343}
$$

*(7 marks)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the difference between **Binary**, **Multinomial**, and **Ordinal** Logistic Regression. Derive the hypothesis for **Multinomial Logistic Regression** using the **Softmax function**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **overfitting** in Logistic Regression? Explain **L1 (Lasso)** and **L2 (Ridge)** regularization techniques with their mathematical formulations. Which regularization is preferred for **feature selection** and why? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Types and Multinomial Derivation

**Types of Logistic Regression:**

| Type | Output | Use Case |
|------|--------|----------|
| **Binary** | $y \in \{0, 1\}$ | Spam detection (Spam/Not Spam) |
| **Multinomial** | $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ | Digit recognition (0–9) |
| **Ordinal** | $y$ has natural order | Rating (Low < Med < High) |

**Multinomial Logistic Regression (Softmax Regression):**

For $K$ classes, the model assigns $K$ linear scores and applies the **Softmax function** to obtain normalized probabilities:

$$
P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{W}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}_k^T \mathbf{x}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\mathbf{w}_j^T \mathbf{x}}}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, K
$$

**Derivation Logic:** The Softmax is a natural generalization of the sigmoid. For $K = 2$:

$$
\frac{e^{w_1^T x}}{e^{w_1^T x} + e^{w_2^T x}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(w_2 - w_1)^T x}} = \sigma(-(w_2-w_1)^T x)
$$

which reduces to the binary case.

**Properties of Softmax:**
- Outputs form a valid probability distribution: $\sum_k P(y=k) = 1$
- Each $P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}) \in (0, 1)$
- Predicted class: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x})$

**Cost Function (Categorical Cross-Entropy):**

$$
J(\mathbf{W}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}\{y^{(i)} = k\} \log \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}_k^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\mathbf{w}_j^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)}}}
$$

*(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — Regularization Techniques

**Overfitting in Logistic Regression:**
Overfitting occurs when the model learns noise and specific patterns of the training data, leading to **high training accuracy but poor generalization** on unseen test data. Symptoms include very large weight magnitudes and a highly complex decision boundary.

**L2 Regularization (Ridge):**

$$
J_{\text{L2}}(\mathbf{w}, b) = J(\mathbf{w}, b) + \frac{\lambda}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2
$$

Penalizes the **sum of squared weights**. Encourages all weights to be small and distributed. Differentiable everywhere. **Preferred for general-purpose regularization.**

**L1 Regularization (Lasso):**

$$
J_{\text{L1}}(\mathbf{w}, b) = J(\mathbf{w}, b) + \frac{\lambda}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert w_j \vert
$$

Penalizes the **sum of absolute weights**. Drives many weights to **exactly zero**, performing **automatic feature selection**. Non-differentiable at $w_j = 0$ (handled via subgradients).

**Gradient Updates with L2:**

$$
w_j := w_j - \alpha \left[\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} + \frac{\lambda}{m} w_j\right]
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | L1 (Lasso) | L2 (Ridge) |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Penalty Term | $\sum \vert w_j \vert$ | $\sum w_j^2$ |
| Effect on Weights | Sparse (many become 0) | Small but non-zero |
| Feature Selection | Yes (built-in) | No |
| Computational Ease | Subgradient methods | Direct closed-form |
| Robust to Multicollinearity | Selects one feature | Distributes weights |

**Preferred for Feature Selection: L1 Regularization (Lasso)**
Because it forces irrelevant feature weights to become exactly zero, effectively removing them from the model. This is mathematically and computationally equivalent to performing feature selection automatically. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Confusing Regression with Classification:** Students often describe Logistic Regression as a "regression algorithm." It is a **classification algorithm** despite its name. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this conceptual error.
> 2. **Forgetting the Negative Sign in Cost Function:** Many students write the log-likelihood instead of the cost function (which has a negative sign). This leads to **incorrect minimization direction**.
> 3. **MSE Instead of Log Loss:** Using $\frac{1}{2}(h-y)^2$ as the cost function for Logistic Regression. This is **non-convex** when applied to the sigmoid output. Always use **log loss / cross-entropy**.
> 4. **Ignoring the Vectorization:** Examiners reward matrix-vector formulations. Writing scalar forms only may lose partial marks.
> 5. **Softmax vs Sigmoid Confusion in Multinomial:** Students sometimes use sigmoid for multi-class problems instead of softmax. Remember: **sigmoid for binary, softmax for multi-class**.
> 6. **Skipping the Decision Boundary Explanation:** Always explicitly mention the threshold ($\geq 0.5$) and the equation $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ when describing decision making.
> 7. **Numerical Errors in Gradient Descent:** Show ALL intermediate computations; even one skipped arithmetic step costs marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Logistic Regression**
>
> ✅ Logistic Regression is a **classification algorithm**, not regression.
>
> ✅ The **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ maps real numbers to $(0,1)$.
>
> ✅ **Hypothesis:** $h(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b)$; outputs a **probability**.
>
> ✅ **Cost Function** = **Binary Cross-Entropy / Log Loss** (derived via MLE).
>
> ✅ **Gradient descent** update rule has the **same elegant form** as linear regression: $\partial J / \partial w_j = \frac{1}{m}\sum(h-y)x_j$.
>
> ✅ **Decision Boundary:** $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b = 0$; classify as 1 if $h \geq 0.5$, else 0.
>
> ✅ **Multiclass extension** uses **Softmax** function, not sigmoid.
>
> ✅ **L1 (Lasso)** → Feature selection via sparsity; **L2 (Ridge)** → General regularization.
>
> ✅ **Odds Ratio:** $e^{w_j}$ is the multiplicative change in odds for a unit increase in $x_j$.
>
> ✅ **Convexity:** The log-loss cost function is **convex** in $\mathbf{w}$, guaranteeing a global minimum.
>
> ✅ **Evaluation metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC (NOT MSE).
>
> ✅ **Assumption:** Classes are linearly separable in the transformed (sigmoid) space.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a supervised machine learning algorithm used for **classification** and **regression** tasks. It finds an **optimal hyperplane** in an N-dimensional feature space that maximally separates data points belonging to different classes. The "optimality" is defined by the **maximum margin** — the largest perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the nearest training samples from any class.

In the context of robotics (course context **PERUT521**), SVMs are extensively used for:
- **Object recognition and classification** in vision pipelines.
- **Gesture and sign-language recognition** in human-robot interaction (HRI).
- **Fault detection and diagnosis** in robotic actuators and sensors.
- **Terrain classification** for autonomous ground vehicles.
- **One-class novelty detection** for anomaly detection in robotic systems.

## 1.2 Core Terminology (Syllabus Highlight)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Hyperplane** | Decision boundary that separates classes in feature space. |
| **Support Vectors** | Data points closest to the hyperplane; they "support" or define it. |
| **Margin** | Perpendicular distance from the hyperplane to the nearest support vectors. |
| **Kernel Function** | A mathematical function that maps data into higher-dimensional space. |
| **Slack Variable ($\xi_i$)** | Permits margin violations for non-separable data. |
| **Regularization Parameter ($C$)** | Controls trade-off between margin width and classification error. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Widest Road"

Imagine a busy city where two rival neighborhoods (Class **+1** and Class **-1**) need to be divided by a road. A city planner must decide:
1. **Where to draw the road** (the hyperplane).
2. **How wide the road should be** (the margin).

The city planner's rule: **Build the WIDEST possible road** between the neighborhoods without demolishing any house. The houses closest to the road's edges are the **support vectors** — they dictate the road's position and width. If a few stray houses fall inside the road (misclassified or near the boundary), we use **slack variables** to forgive them — analogous to a small fine rather than demolition.

> [!NOTE]
> The road's edge on each side is defined mathematically by:
> **w·x + b = +1** (positive margin) and **w·x + b = -1** (negative margin).
> Any point **on** the road centerline satisfies **w·x + b = 0**.

## 1.4 Why SVM for Robotics?

Robotic datasets are often:
- **High-dimensional** (sensor arrays, image features).
- **Limited in size** (expensive to label).
- **Not linearly separable** in raw form.

SVMs excel in these regimes because the **kernel trick** enables non-linear decision boundaries without an explicit, computationally expensive mapping to high-dimensional space. The maximum-margin principle provides **strong generalization** with limited training data — critical in robotics where labeling is costly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear SVM — The Mathematical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Hyperplane Equation

In an $n$-dimensional feature space $\mathbb{R}^n$, a hyperplane is defined as:

$$
\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b = 0
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **weight (normal) vector**, perpendicular to the hyperplane.
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **bias term** (offset from origin).
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is a feature vector.

### 2.1.2 Decision Rule

For a binary classification problem with labels $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$:

$$
\hat{y} = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The classification is correct if and only if $y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ for all training samples — this is the **canonical (functional) margin constraint**.

### 2.1.3 Margin Width Derivation

The perpendicular distance from any point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane is:

$$
d = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

For the closest support vectors on each side, $\vert \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b \vert = 1$, hence the **total margin** between the two boundary hyperplanes is:

$$
\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

## 2.2 The Optimization Problem — Hard Margin

To **maximize** the margin $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ is mathematically equivalent to **minimizing** $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ (a convex, quadratic objective). The primal problem is:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2
$$

$$
\text{subject to: } y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, N
$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is for mathematical convenience (its derivative is $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$, avoiding a factor of 2).

## 2.3 The Optimization Problem — Soft Margin (Real-World Data)

For data that is **not perfectly linearly separable**, slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ are introduced (Cortes & Vapnik, 1995):

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$

$$
\text{subject to: } y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0
$$

The parameter $C > 0$ controls the penalty for margin violations:
- **Large $C$** → Strict penalty on misclassification → Narrower margin, fewer violations (risk of **overfitting**).
- **Small $C$** → Lenient penalty → Wider margin, more violations tolerated (risk of **underfitting**).

## 2.4 The Lagrangian Dual Formulation

Introducing Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ (for hard margin), the **primal** converts to the **dual**:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

$$
\text{subject to: } \alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KKT Complementarity Condition**: $\alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0$.
> This means **$\alpha_i > 0$ only for support vectors**, and $\alpha_i = 0$ for all other points — confirming the **sparsity** property of SVM.

## 2.5 The Kernel Trick — Non-Linear SVM

The dual formulation reveals that all data appears as inner products $\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j$. We can replace this inner product with a **kernel function**:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \cdot \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)
$$

Where $\phi(\cdot)$ is an implicit mapping to a higher-dimensional space. By **Mercer's Theorem**, $K$ is a valid kernel if and only if it is symmetric and positive semi-definite.

### 2.5.1 Common Kernel Functions

| Kernel Name | Mathematical Form | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linear Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{x}'$ | Linearly separable data; baseline. |
| **Polynomial Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{x}' + r)^d$ | Image processing, moderate non-linearity. |
| **RBF (Gaussian) Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}' \Vert^2)$ | Most popular; handles complex non-linearity. |
| **Sigmoid Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \tanh(\gamma \, \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{x}' + r)$ | Neural network analogue. |

Where $\gamma > 0$ is the kernel coefficient and $r$ is a constant offset.

### 2.5.2 Kernel Decision Function

After training, the decision function becomes:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right)
$$

The sum runs only over **support vectors** — again demonstrating SVM's sparsity advantage.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Hyperplane equation | $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $b \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Total margin width | Maximize this |
| $\dfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ | Primal objective (hard margin) | Minimize this |
| $\dfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ | Primal objective (soft margin) | $C > 0$ regularization |
| $\xi_i \geq 0$ | Slack variable | Permits margin violation |
| $\alpha_i \geq 0$ | Lagrange multipliers | Non-zero only for SVs |
| $y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ | Canonical margin constraint | Hard separable |
| $\sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum \sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Dual objective | Maximize |
| $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ | Dual equality constraint | Necessary condition |
| $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}' \Vert^2)$ | RBF kernel | $\gamma > 0$ |
| $\text{sign}(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b)$ | Decision rule | Output: +1 or -1 |
| **0/1 Loss** | $\mathbb{1}\{y \neq \hat{y}\}$ | Not convex, used as reference |
| **Hinge Loss** | $\max(0, 1 - y \cdot f(\mathbf{x}))$ | Convex upper bound of 0/1 loss |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | SVM Role |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Robotic Vision** | Object/face/gesture classification via HOG + SVM. |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Pedestrian and lane detection. |
| **Medical Robotics** | Tumor classification in surgical imagery. |
| **Predictive Maintenance** | Fault classification in actuator sensor streams. |
| **NLP for HRI** | Intent classification in voice-controlled robots. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Distance from a Point to the Hyperplane

**Goal**: Show that the perpendicular distance from point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ equals $\frac{\vert \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

**Step 1 — Geometry setup**:
Let $\mathbf{x}_0$ be any point. The foot of the perpendicular from $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane is some point $\mathbf{x}_p$. The vector from $\mathbf{x}_p$ to $\mathbf{x}_0$ must be parallel to $\mathbf{w}$ (since $\mathbf{w}$ is the normal to the hyperplane).

**Step 2 — Parameterize the perpendicular line**:
The perpendicular line in the direction of $\mathbf{w}$ can be written as:

$$
\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}_p = t \cdot \mathbf{w}
$$

Where $t$ is a real scalar. Solving for $\mathbf{x}_p$:

$$
\mathbf{x}_p = \mathbf{x}_0 - t \cdot \mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply hyperplane constraint**:
Since $\mathbf{x}_p$ lies ON the hyperplane, it must satisfy:

$$
\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_p + b = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\mathbf{x}_p$ into the constraint**:

$$
\mathbf{w} \cdot (\mathbf{x}_0 - t \cdot \mathbf{w}) + b = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Expand using distributive property**:

$$
\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_0 - t (\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{w}) + b = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize $\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{w} = \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$**:

$$
\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_0 + b - t \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $t$**:

$$
t = \frac{\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_0 + b}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2}
$$

**Step 8 — The perpendicular distance is $\vert t \vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$** (length of vector $t \cdot \mathbf{w}$):

$$
d = \vert t \vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = \left\vert \frac{\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_0 + b}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2} \right\vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert
$$

**Step 9 — Simplify** (cancel one $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$):

$$
d = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This expression is foundational for understanding the geometric margin and is the entry point to the SVM optimization problem.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Margin Maximization as Minimization of $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$

**Goal**: Show that maximizing the margin $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$.

**Step 1 — Express the margin using the distance formula**:
The margin is the sum of distances from the hyperplane to the nearest positive-class support vector $\mathbf{x}_+$ and the nearest negative-class support vector $\mathbf{x}_-$:

$$
\text{Margin} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_+ + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} + \frac{\vert \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_- + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply canonical scaling**:
By rescaling $(\mathbf{w}, b)$, we can enforce $\vert \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_+ + b \vert = 1$ and $\vert \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_- + b \vert = 1$ (this is the **canonical hyperplane** assumption).

**Step 3 — Substitute**:

$$
\text{Margin} = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} + \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximize the margin**:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to minimization using monotonic equivalence**:
Since the function $f(t) = \frac{1}{t}$ is monotonically decreasing for $t > 0$, maximizing $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$. Squaring preserves the optimization direction:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2
$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is purely for algebraic convenience (gradient becomes $\mathbf{w}$ instead of $2\mathbf{w}$).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lagrangian Dual Transformation

**Goal**: Convert the hard-margin primal into its dual form.

**Step 1 — Primal problem recap**:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \leq 0, \; \forall i
$$

**Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian** with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]
$$

The minus sign arises because we penalize violation of $y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \geq 0$.

**Step 3 — Take partial derivatives and set to zero**:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\mathbf{w}$**:

$$
\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **support vector expansion** — the weight vector is a linear combination of training samples, with $\alpha_i \neq 0$ only for support vectors. This expression is the gateway to the kernel trick.

**Step 5 — Substitute back into $\mathcal{L}$**:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i \right) \cdot \left( \sum_{j} \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j \right) - \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i \cdot \left( \sum_{j} \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j \right) - \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i b + \sum_{i} \alpha_i
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the first two terms** (they combine):

$$
\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j) - \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j) = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

**Step 7 — Eliminate $b$ via the constraint** $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_D(\alpha) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

**Step 8 — Dual problem**:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \mathcal{L}_D(\alpha) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Kernelized Decision Function

**Goal**: Show how the decision function becomes kernelized.

**Step 1 — Start from the primal decision rule**:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the support vector expansion** for $\mathbf{w}$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i \right) \cdot \mathbf{x} + b
$$

**Step 3 — Distribute the dot product**:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i (\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}) + b
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the kernel substitution** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \cdot \phi(\mathbf{x})$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b
$$

**Step 5 — Restrict sum to support vectors** ($\alpha_i \neq 0$ only for SVs):

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b
$$

**Step 6 — Final classification**:

$$
\hat{y} = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial insight**: To classify a new test point, we only need kernel evaluations against the **support vectors** — NOT against every training point. This is the **sparsity property** of SVM and the computational efficiency of the kernelized form.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Hyperparameter Selection Guide

| Scenario | Recommended Kernel | Recommended $C$ | Recommended $\gamma$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linearly separable, low-dim | Linear | High (e.g., 100) | Not applicable |
| Linearly separable, high-dim | Linear or RBF | Moderate (e.g., 1) | Low (e.g., 0.01) |
| Non-linear, small dataset | RBF | Low (e.g., 0.1) | Scale-dependent |
| Non-linear, large dataset | Linear (scaled) or RBF | Tune via CV | Tune via CV |
| Image classification | RBF or Chi-squared | Tune via CV | Tune via CV |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SVM Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram depicts the modular pipeline of an SVM-based classification system in a robotics context.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sensor Input] --> B[Preprocessing Module]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction Module]
    C --> D[Feature Vector x]
    D --> E[Kernel Mapping K]
    E --> F[SVM Decision Function]
    F --> G[Sign Function]
    G --> H[Class Label]
    I[Training Samples] --> J[Compute w and b]
    J --> K[Lagrange Multipliers]
    K --> F
    L[Hyperparameter C] --> J
    M[Kernel gamma] --> E
```

## 4.2 Maximum Margin Concept (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Class Negative Points] -->|distance d1| B[Hyperplane w.x + b = 0]
    C[Class Positive Points] -->|distance d2| B
    B --> D[Width Equals 2 over norm w]
    E[Support Vector Negative] --> A
    F[Support Vector Positive] --> C
```

## 4.3 Kernel Trick — Implicit Mapping Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Input Space R2] --> B[Apply Kernel Function K]
    B --> C[Implicit Higher Dimensional Space Rn]
    C --> D[Linear Separation Possible]
    D --> E[Project Back to Input Space]
    E --> F[Non-Linear Boundary in R2]
```

## 4.4 SVM Training Workflow — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Training] --> B[Initialize w and b to zero]
    B --> C[Compute Margin Violations]
    C --> D{Violations Exist?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Update w using alpha]
    D -->|No| F[Convergence Check]
    E --> C
    F --> G{Converged?}
    G -->|No| C
    G -->|Yes| H[Store Support Vectors and b]
    H --> I[End Training]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above use semantic flow descriptions to convey the operational architecture of the SVM pipeline in lieu of complex physical drawings (such as high-dimensional kernel plots), which are not representable in text-based diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define Support Vector Machine. What are support vectors in the context of SVM?**

**Model Answer:**

A Support Vector Machine (SVM) is a supervised learning algorithm that classifies data by finding an **optimal hyperplane** that maximally separates samples of different classes in feature space.

**Support vectors** are the data points that lie closest to the decision hyperplane — they directly determine its position and orientation. They are the only training samples with non-zero Lagrange multipliers ($\alpha_i > 0$). Removing a non-support-vector from the training set does **not change** the decision boundary, while removing a support vector **does**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The name "Support Vector **Machine**" comes from these support vectors — they "support" (define) the maximum-margin hyperplane. *(3 marks — Definition 2, Support vectors 1)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**What is the kernel trick in SVM? Mention any two commonly used kernel functions.**

**Model Answer:**

The **kernel trick** is a technique that enables SVM to learn non-linear decision boundaries by replacing the inner product $\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j$ in the dual formulation with a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \cdot \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$, without explicitly computing the higher-dimensional mapping $\phi(\cdot)$.

**Two common kernels:**
1. **Polynomial Kernel**: $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{x}' + r)^d$
2. **Radial Basis Function (RBF) Kernel**: $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}' \Vert^2)$

> [!NOTE]
> For the kernel trick to be valid, $K$ must satisfy **Mercer's Theorem** (symmetric, positive semi-definite). *(3 marks — Definition 2, Kernels 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Formulate the optimization problem for a **soft-margin linear SVM**. Explain the role of the **slack variables** $\xi_i$ and the **regularization parameter** $C$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **maximum margin classifier**. Derive the expression for the **margin width** in terms of the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a)** Soft-margin linear SVM formulation:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$

$$
\text{subject to: } y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \; \forall i
$$

**Role of slack variables $\xi_i$:**
- They quantify the **degree of margin violation** for each training sample.
- $\xi_i = 0$ → Point is correctly classified and outside or on the margin.
- $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$ → Point lies inside the margin but correctly classified.
- $\xi_i > 1$ → Point is misclassified.

**Role of regularization parameter $C$:**
- $C$ controls the trade-off between **margin maximization** and **training error minimization**.
- **Large $C$** → Heavy penalty for violations → Narrow margin, low bias, high variance (overfitting risk).
- **Small $C$** → Lenient penalty → Wide margin, high bias, low variance (underfitting risk).

---

**(b)** Maximum margin classifier (refer to diagram in SECTION 4.2):

The maximum margin classifier chooses the hyperplane $\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ such that the **perpendicular distance** from the hyperplane to the nearest data point of either class is maximized.

**Derivation of margin width:**

The two parallel margin boundaries are:

$$
\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b = +1 \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b = -1
$$

Using the point-to-hyperplane distance formula (derived in SECTION 3.1), the perpendicular distance from a point to a hyperplane is $\frac{\vert \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

For the nearest positive support vector $\mathbf{x}_+$:

$$
d_+ = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_+ + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

For the nearest negative support vector $\mathbf{x}_-$:

$$
d_- = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_- + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

Total margin width:

$$
M = d_+ + d_- = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} + \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

Maximizing $M$ is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$, which is the primal SVM objective.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the **dual formulation** of SVM. How does the dual lead to the concept of the **kernel trick**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss any **three kernel functions** used in SVM, with their mathematical formulations and typical use cases. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a)** Dual formulation of SVM:

Starting from the primal:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1
$$

We form the Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]
$$

Setting partial derivatives to zero:

$$
\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

Substituting back yields the dual:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

$$
\text{subject to: } \alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

**Connection to the kernel trick:**

In the dual, training data appears **only as inner products** $(\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j)$. Replacing each inner product with a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ allows SVM to operate in an implicit high-dimensional feature space **without ever computing the explicit mapping** $\phi(\mathbf{x})$. This is the **kernel trick** — it converts a non-linear problem into a linear one in higher dimensions, while computational cost remains controlled because $K$ can often be evaluated cheaply.

---

**(b)** Three kernel functions:

**1. Linear Kernel**:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{x}'
$$

- Simplest kernel; no parameters.
- Equivalent to a linear classifier in original space.
- Use case: Linearly separable data, baseline, large high-dimensional sparse data (e.g., text).

**2. Polynomial Kernel**:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{x}' + r)^d
$$

- Parameters: degree $d$, coefficient $\gamma$, constant $r$.
- Captures polynomial feature interactions up to order $d$.
- Use case: Image processing, NLP where moderate non-linearity exists; $d = 2$ or $3$ typical.

**3. Radial Basis Function (RBF) / Gaussian Kernel**:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \exp\left( -\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}' \Vert^2 \right)
$$

- Parameter: kernel width $\gamma > 0$.
- Maps data to **infinite-dimensional** space (Taylor expansion of Gaussian).
- Use case: Default choice for non-linear SVM; handles complex, arbitrary decision boundaries; works well in robotics for gesture, terrain, and object classification.

> [!NOTE]
> For all kernels, **Mercer's condition** (positive semi-definiteness) must hold for the optimization to remain convex and well-posed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in SVM questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing primal and dual** — The primal optimizes over $(\mathbf{w}, b)$, the dual optimizes over $\alpha$. Many students mix these up.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor** in the primal objective — It is there for mathematical convenience; omitting it does NOT change the solution but loses a mark for "incomplete formulation."
> 3. **Misstating the margin** — The total margin is $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$, not $\frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$. The latter is only ONE side.
> 4. **Forgetting constraints** in soft-margin formulation — Both $y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ AND $\xi_i \geq 0$ are required.
> 5. **Kernel trick misconception** — Students often think $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ is computed explicitly. **It is NEVER computed**; only $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')$ is evaluated.
> 6. **Missing the role of $C$ vs $\gamma$** — $C$ trades off margin width vs misclassification; $\gamma$ (in RBF) controls the locality/influence radius of each support vector.
> 7. **Forgetting the bias $b$** in the decision function — Many students write only $\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}$, omitting the offset.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SVM**
>
> - **Definition**: SVM is a maximum-margin classifier that finds the optimal separating hyperplane.
> - **Margin formula**: Total margin width $= \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.
> - **Primal objective (hard margin)**: $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$.
> - **Soft margin**: Adds $C \sum \xi_i$ penalty and relaxes constraint to $y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$.
> - **Support vectors**: Training points closest to the hyperplane; the **only** points with $\alpha_i > 0$.
> - **Support vector expansion**: $\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$.
> - **Dual objective**: $\max_\alpha \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.
> - **Dual constraints**: $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.
> - **Kernel trick**: Replace $\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j$ with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ to enable non-linear boundaries.
> - **Most common kernel**: RBF (Gaussian) — $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}' \Vert^2)$.
> - **Decision rule**: $\hat{y} = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right)$.
> - **KKT complementary slackness**: $\alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0$.
> - **Robotic applications**: Object recognition, gesture classification, terrain analysis, fault detection.
> - **Hyperparameter intuition**: Large $C$ = strict; small $C$ = lenient. Large $\gamma$ = local/bumpy; small $\gamma$ = smooth/global.
> - **Sparsity advantage**: Only support vectors are needed for prediction — efficient inference.
> - **Mercer's condition**: Kernel must be symmetric and positive semi-definite for valid SVM optimization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning Algorithms: K-Means Clustering, K-Nearest Neighbors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning Algorithms: K-Means Clustering, K-Nearest Neighbors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Unsupervised Learning — Formal Definition

**Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning in which an agent (or algorithm) is exposed to a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \dots, x^{(n)}\}$ consisting of **unlabeled input vectors** $x^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, and must discover an internal structure, grouping, density profile, or low-dimensional manifold without any externally provided supervisory signal $y^{(i)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** Unsupervised learning algorithms infer a function that describes the hidden structure of *unlabeled* data. Since no ground-truth labels exist, the objective is to model the underlying probability distribution $\mathcal{P}(x)$ or the geometric relationships in the feature space $\mathcal{F}$.

The two principal families of unsupervised tasks relevant to **AI for Robotics** are:

| Family | Goal | Robotics Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Clustering** | Group similar observations into $k$ partitions | SLAM loop-closure detection, point-cloud segmentation |
| **Dimensionality Reduction** | Project $\mathbb{R}^{m} \to \mathbb{R}^{p}$, $p \ll m$ | Visual feature compression for embedded GPUs |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 module treats **K-Means** strictly as an unsupervised algorithm. However, **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** is a *supervised* algorithm. It is grouped alongside K-Means because both rely on a **distance-based, instance-memory learning philosophy** — and KNN forms the conceptual bridge toward more advanced supervised robotics classifiers (e.g., gesture recognition, terrain classification).

---

## 1.2 K-Means Clustering — Definition

**K-Means Clustering** is an **unsupervised, centroid-based, partitional clustering algorithm** proposed by Stuart Lloyd (1957) and later formalised by MacQueen (1967). Given $n$ data points in $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ and a pre-specified integer $k$, K-Means partitions the data into exactly $k$ non-overlapping clusters $\{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ such that the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** is minimised.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Candy Jar Sorter" 🍬

> Imagine you are given a mixed jar of 100 candies of **3 different colours** (red, blue, green) thrown randomly together. You have **no labels** telling you the colour boundaries. You are told $k=3$. What do you do?
> 1. **You guess 3 random points** on the table as your initial "sorting centres."
> 2. **You assign each candy** to the centre nearest to it.
> 3. **You physically move each centre** to the average position of the candies assigned to it.
> 4. **You repeat** until the centres stop moving.
>
> That is *exactly* K-Means. The candies are the data points $x^{(i)}$, the sorting centres are the centroids $\mu_j$, and the "stop moving" condition is convergence.

---

## 1.3 K-Nearest Neighbors — Definition

**K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** is a **supervised, non-parametric, instance-based (lazy) learning algorithm** used for both classification and regression. For a query point $x_q \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, the algorithm identifies the $k$ training examples closest to $x_q$ (using a distance metric) and outputs:

- The **mode** (majority class label) for classification.
- The **mean** of the $k$ target values for regression.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Neighbourhood Vote" 🏘️

> Suppose you are new to a city and want to know whether a house you are inspecting is in a "safe" or "unsafe" neighbourhood. You have no map, but you do know the labels of $N$ previously inspected houses. With $k=5$, you **walk out and find the 5 closest houses you have data on**. If 4 of them are "safe" and 1 is "unsafe", the majority vote (4 vs 1) classifies your new house as "safe." That is KNN in plain English.

---

## 1.4 High-Yield Vocabulary for KTU 2024

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Centroid** | $\mu_j$ | Mean vector of all points in cluster $C_j$ |
| **Inertia / WCSS** | $J$ | Sum of squared distances of points to their assigned centroid |
| **Query Point** | $x_q$ | New, unseen data point to be classified/clustered |
| **Distance Metric** | $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ | Function measuring dissimilarity (Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski) |
| **Hyperparameter $k$** | $k$ | Number of clusters (K-Means) or neighbours (KNN) |
| **Curse of Dimensionality** | — | Exponential sparsity of data as $m \to \infty$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold constants/metrics to memorise for KTU 2024 ESE:** $k$, $J$ (WCSS), Euclidean distance $d_2$, Manhattan distance $d_1$, Minkowski parameter $p \geq 1$.

---

## 1.5 Real-World Robotics Use-Cases

| Algorithm | Robotics Application |
|---|---|
| **K-Means** | Colour-based object segmentation; LiDAR point-cloud clustering for obstacle detection; trajectory grouping for behaviour analysis |
| **KNN** | Terrain classification from IMU/foot-pressure sensors; nearest-neighbour grasp selection; visual place recognition; anomaly detection in sensor streams |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 K-Means — Theoretical Decomposition

K-Means solves the following **NP-hard combinatorial optimisation problem**:

$$\min_{\{C_1, \dots, C_k\}} \;\; J(\{C_j\}_{j=1}^{k}) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_j} \left\| x^{(i)} - \mu_j \right\|_2^2$$

Because exact minimisation is intractable for large $n$, K-Means uses **Lloyd's heuristic** — an iterative, expectation-maximisation-style algorithm guaranteed to **converge to a local minimum** (not necessarily the global optimum).

### Step-by-Step Algorithmic Logic

1. **Input specification:** Choose $k$, dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(n)}\}$, and a convergence threshold $\varepsilon > 0$.
2. **Centroid initialisation:** Initialise $\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_k^{(0)}$. Common methods: Forgy (random data points), Random Partition, K-Means++.
3. **Assignment step (Expectation):** For every $x^{(i)} \in \mathcal{D}$, assign it to the cluster whose centroid is closest.

$$C_j^{(t)} = \left\{ x^{(i)} : \left\| x^{(i)} - \mu_j^{(t)} \right\|_2^2 \;\le\; \left\| x^{(i)} - \mu_\ell^{(t)} \right\|_2^2 \;\;\forall \ell \in \{1,\dots,k\} \right\}$$

4. **Update step (Maximisation):** Recompute each centroid as the mean of points currently assigned to it.

$$\mu_j^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_j^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_j^{(t)}} x^{(i)}$$

5. **Convergence check:** Stop when $\sum_{j=1}^{k} \left\| \mu_j^{(t+1)} - \mu_j^{(t)} \right\|_2 < \varepsilon$, or when cluster memberships stabilise, or a maximum iteration count is reached.

> [!NOTE]
> The "Why" behind Lloyd's heuristic: it is mathematically equivalent to the **Expectation–Maximisation (EM) algorithm** applied to a mixture of $k$ isotropic Gaussians with hard (rather than soft) assignments. Each iteration is **guaranteed to monotonically decrease** $J$.

---

## 2.2 K-Nearest Neighbors — Theoretical Decomposition

KNN is a **non-parametric, lazy learner**: it stores the entire training set $\mathcal{T} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ in memory and defers all computation until a query arrives. There is **no explicit training phase**.

### Step-by-Step Algorithmic Logic

1. **Choose $k$** (an odd value is preferred for binary classification to avoid tie-breaking).
2. **Choose a distance metric** $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ (typically Euclidean).
3. **Compute distances** from the query $x_q$ to all $N$ training samples.
4. **Sort distances** in ascending order; pick the top $k$ samples.
5. **Aggregate labels:** For classification, the predicted class is

$$\hat{y}_q = \mathrm{mode}\left(\{y^{(i)} : x^{(i)} \in \mathcal{N}_k(x_q)\}\right)$$

For regression, the predicted value is

$$\hat{y}_q = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in \mathcal{N}_k(x_q)} y^{(i)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of the $k$ choice:** A small $k$ (e.g., $k=1$) makes the model **highly sensitive to noise** (high variance, low bias). A large $k$ **over-smooths** the decision boundary (low variance, high bias). The optimal $k$ minimises generalisation error and is typically chosen via **cross-validation**.

---

## 2.3 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table of all critical equations, boundary conditions, and units for Module 2 / Topic: K-Means & KNN.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Constraints |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_j} \left\| x^{(i)} - \mu_j \right\|_2^2$ | WCSS objective for K-Means | $J \geq 0$; $J \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 2 | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_j} x^{(i)}$ | Centroid update rule | $\mu_j \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| 3 | $d_2(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | **Euclidean distance** ($\ell_2$-norm) | $d_2 \geq 0$; symmetric; triangle inequality holds |
| 4 | $d_1(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | **Manhattan distance** ($\ell_1$-norm) | $d_1 \geq 0$ |
| 5 | $d_p(x, y) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | **Minkowski distance** (general form) | $p \geq 1$ integer; $p=2 \Rightarrow$ Euclidean |
| 6 | $\hat{y}_q = \mathrm{arg}\max_{c} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}\{y^{(i)} = c\}$ | KNN classification rule | $c$ over class labels |
| 7 | $\hat{y}_q = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in \mathcal{N}_k} y^{(i)}$ | KNN regression rule | — |
| 8 | $J_{opt} = \mathrm{arg}\min_k \sum_{i=1}^{k} J_i(k)$ | Elbow method: $k^*$ at the "elbow" | Heuristic, not a closed form |
| 9 | $\mathrm{SSE}_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_j} d(x^{(i)}, \mu_j)^2$ | Sum of Squared Errors (alternative notation for WCSS) | Same as $J$ |
| 10 | $\text{Silhouette}(i) = \frac{b_i - a_i}{\max(a_i, b_i)}$ | Silhouette score for cluster validation | $s(i) \in [-1, +1]$; higher is better |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of the WCSS equation:** In a **robotic assembly cell**, K-Means can group welding-spark acoustic signatures into $k$ clusters corresponding to weld-quality classes. Minimising WCSS yields the most compact, well-separated acoustic clusters, directly translating into a higher weld-defect detection rate.

---

## 2.4 Choosing the Optimal $k$ — The Elbow Method

The **WCSS $J(k)$ is a monotonically non-increasing function of $k$**. As $k \to n$, $J \to 0$ (every point is its own cluster). The "elbow" point $k^*$ is the smallest $k$ beyond which the marginal reduction in $J$ becomes negligible — the trade-off between **model simplicity** and **fit quality**.

Plot $J$ vs $k$; visually identify the inflection point. The elbow point is the recommended $k^*$.

---

## 2.5 Computational Complexity

| Algorithm | Time per Iteration | Space Complexity | Convergence Guarantee |
|---|---|---|---|
| **K-Means** | $O(n \cdot k \cdot m)$ | $O((n+k) \cdot m)$ | Yes, to a local minimum |
| **KNN (Brute Force)** | $O(N \cdot m)$ per query | $O(N \cdot m)$ | No training phase |

> [!WARNING]
> K-Means is **not** guaranteed to find the *global* optimum. The result is **highly sensitive to centroid initialisation**. The K-Means++ initialisation scheme (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007) provides a probabilistic $O(\log k)$-approximation guarantee and is the de-facto industry default.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — K-Means Clustering

### Problem Statement (KTU 2024-Style)

Given the six 2-D data points:

$$A = (1, 1), \;\; B = (1, 2), \;\; C = (2, 1), \;\; D = (8, 8), \;\; E = (9, 9), \;\; F = (9, 8)$$

Apply the **K-Means algorithm** with $k = 2$ and initial centroids:

$$\mu_1^{(0)} = (2, 1), \qquad \mu_2^{(0)} = (8, 8)$$

Compute (a) the cluster assignments after **Iteration 1** and **Iteration 2**; (b) the final WCSS value.

---

### Step 1 — Distance Computation (Iteration 1)

We compute the squared Euclidean distance $\left\| x^{(i)} - \mu_j^{(0)} \right\|_2^2$ for every point to every centroid.

| Point | $\left\| x - \mu_1^{(0)} \right\|_2^2$ | $\left\| x - \mu_2^{(0)} \right\|_2^2$ | Assigned Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A=(1,1)$ | $(1-2)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 1 + 0 = 1$ | $(1-8)^2 + (1-8)^2 = 49 + 49 = 98$ | $C_1$ |
| $B=(1,2)$ | $(1-2)^2 + (2-1)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$ | $(1-8)^2 + (2-8)^2 = 49 + 36 = 85$ | $C_1$ |
| $C=(2,1)$ | $(2-2)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 0 + 0 = 0$ | $(2-8)^2 + (1-8)^2 = 36 + 49 = 85$ | $C_1$ |
| $D=(8,8)$ | $(8-2)^2 + (8-1)^2 = 36 + 49 = 85$ | $(8-8)^2 + (8-8)^2 = 0 + 0 = 0$ | $C_2$ |
| $E=(9,9)$ | $(9-2)^2 + (9-1)^2 = 49 + 64 = 113$ | $(9-8)^2 + (9-8)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$ | $C_2$ |
| $F=(9,8)$ | $(9-2)^2 + (8-1)^2 = 49 + 49 = 98$ | $(9-8)^2 + (8-8)^2 = 1 + 0 = 1$ | $C_2$ |

**Cluster assignments after Iteration 1:**

$$C_1^{(1)} = \{A, B, C\}, \qquad C_2^{(1)} = \{D, E, F\}$$

---

### Step 2 — Centroid Update (Iteration 1 → Iteration 2)

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{A + B + C}{3} = \left(\frac{1+1+2}{3}, \frac{1+2+1}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right) \approx (1.33, 1.33)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{D + E + F}{3} = \left(\frac{8+9+9}{3}, \frac{8+9+8}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{26}{3}, \frac{25}{3}\right) \approx (8.67, 8.33)$$

---

### Step 3 — Re-Assignment (Iteration 2)

| Point | Distance² to $\mu_1^{(1)}$ | Distance² to $\mu_2^{(1)}$ | Assigned Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A=(1,1)$ | $(1-1.33)^2 + (1-1.33)^2 = 0.11 + 0.11 = 0.22$ | $(1-8.67)^2 + (1-8.33)^2 = 58.83 + 53.73 = 112.56$ | $C_1$ |
| $B=(1,2)$ | $(1-1.33)^2 + (2-1.33)^2 = 0.11 + 0.45 = 0.56$ | $(1-8.67)^2 + (2-8.33)^2 = 58.83 + 40.07 = 98.90$ | $C_1$ |
| $C=(2,1)$ | $(2-1.33)^2 + (1-1.33)^2 = 0.45 + 0.11 = 0.56$ | $(2-8.67)^2 + (1-8.33)^2 = 44.49 + 53.73 = 98.22$ | $C_1$ |
| $D=(8,8)$ | $(8-1.33)^2 + (8-1.33)^2 = 44.49 + 44.49 = 88.98$ | $(8-8.67)^2 + (8-8.33)^2 = 0.45 + 0.11 = 0.56$ | $C_2$ |
| $E=(9,9)$ | $(9-1.33)^2 + (9-1.33)^2 = 58.83 + 58.83 = 117.66$ | $(9-8.67)^2 + (9-8.33)^2 = 0.11 + 0.45 = 0.56$ | $C_2$ |
| $F=(9,8)$ | $(9-1.33)^2 + (8-1.33)^2 = 58.83 + 44.49 = 103.32$ | $(9-8.67)^2 + (8-8.33)^2 = 0.11 + 0.11 = 0.22$ | $C_2$ |

**Cluster assignments after Iteration 2:**

$$C_1^{(2)} = \{A, B, C\}, \qquad C_2^{(2)} = \{D, E, F\}$$

Since the cluster memberships have **not changed** from Iteration 1, the algorithm has **converged**.

---

### Step 4 — Final WCSS Computation

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_j} \left\| x^{(i)} - \mu_j^{(1)} \right\|_2^2$$

$$J_{C_1} = 0.22 + 0.56 + 0.56 = 1.34$$

$$J_{C_2} = 0.56 + 0.56 + 0.22 = 1.34$$

$$\boxed{J_{\text{final}} = J_{C_1} + J_{C_2} = 1.34 + 1.34 = 2.68}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — K-Nearest Neighbors

### Problem Statement (KTU 2024-Style)

Given the labelled 2-D training set:

| Class | Training Points |
|---|---|
| $\omega_1$ (Safe) | $(1, 1), (1, 2), (2, 1)$ |
| $\omega_2$ (Unsafe) | $(8, 8), (9, 9), (8, 9)$ |

A new query point $x_q = (3, 3)$ must be classified using **KNN with $k=3$** and **Euclidean distance**.

---

### Step 1 — Pairwise Euclidean Distance Computation

$$d_2(x_q, x^{(i)}) = \sqrt{(x_q^{(1)} - x_i^{(1)})^2 + (x_q^{(2)} - x_i^{(2)})^2}$$

We compute the squared distances first for numerical efficiency, then take the square root only for the final table.

| Training Point $x^{(i)}$ | Class | $(3 - x_1)^2$ | $(3 - x_2)^2$ | $d_2^2$ | $d_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(1, 1)$ | $\omega_1$ | $4$ | $4$ | $8$ | $\sqrt{8} \approx 2.83$ |
| $(1, 2)$ | $\omega_1$ | $4$ | $1$ | $5$ | $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$ |
| $(2, 1)$ | $\omega_1$ | $1$ | $4$ | $5$ | $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$ |
| $(8, 8)$ | $\omega_2$ | $25$ | $25$ | $50$ | $\sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$ |
| $(9, 9)$ | $\omega_2$ | $36$ | $36$ | $72$ | $\sqrt{72} \approx 8.49$ |
| $(8, 9)$ | $\omega_2$ | $25$ | $36$ | $61$ | $\sqrt{61} \approx 7.81$ |

---

### Step 2 — Sort & Select Top-$k$

Sorting the distances in ascending order:

1. $d_2((1,2), x_q) = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24 \;\; \to \;\; \omega_1$
2. $d_2((2,1), x_q) = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24 \;\; \to \;\; \omega_1$
3. $d_2((1,1), x_q) = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83 \;\; \to \;\; \omega_1$
4. $d_2((8,8), x_q) = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07 \;\; \to \;\; \omega_2$
5. $d_2((8,9), x_q) = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.81 \;\; \to \;\; \omega_2$
6. $d_2((9,9), x_q) = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.49 \;\; \to \;\; \omega_2$

The **top $k=3$ nearest neighbours** are: $\{(1,2), (2,1), (1,1)\}$ — all three belong to class $\omega_1$.

---

### Step 3 — Majority Voting

$$\hat{y}_q = \mathrm{mode}\left(\{\omega_1, \omega_1, \omega_1\}\right) = \omega_1$$

$$\boxed{\text{The query point } x_q = (3, 3) \text{ is classified as } \omega_1 \text{ (Safe)}.}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — K-Means vs. KNN

| Dimension | K-Means | KNN |
|---|---|---|
| **Learning Paradigm** | Unsupervised | Supervised (despite KTU module placement) |
| **Training Phase** | Iterative centroid updates | None (lazy) |
| **Output Type** | Cluster labels $\{1, \dots, k\}$ | Class label or real value |
| **Key Hyperparameter** | $k$ (number of clusters) | $k$ (number of neighbours) |
| **Distance Metric** | Euclidean (default) | Euclidean / Manhattan / Minkowski |
| **Sensitivity to Noise** | Moderate (centroid shift) | High when $k$ is small |
| **Scalability** | $O(n \cdot k \cdot m \cdot T)$ | $O(N \cdot m)$ per query |
| **Use in Robotics** | Segmentation, SLAM loop closure | Terrain / gesture recognition |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-Means Algorithm — Operational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Dataset D and k] --> B[Initialize k Centroids mu1 mu2 ... muk]
    B --> C[Assignment Step: Compute Distance x_i to mu_j for all points]
    C --> D[Assign Each x_i to the Nearest Centroid Cluster C_j]
    D --> E[Update Step: Recompute mu_j as Mean of Points in C_j]
    E --> F{Convergence Check: Centroid Shift less than epsilon}
    F -->|No| C
    F -->|Yes| G[Output Final Clusters C1 C2 ... Ck]
    G --> H[End]
```

## 4.2 K-Nearest Neighbors — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Query Point xq] --> B[Choose Hyperparameter k and Distance Metric d]
    B --> C[Compute d xq x_i for all N training samples]
    C --> D[Sort Distances in Ascending Order]
    D --> E[Select Top k Nearest Neighbors N_k of xq]
    E --> F{Task Type}
    F -->|Classification| G[Majority Vote: Mode of Neighbor Labels]
    F -->|Regression| H[Mean of Neighbor Target Values]
    G --> I[Output Predicted Class Label yhat_q]
    H --> J[Output Predicted Real Value yhat_q]
    I --> K[End]
    J --> K[End]
```

## 4.3 K-Means++ Centroid Initialisation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Randomly Pick First Centroid mu1 from D] --> B[Compute Distance Squared d2 x mu1 for all remaining points]
    B --> C[Assign Selection Probability Proportional to d2]
    C --> D[Pick Next Centroid mu2 with Weighted Probability]
    D --> E{All k Centroids Initialized}
    E -->|No| C
    E -->|Yes| F[Run Standard Lloyd K Means Iterations]
    F --> G[Final Stable Clusters]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Clustering vs. Classification Pipeline for Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sensor Data: LiDAR Camera IMU] --> B[Preprocessing: Noise Filter Normalization]
    B --> C{Application Pipeline}
    C -->|Unsupervised Path| D[K Means Clustering]
    C -->|Supervised Path| E[KNN Classifier]
    D --> F[Robot Action: Object Grouping Path Planning]
    E --> G[Robot Action: Terrain Label Grasp Selection]
    F --> H[Actuator Output]
    G --> H[Actuator Output]
```
