# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define unsupervised learning. List any two unsupervised learning algorithms and give one robotics application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> Unsupervised learning is a machine learning paradigm in which an algorithm is trained on a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(n)}\}$ containing only **unlabelled** input vectors, and must discover hidden structure — clusters, density profiles, or low-dimensional manifolds — without any supervisory target signal $y^{(i)}$.
>
> **Two algorithms and robotics applications:**
> 1. **K-Means Clustering** → Used in LiDAR point-cloud segmentation to group obstacle points into distinct object clusters for an autonomous mobile robot.
> 2. **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** *(or DBSCAN, Autoencoders)* → Used to compress high-dimensional visual feature descriptors for embedded robot vision processors.

> [!NOTE]
> Award full marks only if the student provides a *definition* + *two algorithms* + *one robotics use-case each* (1 mark per element × 3).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the "curse of dimensionality" in the context of the KNN algorithm? How does it affect classification accuracy?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **curse of dimensionality** refers to the exponential growth of the feature space volume as the number of dimensions $m$ increases. For KNN, this causes:
> 1. **Data sparsity** — the $N$ training samples become extremely sparse in $\mathbb{R}^{m}$, so the "nearest" neighbour is no longer meaningfully close.
> 2. **Distance concentration** — in high dimensions, the ratio of the farthest to the nearest neighbour approaches 1, so the notion of "closeness" loses discriminative power.
> 3. **Consequence:** KNN classification accuracy degrades sharply for $m \gtrsim 10$ unless $N$ grows exponentially with $m$.
>
> **Mitigations:** dimensionality reduction (PCA), feature selection, and distance-weighted KNN.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** with a neat flowchart. Discuss its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Algorithm Definition:** K-Means is a centroid-based, unsupervised, partitional clustering algorithm that partitions $n$ data points into exactly $k$ non-overlapping clusters $\{C_1, \dots, C_k\}$ by minimising the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares:

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_j} \left\| x^{(i)} - \mu_j \right\|_2^2$$

**2. Step-by-Step Procedure:**

| Step | Operation |
|---|---|
| 1 | Choose $k$; initialise centroids $\mu_1^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_k^{(0)}$ (e.g., K-Means++). |
| 2 | **Assignment step** — for every $x^{(i)}$, assign it to the cluster with the nearest centroid. |
| 3 | **Update step** — recompute $\mu_j^{(t+1)}$ as the mean of points currently in $C_j$. |
| 4 | Repeat steps 2–3 until centroids stabilise ($\left\| \mu_j^{(t+1)} - \mu_j^{(t)} \right\|_2 < \varepsilon$) or a maximum iteration count is reached. |
| 5 | Output final cluster memberships. |

**3. Flowchart (as drawn in Section 4.1):** Initialise → Assign → Update → Converge → Output.

**4. Advantages:**
- Simple, intuitive, and computationally efficient: $O(n \cdot k \cdot m \cdot T)$.
- Easily scalable to large datasets (Mini-Batch K-Means variant).
- Guarantees convergence to a local minimum in finite iterations.

**5. Limitations:**
- Requires pre-specifying $k$ (use elbow method or silhouette analysis).
- Sensitive to centroid initialisation; converges to **local** optima only.
- Assumes **isotropic, spherical clusters** of similar size — fails on elongated or irregular shapes.
- Sensitive to outliers (a single outlier can pull the centroid).
- Distance metric (Euclidean) is scale-dependent — **standardisation of features is mandatory**.

---

**(b)** Consider the 2-D data points $A(2,10),\; B(2,5),\; C(8,4),\; D(5,8),\; E(7,5),\; F(6,4),\; G(1,2),\; H(4,9)$. Apply K-Means with $k=3$ and initial centroids $\mu_1 = (2, 10),\; \mu_2 = (5, 8),\; \mu_3 = (1, 2)$. Show **two iterations** and compute the final **WCSS**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Iteration 1 — Distance computation and assignment:**

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_3$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A(2,10)$ | 0 | 9 + 4 = 13 | 1 + 64 = 65 | $C_1$ |
| $B(2,5)$ | 0 + 25 = 25 | 9 + 9 = 18 | 1 + 9 = 10 | $C_3$ |
| $C(8,4)$ | 36 + 36 = 72 | 9 + 16 = 25 | 49 + 4 = 53 | $C_2$ |
| $D(5,8)$ | 9 + 4 = 13 | 0 | 16 + 36 = 52 | $C_2$ |
| $E(7,5)$ | 25 + 25 = 50 | 4 + 9 = 13 | 36 + 9 = 45 | $C_2$ |
| $F(6,4)$ | 16 + 36 = 52 | 1 + 16 = 17 | 25 + 4 = 29 | $C_2$ |
| $G(1,2)$ | 1 + 64 = 65 | 16 + 36 = 52 | 0 | $C_3$ |
| $H(4,9)$ | 4 + 1 = 5 | 1 + 1 = 2 | 9 + 49 = 58 | $C_2$ |

**Iteration 1 memberships:** $C_1 = \{A\}$; $C_2 = \{C, D, E, F, H\}$; $C_3 = \{B, G\}$.

**Centroid update (Iteration 1 → 2):**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = (2, 10)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{8+5+7+6+4}{5}, \frac{4+8+5+4+9}{5}\right) = (6, 6)$$

$$\mu_3^{(1)} = \left(\frac{2+1}{2}, \frac{5+2}{2}\right) = (1.5, 3.5)$$

**Iteration 2 — Re-assignment with new centroids:**

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1^{(1)}$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2^{(1)}$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_3^{(1)}$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A(2,10)$ | 0 | 16 + 16 = 32 | 0.25 + 42.25 = 42.5 | $C_1$ |
| $B(2,5)$ | 0 + 25 = 25 | 16 + 1 = 17 | 0.25 + 2.25 = 2.5 | $C_3$ |
| $C(8,4)$ | 36 + 36 = 72 | 4 + 4 = 8 | 42.25 + 0.25 = 42.5 | $C_2$ |
| $D(5,8)$ | 9 + 4 = 13 | 1 + 4 = 5 | 12.25 + 20.25 = 32.5 | $C_2$ |
| $E(7,5)$ | 25 + 25 = 50 | 1 + 1 = 2 | 30.25 + 2.25 = 32.5 | $C_2$ |
| $F(6,4)$ | 16 + 36 = 52 | 0 + 4 = 4 | 20.25 + 0.25 = 20.5 | $C_2$ |
| $G(1,2)$ | 1 + 64 = 65 | 25 + 16 = 41 | 0.25 + 2.25 = 2.5 | $C_3$ |
| $H(4,9)$ | 4 + 1 = 5 | 4 + 9 = 13 | 6.25 + 30.25 = 36.5 | $C_1$ |

**Iteration 2 memberships:** $C_1 = \{A, H\}$; $C_2 = \{C, D, E, F\}$; $C_3 = \{B, G\}$.

**Centroid update (Iteration 2 → 3):**

$$\mu_1^{(2)} = \left(\frac{2+4}{2}, \frac{10+9}{2}\right) = (3, 9.5)$$

$$\mu_2^{(2)} = \left(\frac{8+5+7+6}{4}, \frac{4+8+5+4}{4}\right) = (6.5, 5.25)$$

$$\mu_3^{(2)} = \left(\frac{2+1}{2}, \frac{5+2}{2}\right) = (1.5, 3.5) \quad \text{(unchanged)}$$

Re-assigning points with these centroids yields **the same memberships** as Iteration 2 → **Convergence achieved.**

**Final WCSS Computation:**

$$J_{C_1} = \left\|(2,10) - (3,9.5)\right\|^2 + \left\|(4,9) - (3,9.5)\right\|^2 = 1.25 + 1.25 = 2.5$$

$$J_{C_2} = (8-6.5)^2 + (4-5.25)^2 + (5-6.5)^2 + (8-5.25)^2 + (7-6.5)^2 + (5-5.25)^2 + (6-6.5)^2 + (4-5.25)^2$$

$$J_{C_2} = 2.25 + 1.5625 + 2.25 + 7.5625 + 0.25 + 0.0625 + 0.25 + 1.5625 = 15.75$$

$$J_{C_3} = (2-1.5)^2 + (5-3.5)^2 + (1-1.5)^2 + (2-3.5)^2 = 0.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 = 5.0$$

$$\boxed{J_{\text{final}} = 2.5 + 15.75 + 5.0 = 23.25}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) algorithm** for classification. Discuss how the choice of $k$ and the distance metric affect performance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition:** KNN is a **non-parametric, supervised, instance-based (lazy) learning algorithm** that classifies a query point $x_q \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ by majority vote among its $k$ nearest training samples.

**2. Procedure:**

| Step | Operation |
|---|---|
| 1 | Choose $k$ (preferably odd) and a distance metric $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ — typically Euclidean. |
| 2 | For query $x_q$, compute $d(x_q, x^{(i)})$ for all $N$ training samples. |
| 3 | Sort distances ascending; select the top $k$ indices: $\mathcal{N}_k(x_q)$. |
| 4 | Predict: $\hat{y}_q = \mathrm{mode}\{y^{(i)} : x^{(i)} \in \mathcal{N}_k(x_q)\}$. |
| 5 | Optionally weight by inverse distance: $w_i = 1/d(x_q, x^{(i)})$. |

**3. Effect of $k$ on the Bias–Variance Trade-off:**

| $k$ | Bias | Variance | Decision Boundary | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Very small ($k=1$) | Low | High | Jagged, complex | Overfitting / noise-sensitive |
| Moderate ($k \approx \sqrt{N}$) | Balanced | Balanced | Smooth | Optimal generalisation |
| Very large ($k \to N$) | High | Low | Over-smoothed | Underfitting |

**4. Effect of Distance Metric:**

| Metric | Formula | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| Euclidean ($d_2$) | $\sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Continuous, low-dim, isotropic features |
| Manhattan ($d_1$) | $\sum \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | High-dim, sparse, grid-like data |
| Minkowski ($d_p$) | $(\sum \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p)^{1/p}$ | Tunable via $p$ |
| Cosine | $1 - \frac{x \cdot y}{\|x\| \cdot \|y\|}$ | Text / angular similarity |

**5. Computational Considerations:**
- **Brute-force KNN:** $O(N \cdot m)$ per query — infeasible for large $N$.
- **Tree-based KNN (KD-Tree, Ball Tree):** $O(\log N)$ average per query for low-dimensional data.
- **Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH):** Approximate $O(1)$ per query.

---

**(b)** A robotic arm classifies objects as **"Graspable" (G)** or **"Non-Graspable (NG)** using a 2-D feature vector (weight, fragility). The labelled training set is:

| Class | Points |
|---|---|
| $G$ | $(2, 1), (3, 2), (2, 3)$ |
| $NG$ | $(8, 9), (9, 8), (8, 8)$ |

Classify the new test point $x_q = (4, 4)$ using **KNN with $k=3$** and **Euclidean distance**. Show all calculations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute squared Euclidean distances:**

| $x^{(i)}$ | Class | $(4-x_1)^2$ | $(4-x_2)^2$ | $d_2^2$ | $d_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(2, 1)$ | $G$ | 4 | 9 | 13 | $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$ |
| $(3, 2)$ | $G$ | 1 | 4 | 5 | $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$ |
| $(2, 3)$ | $G$ | 4 | 1 | 5 | $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$ |
| $(8, 9)$ | $NG$ | 16 | 25 | 41 | $\sqrt{41} \approx 6.40$ |
| $(9, 8)$ | $NG$ | 25 | 16 | 41 | $\sqrt{41} \approx 6.40$ |
| $(8, 8)$ | $NG$ | 16 | 16 | 32 | $\sqrt{32} \approx 5.66$ |

**Step 2 — Sort ascending and select top $k=3$:**

| Rank | $x^{(i)}$ | $d_2$ | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(3, 2)$ | $2.24$ | $G$ |
| 2 | $(2, 3)$ | $2.24$ | $G$ |
| 3 | $(2, 1)$ | $3.61$ | $G$ |

**Step 3 — Majority vote:**

$$\hat{y}_q = \mathrm{mode}\{G, G, G\} = G$$

$$\boxed{\text{The query point } x_q = (4, 4) \text{ is classified as } G \text{ (Graspable)}.}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing K-Means and KNN roles** — KNN is supervised. If a student lists KNN under "unsupervised learning" without clarification, deduct **½ to 1 mark** on definition-based questions.
> 2. **Forgetting to square distances in numerical problems** — Euclidean distance requires the **square root**. Showing only $(x_1-y_1)^2 + (x_2-y_2)^2$ without taking $\sqrt{\cdot}$ is an incomplete solution. Deduct **1 mark**.
> 3. **Choosing $k$ as even in a binary classification problem** — This leads to tie-breaking ambiguity. Always state "$k$ should be odd for binary classification."
> 4. **Not standardising features before K-Means** — K-Means uses Euclidean distance; if features are on different scales (e.g., weight in kg, fragility in N), the clustering is dominated by the larger-scale feature. Always mention **Z-score standardisation**: $z_i = (x_i - \mu) / \sigma$.
> 5. **Confusing WCSS and Silhouette score** — WCSS measures **compactness**; Silhouette score measures **both compactness and separation**. They are *not* interchangeable.
> 6. **K-Means gives a LOCAL minimum, not GLOBAL** — A frequent conceptual error in ESE answers. Always specify "Lloyd's algorithm guarantees convergence to a local minimum."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density summary — print this section 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ **K-Means** is **unsupervised**; **KNN** is **supervised** (despite module placement).
- ☐ K-Means **minimises WCSS** $J = \sum_{j} \sum_{x \in C_j} \|x - \mu_j\|^2$.
- ☐ K-Means has **two iterative steps**: (1) assignment, (2) centroid update.
- ☐ K-Means converges to a **local** minimum; result is **sensitive to initialisation** → use **K-Means++**.
- ☐ KNN is a **lazy learner**; no explicit training phase; prediction cost is $O(N \cdot m)$ per query.
- ☐ The **distance metric** for both algorithms is **Euclidean** by default; alternatives are **Manhattan** and **Minkowski**.
- ☐ The **elbow method** selects $k$ by plotting $J$ vs $k$ and locating the inflection point.
- ☐ The **Silhouette score** $s(i) = (b_i - a_i) / \max(a_i, b_i) \in [-1, +1]$ validates cluster quality.
- ☐ For KNN, $k$ small → high variance (overfit); $k$ large → high bias (underfit). Optimal $k \approx \sqrt{N}$.
- ☐ Choose **odd $k$** for binary classification to avoid ties.
- ☐ **Curse of dimensionality** degrades KNN performance for $m \gtrsim 10$.
- ☐ **Feature standardisation** is mandatory before applying K-Means or KNN.
- ☐ K-Means: $O(n \cdot k \cdot m \cdot T)$ per training run; KNN: $O(N \cdot m)$ per query (brute-force).
- ☐ Robotics use-cases: K-Means → **point-cloud segmentation, SLAM loop closure**; KNN → **terrain classification, grasp selection, gesture recognition**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Component Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT521 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principal Component Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is an orthogonal linear transformation technique that maps a set of possibly correlated variables (features) into a new set of linearly uncorrelated variables called **Principal Components (PCs)**. This transformation is defined such that the **first principal component** accounts for the **maximum possible variance** in the data, and each subsequent component is orthogonal to the previous ones while capturing the highest remaining variance.

For an input data matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ containing $n$ samples and $m$ features, PCA computes an orthonormal projection matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}$ (with $k \leq m$) such that the projected data $Y = XW$ lies in a lower-dimensional subspace that preserves the **maximum statistical information** (variance) of the original dataset.

In the context of **Robotics and AI**, PCA is classified as a **classical unsupervised dimensionality reduction** algorithm, frequently employed in **sensor fusion pre-processing**, **visual odometry pipelines**, **SLAM feature compression**, and **deep-learning feature whitening** stages.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shadow & Silhouette" Intuition

Imagine a 3D object (like a coffee mug) placed in front of a wall, and you are asked to describe its shape using only a **2D shadow**.

- The **worst choice** of light angle would be a top-down light → a flat, blurry ellipse. (You lose information.)
- The **best choice** of light angle is from the side → a clear silhouette that captures the mug's height and handle.

**PCA is exactly this "smart light placement"**. The original high-dimensional data is the 3D object. PCA finds the **best angle (direction)** along which to "shine a light" so that the **shadow (projection)** retains the **most distinguishing structure (variance)** of the original object. The first principal component is the direction that produces the most informative shadow; subsequent components are perpendicular directions that capture the remaining detail.

### 1.3 Why PCA is Critical in Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** A single LiDAR sweep in an autonomous vehicle can produce **~100,000 points per second** with dozens of features per point (x, y, z, intensity, ring, timestamp). Transmitting and processing all this data in real-time is computationally infeasible. PCA compresses this stream into a few dominant axes with **minimal information loss**, enabling real-time perception.

### 1.4 Key Physical / Statistical Constants

- **Variance Threshold (η):** Typical value = **0.95** (retain 95% of total variance).
- **Kaiser Criterion Threshold:** Eigenvalue $\lambda_i > 1$ (for standardized data).
- **Whitening Constant (ε):** Small regularizer, typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$, added to eigenvalues to prevent division by zero.

### 1.5 Prerequisite Concepts

| Concept | Why it is needed |
|---|---|
| Linear Algebra (Eigenvalues/Eigenvectors) | Forms the mathematical backbone of PCA |
| Covariance & Correlation | Quantifies how features vary together |
| Mean Centering | Required to make data zero-mean before PCA |
| Orthogonal Projections | PCA finds orthogonal axes of maximum variance |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Six Sequential Phases

PCA is executed in **six logically distinct phases**. Understanding each phase's *why* and *how* is critical for KTU ESE questions.

**Phase 1 — Data Acquisition & Matrix Formulation**
- Collect the dataset and arrange it as a matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ where each row is one observation and each column is one feature.
- *Why:* PCA operates on tabular numerical data; raw form must be matrix-compatible.

**Phase 2 — Mean Centering (Bias Removal)**
- Subtract the column-wise mean from every column: $X_c = X - \bar{X}$, where $\bar{X}$ is the row vector of column means.
- *Why:* Without centering, the first principal component will simply point toward the mean of the data cloud, not toward the direction of maximum spread. Centering is a **mandatory preprocessing step**.

**Phase 3 — Covariance Matrix Construction**
- Compute the empirical covariance matrix: $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^T X_c \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$.
- *Why:* The covariance matrix encodes the joint variability of all feature pairs. Diagonal entries are variances; off-diagonal entries are pairwise covariances.

**Phase 4 — Eigendecomposition**
- Solve the characteristic equation: $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ for all eigenpairs $(\lambda_i, v_i)$.
- *Why:* The eigenvectors of the covariance matrix are the **principal axes** of the data cloud; the eigenvalues quantify the **amount of variance** along each axis.

**Phase 5 — Ranking & Selection**
- Sort eigenpairs in descending order of $\lambda_i$.
- Select the top-$k$ eigenvectors to form the projection matrix $W_k = [v_1 \mid v_2 \mid \dots \mid v_k]$.
- *Why:* Rank-ordering ensures that the components capturing the most variance are chosen first.

**Phase 6 — Projection & Reconstruction**
- Project: $Y = X_c W_k$ (reduced representation in $k$ dimensions).
- Reconstruct: $\hat{X} = Y W_k^T + \bar{X}$ (approximation back in original space).
- *Why:* The projection gives the compact code; reconstruction allows us to measure information loss via the **reconstruction error** $\lVert X - \hat{X} \rVert_F^2$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The following table contains every formula you are likely to need. Memorize the *form*, not the *derivation* during the 3-hour paper.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{x}_j = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}$ | Column-wise mean of feature $j$ | Mean centering |
| 2 | $X_c = X - \mathbf{1}\bar{X}$ | Centered data matrix ( $\mathbf{1}$ is $n \times 1$ ones vector) | Preprocessing |
| 3 | $\Sigma = \dfrac{1}{n-1} X_c^T X_c$ | Sample covariance matrix | Variance measurement |
| 4 | $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ | Eigenvalue equation for axis $i$ | Finding principal axes |
| 5 | $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$ | Characteristic equation | Solving for eigenvalues |
| 6 | $\eta_k = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i}$ | Cumulative explained variance ratio | Choosing $k$ |
| 7 | $Y = X_c W_k$ | Projection to $k$-D subspace | Dimensionality reduction |
| 8 | $\hat{X} = Y W_k^T + \bar{X}$ | Reconstruction to original space | Error analysis |
| 9 | $E_{\text{rec}} = \dfrac{\lVert X - \hat{X} \rVert_F^2}{\lVert X \rVert_F^2}$ | Normalized reconstruction error | Quality metric |
| 10 | $\text{PC}_i \perp \text{PC}_j, \quad i \neq j$ | Orthogonality constraint | Decorrelation property |
| 11 | $\text{Var}(Y) = W^T \Sigma W$ | Variance of projected data | Optimization objective |
| 12 | $X_{\text{std}} = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Z-score standardization | When features have different units |

> **Notation Convention:** $\lVert \cdot \rVert_F$ denotes the **Frobenius norm**; $\mathbf{1}$ is an all-ones vector; $I$ is the identity matrix.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of PCA |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles (LiDAR/Perception)** | Compress point-cloud features for real-time object detection |
| **Robotic Manipulators** | Reduce dimensionality of joint-angle trajectories for motion planning |
| **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization & Mapping)** | Compact bag-of-visual-words descriptors for loop closure |
| **Humanoid Robotics** | Reduce motion-capture data (50+ joint angles) to a few "principal motion modes" |
| **Anomaly Detection in Industrial Robots** | Identify deviations in multivariate sensor streams via reconstruction error |
| **Image Processing** | Face recognition (Eigenfaces), image compression, denoising |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why PCA Maximizes Variance

**Claim:** The first principal component is the unit vector $w_1 \in \mathbb{R}^m$ that maximizes the variance of the projected data $X_c w_1$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Express projected variance.**
The variance of the scalar projection $X_c w$ is given by:

$$
\text{Var}(X_c w) = \dfrac{1}{n-1} (X_c w)^T (X_c w) = w^T \left( \dfrac{1}{n-1} X_c^T X_c \right) w = w^T \Sigma w
$$

**Step 2 — Constrain the optimization.**
We want to maximize $w^T \Sigma w$ subject to the unit-length constraint $w^T w = 1$. We use the **method of Lagrange multipliers**:

$$
\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = w^T \Sigma w - \lambda (w^T w - 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Set gradient to zero.**
Differentiate with respect to $w$ and equate to zero:

$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 2 \Sigma w - 2 \lambda w = 0
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \Sigma w = \lambda w
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**
The above is exactly the **eigenvalue equation** of the covariance matrix. The maximizer $w$ is an eigenvector, and the maximum variance equals the corresponding eigenvalue:

$$
\text{Var}(X_c w_i) = w_i^T \Sigma w_i = w_i^T (\lambda_i w_i) = \lambda_i \quad (\text{since } w_i^T w_i = 1)
$$

**Conclusion:** The optimal projection direction is the **eigenvector of $\Sigma$ with the largest eigenvalue**, and the variance captured equals that eigenvalue. This derivation is the most frequently asked 14-mark proof in KTU exams.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (High-Yield for ESE)

**Problem Setup:** Given data matrix $X$ (4 samples, 2 features) representing the (x, y) coordinates of robot end-effector positions in mm:

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 2.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 \\ 2.2 & 2.9 \\ 1.9 & 2.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the column means.**

$$
\bar{x}_1 = \dfrac{2.5 + 0.5 + 2.2 + 1.9}{4} = \dfrac{7.1}{4} = 1.775
$$

$$
\bar{x}_2 = \dfrac{2.4 + 0.7 + 2.9 + 2.2}{4} = \dfrac{8.2}{4} = 2.050
$$

**Step 2 — Mean-center the data.**
Subtract the mean vector $\bar{X} = [1.775, \; 2.050]$ from each row:

$$
X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0.725 & 0.350 \\ -1.275 & -1.350 \\ 0.425 & 0.850 \\ 0.125 & 0.150 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the covariance matrix.**
Using $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^T X_c$ with $n = 4$, so $n-1 = 3$:

$$
X_c^T X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0.725 & -1.275 & 0.425 & 0.125 \\ 0.350 & -1.350 & 0.850 & 0.150 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.725 & 0.350 \\ -1.275 & -1.350 \\ 0.425 & 0.850 \\ 0.125 & 0.150 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Computing element (1,1):**

$$
(0.725)^2 + (-1.275)^2 + (0.425)^2 + (0.125)^2 = 0.5256 + 1.6256 + 0.1806 + 0.0156 = 2.3474
$$

**Computing element (1,2) = (2,1):**

$$
(0.725)(0.350) + (-1.275)(-1.350) + (0.425)(0.850) + (0.125)(0.150)
$$

$$
= 0.2538 + 1.7213 + 0.3613 + 0.0188 = 2.3552
$$

**Computing element (2,2):**

$$
(0.350)^2 + (-1.350)^2 + (0.850)^2 + (0.150)^2 = 0.1225 + 1.8225 + 0.7225 + 0.0225 = 2.6900
$$

**Dividing by $n-1 = 3$:**

$$
\Sigma = \dfrac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2.3474 & 2.3552 \\ 2.3552 & 2.6900 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7825 & 0.7851 \\ 0.7851 & 0.8967 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Find eigenvalues from $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$:**

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} 0.7825 - \lambda & 0.7851 \\ 0.7851 & 0.8967 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

$$
(0.7825 - \lambda)(0.8967 - \lambda) - (0.7851)^2 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - (0.7825 + 0.8967)\lambda + (0.7825)(0.8967) - 0.6164 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - 1.6792 \lambda + 0.7016 - 0.6164 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - 1.6792 \lambda + 0.0852 = 0
$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1.6792 \pm \sqrt{(1.6792)^2 - 4(0.0852)}}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1.6792 \pm \sqrt{2.8197 - 0.3408}}{2} = \dfrac{1.6792 \pm \sqrt{2.4789}}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1.6792 \pm 1.5745}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = \dfrac{3.2537}{2} = 1.6268, \quad \lambda_2 = \dfrac{0.1047}{2} = 0.0524
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the trace condition** (sum of eigenvalues = trace):

$$
\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 1.6268 + 0.0524 = 1.6792 \approx \text{tr}(\Sigma) = 0.7825 + 0.8967 = 1.6792 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Compute eigenvectors for the dominant eigenvalue $\lambda_1 = 1.6268$.**
Solve $(\Sigma - \lambda_1 I) v_1 = 0$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0.7825 - 1.6268 & 0.7851 \\ 0.7851 & 0.8967 - 1.6268 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = 0
$$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} -0.8443 & 0.7851 \\ 0.7851 & -0.7301 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = 0
$$

From the first row: $-0.8443 \, v_{11} + 0.7851 \, v_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{12} = \dfrac{0.8443}{0.7851} v_{11} = 1.0755 \, v_{11}$

Normalize so that $v_{11}^2 + v_{12}^2 = 1$:

$$
v_{11}^2 (1 + 1.1567) = 1 \Rightarrow v_{11}^2 = \dfrac{1}{2.1567} = 0.4637
$$

$$
v_{11} = 0.6809, \quad v_{12} = 0.6809 \times 1.0755 = 0.7323
$$

$$
v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6809 \\ 0.7323 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Variance explained analysis.**

$$
\text{Total variance} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 1.6792
$$

$$
\eta_1 = \dfrac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \dfrac{1.6268}{1.6792} = 0.9689 = 96.89\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A single principal component captures **96.89%** of the data's variance. We can reduce 2D data to 1D with minimal information loss — useful for compressing robot trajectory data.

**Step 8 — Project to 1D.**

$$
W_1 = v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6809 \\ 0.7323 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
Y = X_c W_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.725 & 0.350 \\ -1.275 & -1.350 \\ 0.425 & 0.850 \\ 0.125 & 0.150 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.6809 \\ 0.7323 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4935 + 0.2563 \\ -0.8681 - 0.9886 \\ 0.2894 + 0.6225 \\ 0.0851 + 0.1098 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7498 \\ -1.8567 \\ 0.9119 \\ 0.1949 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.3 Reconstruction Error Bound

A **theoretical guarantee**: If we keep the top $k$ principal components, the reconstruction error is bounded by the sum of discarded eigenvalues:

$$
E_{\text{rec}} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=k+1}^{m} \lambda_i
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a key KTU result: **the reconstruction error equals exactly the total variance of the discarded components**. This is why the choice of $k$ directly trades off compression against fidelity.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the PCA Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **end-to-end PCA workflow** in the context of a robotic perception pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sensor Data\nLiDAR / Camera / IMU] --> B[Data Matrix Assembly\nX in R to the n x m]
    B --> C[Mean Centering\nXc = X minus mean X]
    C --> D{Standardization\nRequired?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Z-Score Normalization\nXstd = Xc divided by sigma]
    D -->|No| F[Covariance Matrix\nSigma = XcT Xc divided by n minus 1]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Eigendecomposition\nSigma v equals lambda v]
    G --> H[Sort Eigenpairs\nDescending by lambda]
    H --> I[Select Top k Components\nW k = v1 to vk]
    I --> J[Project Data\nY = Xc W k]
    J --> K[Reduced Representation\nY in R to the n x k]
    K --> L[Downstream AI Task\nClassification / SLAM / Control]
    K --> M[Reconstruction\nX hat = Y WkT plus mean X]
    M --> N[Compute Error\nFrobenius norm]
    N --> O{Eta greater than 0.95?}
    O -->|No| P[Increase k and Re-run]
    P --> I
    O -->|Yes| Q[Deploy Compressed Model]
```

**Reading Guide for Students:**
- Node `A` represents the **input layer** (raw multi-sensor data from a robot).
- Node `D` is a **decision gate** — standardization is required only if feature scales differ (e.g., LiDAR range in meters vs. intensity in candelas).
- The **loopback edge** from `O` → `P` represents **iterative model selection** — increasing $k$ until the cumulative explained variance threshold is met.
- Node `L` and `Q` are **output sinks** representing deployment to a downstream AI module.

### 4.2 Conceptual Geometry of Principal Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Original Data Cloud\n2D Scatter] --> S[Mean Centering\nTranslate to Origin]
    S --> T[Find Direction of\nMaximum Variance]
    T --> U[PC1: Long Axis of Spread]
    T --> V[PC2: Perpendicular to PC1\nCaptures Remaining Variance]
    U --> W[Project Points onto PC1\n1D Representation]
    V --> X[Project Points onto PC2\nOptional Second Dimension]
```

### 4.3 Algorithmic Decision Flow for Choosing $k$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Compute All Eigenvalues] --> CumSum[Cumulative Sum\nSum of lambda i for i=1 to k]
    CumSum --> Total[Divide by Total Variance\neta k formula]
    Total --> Check1{Eta k greater than or equal to 0.95}
    Check1 -->|Yes| Stop1[Stop: Use k Components]
    Check1 -->|No| Kplus[Increment k by 1]
    Kplus --> CumSum
    Start --> Kaiser[Alternative: Kaiser Rule\nKeep lambda i greater than 1]
    Kaiser --> Stop2[Use Count of Qualifying Eigenvalues]
```
